paragraphs: If you're happy within yourself, if you preserve....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, if you're happy within yourself you can have peace of mind, quiet, so when you're happy within yourself, when you have peace of mind, well what needs are being fulfilled then? Can one speak in terms of needs being fulfilled, objective needs being fulfilled. If so, what are those needs which are being fulfilled so that you feel happy within yourself and contented?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: When you're psychologically happy, when your....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It would seem to be a matter of emotional positivity; you must be in a state of appreciating your own worth and feeling that your own worth is appreciated by other people; you must be in a state of loving yourself and feeling that you are loved by other people. I think this is the basic requirement, probably, you know, before you can be expected in a sense to go out to other people, to concern yourself to some extent with their needs.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: You say it's twofold, that you appreciate your own worth and see that it is appreciated. Why do we need the second, if you've got...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It does seem, you know, looking at the development of the human being, that unless there is a feeling of being loved, you don't so to speak learn to love yourself. If you feel the absence of love, as it were, in the environment, if you don't feel that you are loved by others, you feel that you are unworthy perhaps there's something wrong with you and that may well make it difficult for you to love yourself. Or if as a young child you are deprived of love, affection, it does seem that your emotional [67] development even can be quite badly crippled. So it would seem that before you can really be outward-going and think in terms of helping others, you must be in a quite highly positive emotional state, brought about as far as we can see, by the fact that you love yourself and have had the consciousness that you are or have been loved by others. So that you feel emotionally secure as well as emotionally positive. In addition it would seem that you would need to have a certain amount of sensitivity, a certain amount of awareness, you must be able to see other people. Of course we are remaining on a common sense level still, you must be able to see other people, appreciate their needs. I mean appreciate that their need is not simply for a better standard of living in the material sense, not for a motor car or a TV set or more education. That their need is for something spiritual, a spiritual path that they can follow. A spiritual ideal to which they can devote themselves.
(pause)

Also it transpires from this particular chapter, that the Bodhisattva is to think in terms of all beings. Bringing that down as it were, to a lower level, one is to try to think not just in terms of one's own needs, try to think in terms of the needs of other people he genuine needs, but not just people of your own natural group, you're to try to think in terms of a wider and wider circle of beings. So think of other beings, think say of other beings in your own family, but don't confine yourself to your own family, don't confine yourself to your own tribe, your own nationality, your own race. You are to try to think eventually in terms of all beings and the needs of all beings, not just the needs of one particular section of humanity, the one that you happen to belong to so to speak. I mean the Bodhisattva doesn't have to think in terms of the needs of all Buddhists, he is asked to think in terms of the needs of all beings. So the Bodhisattva is not only asked to cultivate an interest in the needs of others rather than his own needs, but to cultivate a breadth and width of outlook; not to distinguish between one class of beings and another, but to devote himself impartially to all. I mean if you think in terms of these different kinds of beings, classified according to the mode of birth, well it means you know, you have to devote yourself to beings very different from yourself, who haven't even been born from a womb as you've been born from an egg, or from moisture or spontaneously. In other words you mustn't [68] be put off by the differences amongst beings, feeling that they belong to a slightly different group from you, so they are not your concern. (long pause)

So, if one - to go back to the previous discussion - if one has a preliminary sort of intellectual understanding of the whole matter. Supposing one understands, well, yes it is desirable that one should devote oneself to the spiritual progress of all beings, and then one understands in order to do that, well you've got to think in terms of the needs of others rather than thinking in terms of your own needs. And then of course you have to go one step further back and you realise, well, you must just work on yourself and try to get yourself into a sufficiently positive state so that you can quite naturally and easily start thinking in terms of the needs of others and not just in terms of your own needs you do come really right down to earth.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: On a lesser scale could you perhaps be helping others, if you were to be helping yourself and do social work or something?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think social work is highly suspect; I think very often social work represents an escape from oneself. It does even seem from the reports that one reads in the newspapers that sometimes social workers actually do a great deal of harm, they interfere in people's lives without really understanding what they are doing. I think we've got one ex-social worker with us. I don't know whether you've got any comments to make on that, if not from personal experience at least from observation.

turns.speaker: Vessantra
paragraphs: I think a lot of the so-called social workers I knew were trying to, er, they were people who had difficulties themselves and rather than actually working on them directly, they were, as it were, working on them out there, through other people, and so often the results weren't very good. A lot of it - the ineffectiveness of social work - is to do with the whole way in which you have a sort of legalised, formalised way of helping. Social workers have a whole lot of legislation that they are meant to be carrying out, and so the actual individual personal help is smothered.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well perhaps one can say if the social worker represents [69] you having genuinely personal contact with people who are in difficulties and communicating some genuine sympathy to them, at least giving them the opportunity to air their troubles and difficulties to you, and possibly being able to put them in touch with some (information), some means of them actually getting help, then you might be doing a useful job. If you could visit someone in financial difficulty and say 'there is such and such a provision, you could make and such and such an application, you could get such and such a benefit', they may not know that and you could perform a useful function in that sort of way. But sometimes it's happened, I mean there are cases reported in the papers, a social worker discovering that Mrs So-and-So, well she's not really Mrs, she's not really married, and she's got three or four children, she doesn't keep the house very clean. Well yes, the children are happy but she doesn't keep the house very clean so better take them away from her and put them in care. This is the sort of thing that one sees happening or hears happens. The well-meaning social worker thinks that it would be better for the children, that is better for them to be in a nice clean home with a foster mother rather than in a rather dirty and untidy sort of slovenly home with their own mother who clearly loves them very much but who doesn't know how to bring them up. So the well-meaning social worker rushes and sometimes does harm, causes great confusion not to say suffering.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: If you were, say, generally going out with some sort of vocation,... could you not work on yourself and doing that, working on yourself...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But it would certainly be good for you if you were doing that, with a genuine motive. If you realised that it was helping you and you were helping other people in a clear-sighted sort of way, that would be good. But what happens with many social workers is they don't realise that they are in a sense projecting their problems onto other people, they're not conscious of that, they think they're alright - there's nothing wrong with them. It's the other people who are in a mess and they've got to help them and sort of try and tidy up the mess. Maybe if one really wants to help other people in that sort of way one should avoid the organised Social Services and do [70] it off one's own bat if necessary in one's spare time. I mean, there's plenty of people to help; if you go and work in an ordinary office, you may find that people
come to you with their problems, weep on your shoulder all the rest of it, you may be able to give a bit of good advice. (pause)

So the essence of the matter would seem to be, to be or to keep yourself in a sufficiently positive state so that you can quite spontaneously be concerned with the needs of other people rather than so exclusively with your own. It's not that on this level your own needs are to be neglected, but if they have found reasonable fulfilment then you will be able to concern yourself much more with other people. And see other people much less as instruments of your own needs and much more in a sense, as ends in themselves. It's very very rare I think that people actually put themselves out in a completely disinterested way for other people. They might do it for their own mother or their own father or whatever, wife or husband or friend or boyfriend or child, because clearly there's a personal tie a personal interest but it's very rarely that people take trouble, I think, for someone from whom they can expect nothing, no fulfilment of their own personal needs in any way, that's comparatively rare it seems. (long pause) Well one notices that even in little ways that, for instance, I'm not thinking of just II Convento I'm thinking in a wider context, especially in our own communities, I mean people, even at meal times are more concerned to get what they need than to see that other people are getting what they need, do you see what I mean? It shows itself even in little ways of this sort. You know the old saying, I think it's a Cockney saying, 'look after number one'. I wouldn't say it's the last thing a Bodhisattva would do but he certainly wouldn't think only in terms of looking after number one; it's interesting that number one is called number one.

turns.speaker: Gerry Corr
paragraphs: It's interesting that the freemasons were set up, so as to help people within the freemasonry.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. Well clearly, practically speaking you can't help everybody, well maybe a Bodhisattva can, let's not argue about that as the moment, or a Bodhisattva can at least aspire to helping all beings but the number of beings that oneself can personally [71] help, even given a great deal of willingness, is very limited indeed. Usually one has to start with those who are pretty near at hand, pretty close to you, but one shouldn't be narrow and exclusive. I mean the freemasons have the reputation of being a bit clannish haven't they. Of looking after their own, but one can't blame them altogether for that. Because although they may be rich they are certainly not rich enough to look after everybody. (long pause)

A good example of the sort of thing I've been talking about, you know, comes from the Pali scriptures. That episode on which I gave the talk 'A Case of Dysentry'. What was it that those monks said to the Buddha when he asked them why they were neglecting the sick monk? They said 'Lord he's not useful to us'. What a dreadful admission. They were duly reprimanded and even though in the Pali Canon there was no sort of mention of the Bodhisattva Ideal or anything like that, but clearly something like that is implied, or the seeds

of that are clearly implied, that one is not to concern oneself exclusively with one's own needs, or with others only to the extent that they subserve one's needs. Others have, so to speak, a value of their own. You've got to concern yourself with the needs of others, especially with those of fellow members of the spiritual community, fellow members of the Sangha. "Ye have no father and mother to look after you" the Buddha says, there's no one, you don't belong to any natural group to the members of which you may look for help just because of the tie of blood. So you belong to a spiritual community, so you know, the help can only be motivated by a purely spiritual impulse, feeling of common membership of a purely spiritual body. But this is what we sometimes find when the old natural motivations break down, the purely spiritual motivations do not take their place. You remain in a sort of limbo, and I think one has to watch this, because supposing you are the father of a family, you've got to look after your wife, you've got to look after your children, you are responsible, so that takes you out of yourself to some extent, because you have to concern yourself with the needs of other beings, even though, yes, to some extent we do it for a selfish motive, because it's your wife, it's your children, but none the less you are taken a bit out of yourself. But when you, say, give up having a wife, give up having children, you're living in a spiritual community, well if you're not careful you can, well, become a little bit selfish. Because the people you are living with are not related [72] to you by blood, they're not related to you sexually, so you don't have that sort of personal interest in them. And you haven't developed a spiritual interest in them to take the place of the original natural interest, do you see what I mean? So you're in danger of falling into a sort of limbo, a sort of selfishness and self preoccupation. [73] You've only yourself to please and that isn't always a very good state to be in or a very good condition to be in, of having only to please yourself. It can lead to a great deal of selfishness and self-indulgence.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: I just thought that appreciation is very important. It goes back to what you were saying about other people appreciating your worth too, say people are doing things in quite a selfless manner, it is very good to give appreciation to them.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, goes to show you're at least aware of what they're doing - not just accept it tacitly or ignore it altogether.... like the story of the wife who one day served up her husband a strange lunch, he removed the cover from the dish and found she had roasted or baked an old boot. So he said, "What's this?" She said, "you've never said anything all these years to make me think you'd know the difference'.... (laughter) (pause) So lay people sometimes almost criticize monks saying "well, we lay people, we live at home with our wives and families, we have to think about others, monks are a bit selfish, have only themselves to think about", well it is true, sometimes on the part of the lay person, this represents a bit of a rationalisation, because they don't go into matrimony, they don't have children for purely disinterested reasons. But none the less there can be sometimes an element of truth in what they say, because by having dependants in a worldly sense you are forced to sometimes sacrifice your

interests, or your own preferences, even your own pleasures, and this does have a sort of somewhat maturing influence upon you, often does take you to some extent out of yourself, make you less selfish. None the less this is not to say that the life of the monk, the life of someone living in a spiritual community, is necessarily therefore selfish, but it may be, if he doesn't replace the natural worldly motivations with more spiritual motivations. (pause)

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: It seems to me to be a danger if you're going to have a family and devote yourself [unclear] and see altruism in that sort of sense that is actually done a lot through self- negation, not done really through love; you put yourself out for your children, that is all that really matters to you, it is not somehow really love almost.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You don't widen the circle sufficiently, but none the less objectively you are not just concerned with yourself. One must recognise that [74] there is some improvement no doubt, in as much as you are to some extent however muddy your motives and whether they are limited in altruism you are to some extent aware of other beings and their needs, you become aware that your son needs a new pair of shoes, which means you cannot have a shirt that week. Sometimes it is as basic as that and it does have an effect. (pause) This notion of 'a being', that one should 'not have a notion of a being', because it is easy enough in a way, it is intelligible enough, straightforward enough to speak in terms of changing one's attitude toward other beings, being less selfish, less exploitative in one's attitude towards them, but even though that does help in the long run, in developing the Bodhicitta, in following the Bodhisattva Ideal, it is still very very far removed from getting rid of the notion of a being at all. So how does one bring that down to earth? Perhaps one has done it to some very limited extent already: in as much as one has some feeling for others and their needs. One is no longer seeing them as entirely separate from oneself and therefore to that extent no longer as a self or as a being in the sense of a separate self or separate being. Perhaps one can pursue the matter along those sort of lines, but even that is a long way from, if one takes the statement literally, not having the notion of a being at all.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: How important on our sort of level is it to actually work on that side of things. I accept working on being more receptive to the needs of others but how important is this sort of more -

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: This whole question of not thinking in terms of beings at all is no doubt something which one can leave sort of in full faith to the future and perhaps think of the present simply in terms of, well, of making oneself sufficiently positive, so that one can freely and happily concern oneself with the needs of others and then thinking much more in terms of those needs and especially thinking of people themselves less in terms of them fulfilling your needs than of you fulfilling or trying to fulfil or helping to fulfil or at least being aware of their needs - that itself would seem to be a big enough task to begin with anyway.

I think this is where difficulties arise very often in connection with people's relationships in the more specialised sense of the term, you start off perhaps thinking that you are very devoted to the other person, but you may sooner or later become aware that in fact you are using the other person for purposes of your own and perhaps sometimes they are trying to use you for purposes of their own, and since both the needs are strong, perhaps sometimes quite blind and [75] perhaps sometimes come into conflict both cannot be fulfilled at the same time there are explosions, perhaps a series of explosions which perhaps. In the end blow you right apart with mutual disillusionments, mutual recrimination. This seems to be very often what happens. You are seeking desperately to get a need of yours fulfilled, the other person is seeking no less desperately to get a need of theirs fulfilled, sometimes both needs cannot be simultaneously fulfilled or met, and then there is trouble; especially very often neither of the parties concerned know what their needs actually are - the actual needs, not speak real needs. They very often don't understand what is happening, just blind victims of a game or a situation. But even so very often people don't learn, they think, very often, 'well something went wrong, it is the other person's fault, they try again with somebody else perhaps with no better results. In the end perhaps they just settle down and accept the fact of failure, make the best of a bad job, think, 'well that is life, there's nothing perfect'.

Anyway we won't go into that. But there is one point I think in this chapter that we need to go into a little before going onto the next chapter. It is the last sentence of Conze's commentary. "Candrakirti compares the 'Nirvana with substratum' to a city which after all the criminal gangs in it have been executed, has now itself been razed to the ground". Well, do you think there is a possibility of this simile creating misunderstanding?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Sounds like complete nihilism.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, doesn't it. Because in speaking in terms of the Tathagata, the Buddha said, "it is not be said of him, that he exists after death, does not exist after death, or both or neither". This simile would seem to make him out to completely not exist after death, corresponding to the razing of the city to the ground consequent upon the execution of the criminal gangs.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: It is hard to decide whether this is more unfortunate than [unclear] or less.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Indeed yes, it is as though constantly the Hinayana, even the Mahayana sometimes when it slips into using Hinayana language, stresses the negative at the expense of the positive, which seems to be extremely unfortunate; it is not only that criminal gangs have been executed but they've been replaced by virtuous citizens, it is not simply that the old city has been razed to the ground, but an entirely new city [76] has been built in its place, that is how the simile should be completed. Or that the criminals have been converted, they have become monks,

and the whole of the city has been turned into a vast monastery, into a Pure Land, an Il Convento. They seem to give constantly just one side, one half of the total picture, with very unfortunate results, creating a very misleading impression, as in the case of Bhikkhu Bodhi, an Arahant, going along with bleeding stumps.

And Nirvana is like a piece of waste ground, and everything has been wiped out, a bit like Hiroshima after the atom bomb, (laughter) that is Nirvana. Isn't that the impression that is produced? I really wonder how intelligent people like Chandrakirti couldn't have seen it. I mean the Indians' innate sense of the positive must have been very strong indeed to have survived all these sort of assaults on it, if in fact it did survive, and one hopes that it did. It bears very much in mind the waning out of the conditioned, but is much less aware of the waxing in of the unconditioned, one is aware of the wheel coming to a halt, the Wheel stopping, but not of the Spiral constantly ascending. (pause)
Onto the practice of the perfections:

"2b. The Practice of the Perfections. In chapter 4, the perfection of giving is meant to exemplify all the six Perfections, the Diamond Sutra being an abbreviated version of the Prajnaparamita. The larger works always cover the six perfections in detail. It may even be said, with Vasubandhu, that the perfection of giving includes all the others. The giving of material things then represents the perfection of giving in its narrower sense; the giving of protection results from the perfections of morality and patience; and the gift of the Dana corresponds to the perfections of vigour, meditation and wisdom.

"4. Moreover, Subhuti, a Bodhisattva who gives a gift should not be supported by a thing, nor should he be supported anywhere. When he gives gifts he should not be supported by sight- objects, nor by sounds, smells, tastes, touchables, or mind-objects. For, Subhuti, the Bodhisattva, the great being, should give gifts in such a way that he is not supported by the notion of a sign. And why? Because the heap of merit of that Bodhi-being, who - unsupported gives a gift, is not easy to measure. What do you think, Subhuti, is the extent of space in the East easy to measure? Subhuti replied: No indeed, O Lord. The Lord asked: In like manner, is it easy to measure the extent of space in the South, West or North, downwards, upwards, in the intermediate directions, in all the ten directions all round?-Subhuti replied: No indeed, O Lord. [77]

"The Lord said: Even so the heap of merit of that Bodhi-being who unsupported gives a gift is not easy to measure. That is why, Subhuti, those who have set out in the Bodhisattva- vehicle, should give gifts without being supported by the notion of a sign.

"The term unsupported recurs at 10c and 14e. It will be explained at 10c. The word thing, also at 14g, translates vastu. It means anything associated with the act of giving which departs from a wise attitude to mere emptiness. The larger Sutras speak in this context of the 'Threefold Purity', i.e. the absence of any thought of giver, gift or recipient. (See SS [Some Sayings] no. 42.)

"Notion of a sign: 'Notion' is samjna, also 'perception', 'Sign' (nimitta) is a technical term for the object of false perception. It is defined as a thing (vastu) which is characterized by the fact that it is imagined, or falsely constructed. A person who 'courses in signs' is someone who takes the data of experience for signs of actually existing realities. The opposite of the 'sign', which is also identified with 'defilement', is the 'Signless', an attribute of the Absolute, which is indeed unrecognizable when met.

"The attitude which, in the practice of virtue, ignores all 'things' and 'signs', can be described as completely disinterested. The Bodhisattva is here bidden to forget all about himself, and about the rewards which come to him from his meritorious deeds. The practice of the six perfections must inevitably, in the nature of things, lead to numerous benefits, either in this life or the next. All these benefits - be they honour, prosperity, friends, or good health - are bound up with the six sense-objects, collectively known as 'signs'. It would certainly be foolish to despise this quality of 'merit' which is attached to good deeds as their fruit. For merit is the indispensable condition for all further spiritual progress. Nevertheless, to aim at merit is to diminish it. Any why? Because giving etc, when accompanied by wrong metaphysical views assuming the reality of gift, giver and reward, produces only limited results. But if it aims at emptiness alone, then the reward becomes truly infinite. The selfless Bodhisattva's merit, as Kamalasila says, is here compared to space, or the sky, because it is all-pervading, vast and inexhaustible.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So what are the points that arise here? A general point is that the previous chapter was concerned with the Bodhisattva's vow, while this chapter is concerned with the practice of the paramitas, especially the practice of the dana paramita which as Conze says stands for all the others. First of all this question of all the paramitas being reducible to dana, do you think one can really think in these terms or that it is a little artificial, that sila, samadhi, virya, ksanti, prajna can in fact be reduced to dana?

turns.speaker: Gerry Corr
paragraphs: I think you can look at them as aspects of dana, if you don't see dana in a limited way. [78]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But how does it work out? "The giving of material things then represents the perfection of giving in its narrower sense". That is pretty clear, food, clothing, shelter, medicine, "the
giving of protection, results from the perfections of morality and patience". But how do you give protection by practising morality and patience?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It more like psychological security because you've built up through your own practice of morality and your own practice of patience, you've built up sort of psychological merit so that you are able...

S :Yes, to impart a feeling of self-confidence and security, maybe even more than that, because if you for instance practise the first sila, well yes you are giving protection to beings, at least to a limited number, by virtue of the fact that you are not actually assaulting them, or harming them or injuring them, or robbing them in the case of the second precept, or running off with their wives and daughters in the case of the third, or cheating or deceiving them, or lying to them in the case of the fourth. So to the extent that you practise sila you do give protection and security to other living beings, similarly with patience and forbearance. In addition to which there is as Subhuti said this whole question of your imparting confidence and inspiration to others, giving them protection in that way by virtue of your own personal practice of sila and kshanti. What about the gift of the Dharma, corresponding to perfections of vigour, meditation and wisdom. You do need tremendous vigour to give the gift of the Dharma, especially if you do it constantly on an extensive scale; and meditation, how can you give the gift of the Dharma without some degree of mental clarity, not to speak of insight? So to give the gift of the Dharma properly you do need vigour, meditation and wisdom. It is not just a question of handing out little tracts that you haven't read, or even little tracts that you have read. So a fair case can be made out I think for dana as the embodiment of all the perfections, at least from a certain point of view.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Giving definitely implies other-regarding.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Could you see this in terms of the trikaya doctrine. To the material things you could compare the Nirmanakaya, to the gift of protection, you've got the Bodhisattva Sambhogakaya, the great protectors, and the Dharma, you have got the actual Dharmakaya. You think it is a useful comparison?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Except that the Nirmanakaya Buddha does give protection and does [79] give the Dharma, he doesn't just give material things. Anyway the Buddha goes on to say "Moreover, Subhuti, a Bodhisattva who gives a gift should not be supported by a thing, nor should he be supported anywhere. When he gives gifts he should not be supported by sight objects, nor by sounds, smells, tastes, touchables or mind objects. For Subhuti, the Bodhisattva the great being should give gifts in such a way that he is not supported by the notion of a sign. And why?

Because the heap of merit of that Bodhi-Being who unsupported gives a gift is not easy to measure." Clearly the term 'supported' needs examination. Unsupported is explained in 10c, perhaps we had better quickly look at 10c page 48... So it doesn't really help us at all does it? So, a thought being supported, perhaps one needs to discard the literal meaning of this idiom, what do you think it is trying to convey, this term, "a thought should not be supported?" [See section 10c]

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Is it not more that we should not have a concept of a thing?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: He has a concept, a provisional concept. It is more that he should not treat the concept as being anything more than provisional. In another term that the Perfection of Wisdom also uses, he shouldn't settle down in it. He should use it rather than allow himself to be supported by it. To be supported means that he is resting on it, he is placing his whole weight on it, he is relying upon it, he is taking it literally he is taking it at its face value. He is placing his whole confidence in it, he is almost going for refuge to it, he is allowing his thinking to be totally determined by it. I think that gets as close as we can to the meaning of the expression. Your thinking should not be totally determined by the immediate data, the immediate subject matter of your thinking or your perception.

Being supported by a thing is more or less the same as "coursing in signs". "Coursing in signs is someone who takes the data of experience for signs of actual existing realities, realities other than the data of experience' itself. (pause) Conze paraphrases by saying:

"The attitude which, in the practice of virtue, ignores all 'things' and 'signs', can be described as completely disinterested. The Bodhisattva is here bidden to forget all about himself, and about the rewards which come to him from his meritorious deeds." It is a bit more than that. It is not just that he should be disinterested which is a sort of a moral quality.

turns.speaker: Gerry Corr
paragraphs: He should be doing it for the thing in itself, not for he merit that he can accrue from it. [80]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well this is what Conze is saying but as I said we need to go a bit further than that.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: It isn't just he ignores things, he sees them as being empty?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. He is not ignoring them, he is seeing them as empty, whatever that may mean. But it means one's thinking, one's attitude, is not limited, not determined by the objects of one's perception. It means in a way that you don't allow yourself to be determined by the apparent objects of your perception. You are bigger in a way than the apparent objects of your perception. You allow them to set the terms of your functioning. There is a hint of this in

Blake when he speaks of the senses and the rational mind constituting a ratio which limits you, which hems you in. It is rather like for instance if you are asked a question, maybe it is a question on the Dharma, you can reply in terms of that question or you can go beyond the terms of the question in answering the question. In other words your answer can be limited by the question or not limited by the question, the terms of the question can determine the terms of the answer or not. Do you see what I mean? The Bodhisattva would not allow the terms of the question to determine the terms of the reply, so it as though the sense data put questions to him, they say things to him, but he does not allow his reply or his response to be determined by that, he goes beyond, because he himself is bigger than that. Do you see what I am getting at?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Sort of parallel with the six element practice in a way - at the beginning of the practice you are sort of completely identified with the elements, through the practice you sort appreciate your own... the fact that you are bigger than the elements.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, you progressively liberate yourself from them, it is not that when you have given up the six elements you have a feeling of impoverishment, that you have been deprived of them, you have lost them, but you feel liberated from them, freed from them, because in fact you were greater than them, your being transcends them, that of the six elements - well certainly that of the five elements. You have reasserted so to speak your original being. So the Bodhisattva does not allow the data of experience to lay down the terms of his response to those same data, do you see what I mean?

Again, you might say it is a bit like seeing a landscape and suppose you take a photograph of it, let's say the photograph just reproduces the landscape, perhaps it actually doesn't do that, but for the purposes of this illustration, let it do that, it just reproduces [81] what is there, copies it. But a great artist might come and look at that same landscape and treat it in a completely different way, he doesn't allow himself to be determined, to be limited by it, when he paints a picture of it, in a way he sees something which isn't given, he brings something of himself into the picture in a way that the photograph cannot. In the same way the Bodhisattva is not limited by the data of experience.

So "the opposite of the sign which is also identified with the defilement, is the signless, an attribute of the Absolute, which is indeed unrecognizable when met." How can you recognise it when there is no sign, there is nothing by which to recognise it? So if you think you recognise it, "Ah, this is nirvana", it cannot be nirvana, because if it was you wouldn't be able to recognise it because it is sui generis, as it is said, it is unique, of its own kind, there is no sign which can give you any idea at all what it is like. So you cannot recognise it when you meet it. If you do it isn't it, if you don't it probably is. So of course it is indescribable. (pause) What about this notion of merit. I have discussed this several times, several years ago; at least

three times quite fully but what about this notion of merit in this context? Merit as Dr Conze says, is not to be despised. "Merit is the indispensable condition for all further spiritual progress". What is merit then? In simple down to earth terms? Conze says it is attached to good deeds as their fruit. Well one thinks within a traditional Buddhist context of good deeds performed in former lives, but suppose you don't take that sort of context, suppose you think in terms of good deeds performed in this life, well then what would merit be, what would merit represent?

turns.speaker: Gerry Corr
paragraphs: The appreciation of oneself.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, I wouldn't say that, it represents more the positive emotional experience that comes to you as a result of living a thoroughly skillful life, and clearly this is an indispensable condition for all further spiritual progress. One might say that the Mahayana or Buddhism generally attaches great importance to merit and great importance therefore to this positive emotional state that one finds oneself in as a result of living skillfully, living in accordance with the precepts. And this is the basis on which one builds, so to speak, well people find it within themselves; follow a certain way of life, observe precepts, practise meditation, put yourself in a more skillful mental state, well you feel quite different. [82]

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Is merit not usually interpreted also as providing objective consequences?

S Oh yes in one's physical body even in terms of merit arising as a result of skillful actions performed in previous lives, yes, in terms of even the type of body you have the type of appearance, the effect you have on other people, your complexion, your beauty, all this is the result of or expression of merit, according to traditional teaching, or the fact that things come to you easily, people are kind to you, generous to you, this is all the result of your merits. Some people whenever they set foot outside the door they get knocked down by something, by a bus, or by a car or bicycle (laughter) other people it just never happens. This is explained traditionally to be due to merit or demerit as the case may be, but doesn't one find this?

This is why it is a traditional Buddhist belief that if things seem to be going wrong, well clearly its a sign of a deficiency in merit somewhere, so one should build up a store of merit, usually by making offerings to the monks, feeding them, perhaps overfeeding them, offering them robes, performing pujas. Maybe if you feel your are very deficient in merit, constructing monasteries, and things like that, building stupas. I suppose it could even subjectively, even psychologically, make a big difference to your attitude if after doing all these things you actually felt now you had a pretty good stock of merit in you account and it perhaps could give you increased self-confidence and able you to surmount whatever obstacles and difficulties you were having to face....
End of side one

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Again, bringing things down to a very practical almost common sense level, if one was trying to be a bodhisattva, trying to practise the dana paramita, as distinct from just giving dana, how should one do that, how should one give a gift in such a way that one is not supported by a thing? Is it enough to speak as Conze does in terms of disinterestedness, unselfishness, or is something more required than that? It is almost as though the dana to be dana paramita, this is putting it in very sort of basic terms, has to be an expression of your innermost nature not something that you, as it were, self-consciously do. (pause)

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: So you would almost be surprised if you performed some action and somebody came back and said "that's something really good that you did"?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes in a sense almost surprise to you because you weren't thinking [83] in those terms, you were just (so to speak) being yourself. It is as though someone was to come up to you after lunch and say, "thank you for passing the salt", what is remarkable about that? You just passed the salt, isn't that the thing to do? You don't think in terms of practising the paramita dana. Do you see? Because in the case of a real Bodhisattva if someone might come up to him, "thank you for sacrificing your life and limbs", you know when you have been brought back to life and you would be just really surprised that he bothered to mention it. You would say, "I didn't really sort of feel it, just did what was needed, didn't really do anything in particular". It does point to the fact, this whole teaching, bringing it down from this very lofty Indian altitude, that virtue mustn't be too self-conscious, mustn't be too deliberate. Nietzsche has something to say to this effect doesn't he, talking about chastity in Zarathustra, does anyone remember? I think there is a passage where he says, "Be chaste if you want to practise chastity, if you want to, but don't make too much of it". "Well this is just a fancy I have at the moment to practise chastity." But don't emphasise it too much. You don't have to call the whole world to witness when you give a halfpenny, some people almost do this. You find this among Hindus quite a lot, I am afraid, you find this amongst Buddhists a bit, you find it among Christians, philanthropy and Charity, I remember Christian missionaries in India doing this among the Tibetans, they'd stand there with Tibetan refugees waiting in front of them and they'd be doling out flour or rice from a sack by the spoonful and the Tibetans would be standing there with their bags and blankets and things, just waiting for this spoonful to be dropped in and the missionary would be there with his spoon stretched out saying: "receive this rice or flour, do you know who sent you this, do you know who had given this you? Jesus. Jesus is sending you this, so you have got to be grateful to Jesus and Jesus is sending it to you through us, so don't forget it is all coming from Jesus, and we are giving it to you, you have got to be grateful to Jesus, you have got to be a Christian, do you realise that, Jesus is giving you this."

Well only then do they give the poor refugee the spoonful of flour - or rice or whatever it is. I saw them doing this, making too much of a song and dance about it, as we say. I mean the gospel itself, in one of the gospels, Jesus himself says, "when you give alms give it privately, let not your left hand know what your right hand is doing. [Matt. 6:3] They certainly weren't practising that.

turns.speaker: Gerry Corr
paragraphs: There is the parable of the rich man and the poor man. The rich man was right up to the front saying all the gifts he gives [84] and the poor man just at the back.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There mustn't be too much, any, ideally, self-consciousness in the spiritual life or in the moral life. In its more basic terms this is what it is getting at: you should be moral, you should be spiritual much as a flower blooms, not that you're unconscious, not that you are unaware, but you aren't too self-conscious about it, you are just being yourself, not being especially virtuous, as though virtue was something strange, out of the way of our usual. So perhaps at the end of this chapter the Buddha does answer Subhuti's question: "That is why, Subhuti, those who have set out in the Bodhisattva-vehicle" Subhuti has asked about this, "should give gifts, without being supported by the notion of a sign". It is as though if you want to start being a Bodhisattva, if you want to embark on this Bodhisattva path, this is what you've got to do: practise dana, but practise dana-paramita, give without being supported by the notion of a sign, give disinterestedly, give naturally, give spontaneously, give freely. Only a sort of naturally generous-hearted sort of person can even think in terms of being a Bodhisattva. If you are mean, grasping, selfish and so on you cannot think in terms of being a Bodhisattva.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Can you make clearer the connection between "being supported by the notion of a sign" and this giving in a natural free and disinterested way. It is a bit clearer with a notion of a being, but being supported by a notion of a sign, I don't see the connection so clearly.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well a notion of a sign is sort of a fixed idea almost. It is as though there is a sort of a double barrier, the barrier of beings themselves and the barrier of the signs indicating the presence of beings in that narrow limited sense.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Is it also that one sees that dana paramita itself the actual giving as such, of something fixed, certain, this is dana paramita practice?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, if you do this, well than that's dana. One could look at it in that way. In other words it ties up a little perhaps with the third fetter the sila: you sort of go through the motions, well this is what dana is supposed to be, this is the sign that you are practising dana if you do this, if you do that. It doesn't really become then something free and spontaneous and natural and
even creative. you've got a certain fixed idea about dana and you proceed to act in accordance with that.[85]

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: I didn't quite follow what you said about there being a double barrier.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well first of all there are as it were, "things", and then there are "thoughts about things" and the words corresponding to those thoughts. So it's as though the signs represent the second barrier and the beings, sattvas, represent the first. They are barriers in the sense that they stand between you and reality; it is not only your perception of things which stand between you and reality, but the words which you attach to your perception of things. They form another sort of perhaps even subtler barrier standing between you and reality. You not only mistake things for realities, you mistake words for things.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Your mistaking things for realities isn't simply because you have got confused by language?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well it can be sometimes; but even apart from language you have a perception of things as things, but that can be certainly strengthened, be reinforced, by language which anyway embodies that kind of perception, that kind of confusion even. It is not that if there was no language, you would perceive things as they are, you would still perceive things, and things are not realities, But one does not only perceive things but also uses words in relation to things and that sort of reinforces one's view of those things, those beings as representing realities. Though of course originally it was that kind of perception that gave rise to that kind of language, not maybe individually, but with people in general.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Just taking the words literally as they stand, "who gives a gift should not be supported by a thing", that seems relatively straightforward; "nor should he be supported anywhere", what is being said in addition there?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, "when he gives gifts he should not be supported by sight-objects, by sounds", in other words, he should not be supported in any particular way, in respect of any particular sense organ, even the mind. (pause)

There is also this question of the amount so to speak of merit being related to the extent to which the Bodhisattva does not give supported by a thing, or with a notion of a sign. How is one to bring this down so to speak to a common sense level? Well, the more you function disinterestedly, the more you give disinterestedly, the more positive becomes your emotional state. It seems to be as simple as that on this level.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Just going back to this thing about being supported [86] by objects etc, to become unsupported must be quite a gradual process although you can actually acknowledge that everything around you actually supports and determines the way you see yourself, you can't actually just sort of say, "well I'm not going to be determined by this", you have to start with quite small things.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well this is obviously this whole question of path of vision and the path of transformation, a glimpse of the truth, at least no more than a glimpse to begin with and then only gradually do you bring your whole being, the rest of your being, into alignment with that vi5iOntht insight, that perception.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: I was thinking that you could actually look for very small things in your life, just quite insignificant things, things which actually still do determine you. You can actually look at those quite clearly and you can start on that basis actually trying to break those perceptions down.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: They give a concrete example in the case of this perfection of giving It should be quite easy.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Sorry I have lost you somewhere.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, give a concrete example of the sort of thing you have in mind.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Hmm, perhaps this looking in the mirror when I get up in the morning, I find I've got a bit of a habit of actually looking in the mirror to sort of establish I'm still there. (laughter) It maintains my view of myself - so, a way of getting round that would be to actually stop that habit...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Assuming you were in a sufficiently positive emotional state to be able to do without it, so that it didn't lead to a sense of self-alienation that you weren't there any more, which wouldn't be very positive. No, I was thinking in relation to dana.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Christmas presents and presents and birthdays and so on.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes you give Christmas presents, just because it is Christmas, and other people are giving, other people are observing Christmas, it is in the air, advertisers are encouraging you, every shop is begging you to buy something to give as a Christmas present. Yes. If you just go along with that, it isn't exactly dana paramita. That is a little habit one could well break. Develop the more positive or skillful habit of giving one's presents in connection with Wesak perhaps. [87]

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: It seems important that when we give we actually feel generous, or we feel metta.

S:That is important, but I think we mustn't forget the needs of the per person to whom we are giving. What is most important is that he should get what he needs, not that you should feel good giving it. Do you see what I mean? Yes I think If one gives, well one need not bother too much about the feeling, there must be some feeling there, presumably, but some people won't and they don't want to give until they really feel an absolutely uncontrollable urge to give, meanwhile the wretched person that they were thinking of giving to might be either dying of starvation or freezing with cold or whatever. I think, oh yes you should have a genuine feeling to give but you should be very careful that you don't attach too much importance in connection with dana feeling really good about giving. I mean in a sense who cares how you feel? The important thing is that the deed should be done, the gift should be given. One mustn't be too unduly subjective in one's approach. It is like the doctor, a doctor might get up one morning, might feel in a really grumpy mood, not feel like helping humanity at all, but does that mean he should not go into his surgery, not attend to his patients?

One thing that this whole chapter seems to be getting at in a way one could say is that in a sense there shouldn't be a sort of separate specifically religious life, labelled as such. I've mentioned before a criticism by the Egyptians of the Greeks, one of the criticisms, they had many criticisms of the Greeks, they didn't have much respect for the Greeks at all because they weren't the ancient Greeks in those days, they were the very young [unclear] sort of Greeks, one of their criticisms was that the Greeks had gymnasia, do you remember my mentioning this. So why did they think it a weakness that the Greeks had such things as Gymnasia?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Well in the course of your everyday life you were not getting sufficient exercise.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well exactly. So you had to set aside a separate establishment in an entirely artificial manner, you had to go and take exercise because you were living in a basically unhealthy unbalanced way. This was the Egyptian view. So I could say in the same way there should not be a separate religious life a religious life separate from the rest of life, there shouldn't be specifically religious acts, your whole life should be an embodiment of skillfulness, dana, giving shouldn't be [88] the sort of a way you act in a highly self conscious manner on special occasions. It should be something quite natural to you which you are doing all the time, without thinking about it, as a flower blooms without thinking about it presumably. Not that you are of course unconscious, unaware as a flower is, but you don't think very much in a way of what you are doing, of your so called generosity, it is not the practising a paramita, it is just you being yourself. You are doing what seems a perfectly natural, simple

turns.speaker: straightforward sort of thing
paragraphs: someone hasn't got any money, you have got some, you give them some, well, there is no great deal in that, nothing very special, you have got, he hasn't got, of course you share it, you don't think in terms of yourself being a great Bodhisattva practising a great virtue or paramita. So it is because we have got ourselves into such a profoundly irreligious state such an unskillful state we have to think in terms of religion and being skillful.

Well this is very much the attitude of Lao-tzu in the Tao-te-ching. The things like morality were introduced only when people started degenerating, before that they were perfect, never thought about such things. So the religion of Buddhism isn't something you take up as it were as a separate interest a separate occupation as you might take up stamp collecting or bee keeping, it is something you do all the time because it is a natural expression of yourself, the way you feel the way you are, the way you function.

So what applies to dana, applies also to sila, it should not be a too self conscious practice of ethics, or patience and forbearance, or of energy and vigour and of meditation or even more so prajna, wisdom. It means in a way, looking at it from another point of view, really to understand, to grow or develop is in the deepest sense a natural thing, not sort of some unnatural thing grafted onto you, but an expression of your deepest nature, your deepest need, so that when you are as it were being religious, you are not acting in an artificial manner, like a dog walking on stilts, you are being yourself in the best sense of the term. well that is how some people practise their religion, they are just like dogs walking on stilts, he tries to do it, it is a highly artificial performance, taking him years to learn, he probably does it quite well, quite creditably, he probably can walk on stilts better than any dog in town, but that is not a sort of simile for the spiritual life, it is not just putting your unredeemed doggy nature on a pair of stilts and calling it spiritual development and sort of stalking along on these stilts in a highly artificial [89] manner in front of an admiring audience.

People shouldn't notice it almost when you are being as it were "religious", or if they do only because of naturalness, they are so unaccustomed to naturalness that it is unnatural to them, so they take notice of it. Just as the Egyptians took notice of the Greeks gymnasia, no, sorry, the other way round: the Greeks with their Gymnasia took note of the fact that the Egyptians didn't have any gymnasia. Of course the Egyptians did have lots of temples, you could of course apply the argument, which they applied against the gymnasia, against their own temples. If they had been really balanced and healthy, they wouldn't have needed any temples. Of course they might have come back and said, what were the temples, the temples were houses of the Gods. And the Gods were real and the Gods actually lived in their temples and therefore needed to be served. And that the whole of society the state was in fact geared to that in that way every thing was completely unified everybody was a servant of the God, everything belonged to God.

So for them living in that sort of society, to be religious as to some extent it is to modern Tibetans, is an ordinary natural thing, a part of life. It is not something sort of super - added on to life. so perhaps we can think of the Bodhisattva practising dana or the other paramitas in that sort of way at least as a beginning at least to help us get some sort of feeling for the Bodhisattva and his practising of the paramitas in a greater sense. (long pause)

Whatever else may not be clear, it does seem to be clear that in this chapter the Buddha is answering Subhuti's original question, by saying that one who wants to be a Bodhisattva should practise the dana paramita. This seems to be the gist of the matter. I mean he should practise in a particular way because he is practising the dana paramita, not just dana in the ordinary sense, should try to bring himself to a state of mind in which to give without thought so to speak is natural. The Mahayana at one stage was quite concerned with the question of riches in this connection, because some of them would argue, some teachers argued, that you could not really practise dana paramita unless you had something to give and therefore the Bodhisattva should not eschew riches, shouldn't glorify poverty because then he would be unable to give things, in this case material things, to living beings. So what does one think of this? I mean is it necessary that one should have things, especially material things, and therefore have to earn [90] them or earn the money to buy them in order to be able to give things to other people, can one really give in this basic sense, on this basic level, if one hasn't got something to give? In which case what should one do about it? It's all very well to talk in terms of giving the Dharma, giving fearlessness, but very few people can do that, what about this question of giving on the ordinary common sense level. Surely you have got to have something to give?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: One can give one's work.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: One can give one's work, but is that always appropriate? Sometimes people say, "well I'll come and help you", when I was in India, lots of people wanted to come and help me with my work in England. It was very kind of them, and some of them were Bhikkhus, they were very keen on coming and helping me, they seemed really taken with the idea of work in England, but I didn't really need their help I felt, didn't think they'd be able to help by just coming over and all that sort of thing. If they had offered me a lot of rupees, well I would have accepted that gladly to help my work in England very much, but they only wanted to give themselves, their time and energy and so on, because I knew they were quite limited to say the least with absolutely no idea of how I was to be helped with my work in England at all. Their idea of helping me seemed to consist of me paying their fare to England and looking after them and showing them round and going back to India in a blaze of glory saying that they spent a year helping Sangharakshita in his work. But at times material things, even money, is necessary. So what does one do on that level, does one need as it were to earn so that one can give?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Doesn't it come down to your level of development, that if you haven't got very much to give in terms of experience, then perhaps you do have to work to be able to give physical things.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: It also depends on why you earn. I mean if that is the reason why you earn then it is fair enough.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right, so the process of earning doesn't do you more harm than what you give would do good to the person to whom you give it. Unless you rally want to sacrifice yourself of course.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: This is presumably another example of the inconsequential connections, "and why? Because the heap of merit..."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: This sort of argument if it is an argument is a circle, the argument doesn't develop in a linear manner at all.
End of tape 4. [91]
The Diamond Sutra, Tape 5.1

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Is samjna always perception in the sense of this, er, well, mistaken perception? 'Interpretation' I think you called it somewhere

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well on the sort of common sense level which seems to be that of, say, the Pali Canon; samjna means something more like recognition, as one of the skandhas hmm?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Yes.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: For instance you perceive, let us say, you know, something green of a certain size and shape in the distance and you recognize it as a tree, you know, as a result of your previous experience of trees, your knowledge of trees, that kind of perception is called samjna. (pause) So that is the common sense usage of the term, the word. So it's not just perception in the simple sense, it is also recognition, that is to say the identification of the perception as perception of this or that: that the green patch of a certain size is in fact a tree. Of course in the actual process of recognition or perception its probably not possible to distinguish except with great care, between the purely perceptual element and the element of recognition, hmm?
The whole process takes place so swiftly. Though in some cases, not so swiftly. You may be uncertain what it is that you're seeing, whether it's a tree say, of which the leaves are all

brown, or a rock hmm? You have to be very careful then, quite clear what your perception is, but you're not clear what it is you actually perceive. Then after examining it more closely you recognise it as, in fact, a tree, which is brown, rather than a rock or a boulder.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Mm. Is then samjna in the sense that it's used here the attachment of er, ontological status to that, er, recognised object?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. That question is not gone into in the Pali Canon, it remains on a sort of common sense level. But from a Mahayana point of view it is more a matter, much as it is for Bishop Berkeley in Western thought, that you don't rest content with the perception uh? You don't rest content with the perception-cum-identification or cum-recognition. You posit behind what you actually perceive an entity to which the particular colour, shape and so on pertain as attributes hmm? [92] Something which has a sort of definite nature at the same time. It's for this reason that I intend to say, or have said, that Buddhism is nominalist rather than realist in its attitude. I don't know whether that can be sustained in detail, but certainly it's a sort of pointer to the nature of the Buddhist position.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Sorry, could you repeat that? Buddhism is something opposed to..?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, it's nominalist in the sense that it does not posit, er, a real object behind what is actually perceived and named as the green patch of a certain size, shape and so on. Realism posits a sort of real tree behind all those percepts; it maintains that the tree is more than the sum total of all those percepts, you don't merely as it were, experience percepts, you see a tree which exists independently of the percepts. Buddhism would reply that a tree which exists independently of your perceptions of the tree is sheer illusion, mm? Not that the tree only exists in your own mind, so to speak, its not quite that point of view. (pause) Even if there was a real tree behind your perception of the tree, so to speak, you could never know it because all you would know would be your perceptions. So you might as well drop the whole idea of the tree which exists independently of your perceptions of a tree anyway, or of anybody's possible perception of a tree.

But for common-sense purposes, it's useful to think of a tree which actually exists - apart from its being green and of a certain shape and size and so on. But it does lead to confusion metaphysically, and perhaps spiritually.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It's that, that's being got at here is it, that that attachment of a metaphysical...?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, the positing of an entity behind the actual experience, to which the experience refers. In Buddhism the so-called object is more the objective pole of the experience itself. That there is just perception within - or just awareness - and within that perception, within that

awareness there is a sort of differentiation into opposite poles, one of which we identify as the object, the other as the subject huh? But there are not in fact discrete, separate, independent objects and subjects, huh?, which mutually perceive - and are perceived, huh? [93] (pause) I mean if the object was really different from the subject how would you perceive it, how would you even know it was there.? So Buddhism tends to think not in terms of a world of objects perceived by subjects but to think in terms of a world of perception, a world of experience, a world of awareness within which there are degrees of polarisation into what we identify as subjects and objects.

It's as though, to give a further comparison, which may or may not be helpful, it's as though we usually think in terms of a void, an empty space, an empty room in which there are balls floating about which impinge on one another from time to time. These are the subject and objects experiencing one another, with just empty space in between them. They are solid, concrete. But Buddhism thinks more in terms of that empty space not being empty space but being filled with a very fine, subtle, almost luminous sort of medium, but here and there it sort of coagulates, it thickens a bit, and these thickenings are the subjects and objects. hmm? Or maybe that doesn't help an awful lot. (laughter). Or maybe I should say there are sort of pairs of thickenings in fact, you know? There are pairs of coagulations. A double coagulation here, a double coagulation there, though each one relates to the other as subject and object. Whereas actually there are only sort of areas of relative density in this tenuous medium which is distributed throughout the room.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: (Laughing) its got slightly realist overtones.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes, (unclear) illustration. Or you could say that it's this tenuous medium is sort of brilliant, it's luminous, it's radiant. But here and there there are little sort of double spots of darkness. These are the subjects and objects.
No doubt the world of the Prajnaparamita is a quite rarified sort of world. Even though the language is apparently quite simple and straightforward. Anyway, any final points?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Just a small one. You say that the Bodhisattva accrues merit through, or infinite merit through, no notion of a sign; you also say that it's very difficult to think of the Bodhisattva in personal terms, so how could a force, as it were, a Bodhisattva force accrue merit?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, one might say if one wanted to be paradoxical in the style of the Diamond Sutra itself, who else, or what else, could [94] accrue merit except an impersonal force, real merit? In other words we have to reverse our usual way of thinking. I mean, you are standing in the way of your own accumulator of merit all the time. If you would just step aside or get out of the way, the merit would come streaming and pouring through! (chuckles)

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: So, does that mean that the Buddha's indicating to Subhuti that dana is an innate quality?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: He's not quite saying that, maybe that's our sort of reading of it but I think it's a reasonably fair reading. Dana is an innate quality in the sense that you are innately void, one, could say. If you realized that, well, this innate dana, this innate generosity, will be released. One has to go very deep, you know, beyond the psychological level to, as it were, a metaphysical level of being before that is possible. It's not just releasing one's potentialities in the ordinary sort of encounter group growth-oriented sort of manner, hmm?
If to be a Bodhisattva is natural, to practise dana is natural.
(End of Side A - End of Tape) [95]
Jinavamsa:
"2c. Buddhahood and the thirty-two Marks

"Chapter 4 spoke of the 'signs' by which separate things are recognized and marked off. Chapter 5 now deals with the 'marks', by which the body of the Buddha is distinguished from those of other men. As in the world around us we should not look for 'signs' anywhere, so in the Buddha we should not look for the thirty-two marks of the superman.

"5. The Lord continued: 'What do you think, Subhuti, can the Tathagata be seen by the possession of his marks?'- Subhuti replied: 'No indeed, O Lord. And why? What has been taught by the Tathagata as the possession of marks, that is truly a no-possession of no-marks.' The Lord said: 'Wherever there is possession of marks, there is fraud, wherever there is no- possession of no-marks there is no fraud. Hence the Tathagata is to be seen from no-marks as marks.'

"The possession of marks is discussed also at 20b, 6a, 27. The word sampad, here translated as 'possession', has other meanings as well, and one might also speak of the 'excellence of his marks', or the 'sum total of his marks', Sampad means the attainments or accomplishments of the individual in the course of his religious career, and the word is regularly used for the 'achievement' of morality, trance, wisdom, or Nirvana. The connection of the topic of merit with that of the thirty-two marks was immediately obvious to the average Buddhist at the time of the composition of this Sutra. For he was aware that each one of the Buddha's thirty- two marks was born of a hundred acts of merit. He would, however, infer from the argument of chapter 4 that he was wrong in his belief that the six perfections can be practised in the

hope of winning Buddhahood. And this inference is now confirmed by chapter 5. It would, indeed, be a mistake to suppose that Buddhahood is some real, actually existing, condition produced as a result of the merit gained from giving, and the other perfections. It is true that one side of the Buddha, his glorified body adorned with the marks of the superman, is in fact the fruit of this merit. But the real Buddha, in his Dharma-body, is without these marks. In his true reality the Buddha is not produced by anything, and he is also not marked off from anything that would be different from him. It is easy to see that here the Buddha, as he is perceived with the eye of Faith, is contrasted with the Buddha as Wisdom conceives him. And the point of view of wisdom is declared to be the superior and final one. [96]

"The term fraud alludes to a saying of the Buddha which the Mahayanists were fond of quoting: 'All conditioned things are worthless, unsubstantial, fraudulent, deceptive and unreliable, but only fools are deceived by them. Nirvana alone, the highest reality, is free from deception.' Two classes of facts are here distinguished - the deceptive multiple things on the one side, and the true reality of the Absolute on the other. In its fifth chapter the Sutra prepares the ground for chapters 7 and 26, by pointing out that it would be a mistake to place the Buddha among the false, conditioned things, and that this is done by concentrating on his visible and distinctive features. Our conception of the Buddha must do justice to him as the unconditioned Absolute. It is the constant concern of this Sutra to guard the Absolute against misunderstandings. This theme is so important to it that it recurs again and again, as we shall see in the further course of our exposition.
2d. Buddhahood and the Dharmabody

"Chapter 5 contrasted the Buddha's physical body with his essential being. The next three chapters now deal with the Buddha's 'Dharmabody', which is held to represent the absolute reality of Buddhahood. The Dharmabody is considered from three points of view: (2da) as the body of doctrines, or teachings, in chapter 6; (2db) as the result of gnosis in chapter 7, and (2dc) as the result of merit in chapter 8.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well the basic theme of this chapter is that the Buddha is not to be seen by the possession of his marks - you can't recognise the Buddha simply from the marks. That is to say the marks of a superman which he possesses. (pause) One cannot, that is to say, recognise a Buddha from anything external. Later on in the Sutra it says that even the Chakravatin raja according to tradition possesses the 32 marks, so how then is one to distinguish between the Chakravatin raja and the Buddha as they both appear the same externally. It does tie up with some of the things I was saying some time ago, quoting Ruskin, do you remember? Asking the statue or the image or representation generally of Apollo. (long pause)
Do you think Conze is right in contrasting so much the Buddha as perceived with the ye of Faith as the Buddha as Wisdom perceives him?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: He seems to identify, in a way, faith with popular devotion or something like that, he doesn't mean faith in any very spiritual sense. [97]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Certainly not faith as the emotional equivalent of wisdom. (pause) In effect it is to give precedence to the conceptual mode or approach over the imaginative or imaginal type of approach. In other words it assumes that wisdom is or finds more adequate expression in terms of concepts than it finds in terms of images. Because one might say, suppose one did have a representation of the Buddha complete with these 32 signs or 32 major marks and four, I think it is, minor signs. Well they are no doubt according to tradition the marks of a superman, but couldn't one not do better than that so to speak, in representing the Buddha? Do you see what I mean? If one is thinking in terms of an image of the Buddha one isn't simply thinking in terms of representation of a figure bearing the 32 marks. One is thinking in terms of a figure which expresses wisdom and compassion, energy, peace and so on. (pause) And that sort of image is the object of, well maybe even popular devotion, but certainly of the kind of faith which is the "emotional" counterpart of wisdom. It's as though Conze is assuming that the Buddha, who is the object of faith, is simply the Buddha as conceived of as possessing these 32 marks. It doesn't seem necessarily to be the case.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Where did this concept of the 32 major marks actually come from? Is it Buddhist or is it perhaps pre-Buddhist?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well one doesn't really know. It's represented in the Pali canon as, so to speak, pre- Buddhistic. Learned brahmins are represented as being well versed in these 32 marks and immediately recognising them on the Buddha himself. Until recently I was of the opinion - I must have read this somewhere years ago - that there was no reference to the 32 marks in pre- Buddhist Vedic Literature, but when I was in India I did come across one reference to this, sort of; I can't remember it just now. It's one of the things I'll have to follow up, but certainly the 32 marks do not figure to any degree of prominence in Hindu literature. Whereas clearly the Pali literature represents the Brahmins of the Buddha's day as being well acquainted with these, it represents knowledge of these 32 signs being part of the standard equipment of a learned Brahmin. [98] Many Brahmins of today haven't even heard of them if they haven't read Buddhist texts, they certainly haven't come down to the present day as part of Brahminical tradition.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It's a very bizarre list to make much sense of.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. I think it's Rhys Davids who comments that some of them do seem to have a sort of aesthetic quality, aesthetic value. Others seem to be purely symbolical, and others seem to be left over from the sort of conception of the cosmic man, the purse of earlier pre-Buddhist, Vedic tradition. According to some account I read that some had attributes of the God of fire (Agni). It is a very strange list indeed. But as Conze points out it was general Buddhist tradition, general Buddhist belief, that the Buddha was equipped with these 32 marks on account of his performance of the Paramitas in previous lives. Presumably - Conze doesn't say so but I think - it would be the first five paramitas because you may remember and I think I have said that when the Buddha is represented in Tibetan art with two haloes, one around the body and the other around the head, the halo around the body represents the jnanasamvara - the accumulation of merits, which is of course also connected with the 32 marks or body as possessing the 32 marks. Whereas the halo around the head represents the or the accumulation of knowledge of wisdom. Again there is the point that it is to some extent assumed, in some cases assumed, that the Buddha bore his 32 marks on his physical body, but on the other hand he's shown in some episodes as not being recognised by certain people until he teaches.
