Whereas if he had actually borne the 32 marks on his physical body, well surely he would have been immediately recognised as the Buddha, or as an extraordinary person. So it is therefore sometimes said that the 32 marks are marks which the Buddha bears on his subtle body not on his physical body. On the subtle material or on the astral counterpart of his physical body. Images of the Buddha do not in fact represent him usually as equipped with all 32 plus 84 of these signs.

They represent him as equipped with some, with the ushnisha for instance, the Bodhic protuberance of the crown and the hair, the curl above the eyebrows, even the swastika on the chest, but not much else. Because if you try to introduce too many [99] marks the figure begins to look a little grotesque. (pause) For instance one of the marks is that the hands hang below the knees, which to us would appear rather silly almost. But one must remember that one of the epithets of a hero in Sanskrit is long-armed, so clearly it's to represent he is sort of long of reach. The long arms of the hero who is capable of fighting. In fact if represented literally to that degree well it certainly offends against our aesthetic feelings.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: We do have a similar saying in our culture, we say the long arm of the law. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It is such a persistent theme in the Pali Canon. It's difficult to know what to make of it because it's not as if it was an isolated incident you could sort of explain it away as symbolical

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There's a whole sutta about it anyway, the Lakkhana Sutta.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Yes. Have you ever formulated any theories?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I haven't. I've been on the look out for years for any references to these 32 marks or to any o We in pre-Buddhist literature. I think one would need to go into, one would go into Vedic literature quite thoroughly. My suspicion is that these 32 marks on the whole are sort of left over from the Vedic conception of the cosmic man. Well, you may or may not know that in many civilisations, in many cultures, there's the ideal, the concept of a sort of primeval, archetypal man, are you familiar with this? This is not an original ancestor like Adam. Not a primordial progenitor of the human race. In the Kabbalah for instance there is the concept of the heavenly Adam, Adam Kadmon I think he's called. The sort of archetype of humanity you could say. And in the Vedic literature you have this conception of the purse in that sense and it is sometimes said, well there are hymns, Vedic hymns, where the creation of the world, the creation of the universe, described in terms of the sacrifice, the dismemberment of the primeval, cosmic or archetypal man. This seems to be a quite common, quite a persistent theme in early myth and legend. So it would seem to me that some the attributes, so to speak, of this purusha, [100] this cosmic, this archetypal man, have been sort of gathered together into a sort of tradition about a superman a mahapurusha and maybe the significance of the whole thing lost and then the whole body of attributes as they survived came to be associated with the figure of the Buddha or superimposed on the Buddha. So the Buddha inherited something of the sort of numinous quality of that original purusha figure. (pause) So I think if one wanted to delve into the matter and try to get to the bottom of it, to understand what these marks were all about. I think one would have to investigate Vedic literature quite closely and this whole concept of the purusha. (pause). It's as though the Buddha, though this also apples to the Chakravartin raja, takes on something of the quality of archetypal man. It's as though the Buddha in becoming Enlightened from this point of view becomes a sort of model or archetype or pattern for other men in a spiritual rather than in a sort of mythological sense, do you see what I mean? One must perhaps also bear in mind that the primitive cultures, which were perhaps not quite so primitive as we usually think, didn't have any sort of evolutionary view, any evolutionary idea in the way that we had. They didn't think in terms of man being descended from the apes, descended from, or modelled after some kind of celestial pattern and this very roughly or very broadly speaking was the heavenly man. (pause)

So there is a sort of celestial or heavenly pattern or archetype of man to which in a sense man not exactly conform, but which he must discover within himself. And the Buddha comes among other things to represent that and this is presumably why he takes upon himself the marks of the so-called superman or mahapurusha. It's as though he, by becoming Enlightened, by becoming the Buddha, fulfils that ideal. It could well have been that in the Buddha's day there were brahmins who did possess lists of these 32 major marks, but who perhaps had forgotten whatever the ancient Brahmins originally knew about their significance or their meaning. It's just become a list. (pause) And also it is quite interesting that these 32,

and I believe the 84 marks also, are possessed not only by the Buddha but by the Cakravartin raja - that is to say the so-called universal monarch. This suggests a close association between the two and of course we know there is the legend, the prediction, by the sage Asseta that the infant Gotama would either become a Buddha or would become a Chakravarti raja. If he went forth [101] from home into the homeless life he would become a Buddha and if he didn't he would become a universal monarch. So what does this suggest to one, to go into this a little bit. Doesn't it at first sight seem an extraordinary thing that to someone as spiritually gifted as the Buddha, the two possibilities should be open? That if he wasn't to become a Buddha he would become a Chakravartin raja. Doesn't that suggest that the Chakravartin raja is the next highest thing to a Buddha? The Chakravartin raja is a ruler and he's if you like a politician. He's concerned with the external world, with society, with the state, doesn't that seem rather strange? You might have thought and you (unclear) if someone doesn't become spiritually enlightened well perhaps they become a great poet or a great novelist instead, or a great painter or musician. But you'd hardly think of he didn't become enlightened he'd become the prime minister of his country instead, because one doesn't think of that sort of function as standing in very close relation to spiritual enlightenment. So what do you think of this sort of close association of Enlightenment and universal rulership, being a Buddha and being a Chakravatin raja?

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: In both cases he's leading mankind.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes in both cases he's concerned with mankind; as a Buddha he's more concerned or predominantly concerned with inciting each individual to make an individual spiritual effort; but as a Chakravatinraja, who of course encourages the observance of the ten kusala dharmas, is more concerned with the setting up of the helpful external conditions. It's as though it's very difficult to do both. In that respect the Chakravatinraja functions very much like a manu except that the manu comes at the beginning of things and lays down the original basis for society: He's the primeval law-giver. It's as though the Chakravatinraja keeps the whole thing going, keeps that sort of system going, maintains it in a proper working order. You could in a way say, - this is an idea that has only just occurred to me - that in the Mahayana the conception of Amitabha and his pure land sort of reconciles the two. That he is a Buddha and he sets up an ideal situation, a pure land, and within the context of that pure land, he himself teaches the Dharma. It's almost as if the two figures of the Buddha and the Cakravatinraja have been fused, have been brought together [102] on a perhaps higher level. And of course in Tibet quite consciously they have tried to bring together the two ideals in the so-called theocracy of Tibet and it's interesting that in iconic representations of the Dalai Lamas they are represented holding a golden wheel in their hands, the golden wheel of the Chakravartinrajas. They are Bodhisattvas representing the Buddha yet at the same time they are Chakravatinrajas. Again the two have come together. Anyway that does represent a bit of a digression doesn't it, because there is still this question that you can't recognise a Buddha

from his marks. Well put it this way, the Buddha is more than simply the possessor of those marks There is something in the idea if you like, the concept - to use that expression - of Buddhahood which is not fully expressed or conveyed or communicated by those 32 marks. The 32 marks don't say anything about Wisdom or Compassion do they? Not that I remember, they are quite external, they're bodily, even though it may be bodily in a subtle rather than a gross material sense. Other great people may possess some at least some of those marks. I think, I don't remember, I think in certain later traditions or elaborations great disciples are said to possess so many of these marks and even arahants so many of these marks, do you see what I mean? But perhaps we can approach it in a more simple everyday way, via say our knowledge of other human beings. Supposing you ask about somebody, supposing you want to know about him, find out about him. Maybe you considering his readiness or ordination.

So suppose you ask about him, and you're told, well he's six foot tall, he's fair haired. he's 32 years of age, he's got a mole on his right shoulder. Would that tell you very much about him, would it? That he had bright blue eyes and an agreeable smile, would that really tell you very much about him! Would you really get, so to speak, to the essence of that person, get to his inner nature, get to his inner character? So perhaps one can think of not ever being able to recognise the Buddha by the possession of his marks to begin with, in that rather simple common-sense sort of way. You can't even know an ordinary human being by his marks, humble though his marks may be.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: I think you can tell to a certain degree in that people do shape themselves, but it's more like an indication.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But has one never been deceived? Someone might have an [103] honest smiling sort of face and you might feel you can really trust them, but they turn out to be an absolute, well card-sharp, swindler, cheat etc. You are taken in. And in any case can you fathom the depth without really knowing them by intimate acquaintance? There's so much, so to speak, in the concept of Buddhahood which cannot be adequately expressed by means of any amount of external marks or bodily signs. So as a result of his good actions in previous lives the Buddha may well be equipped with all these extraordinary features. But though they can convey to you that he has practised the five paramitas do they really give any hint of the Wisdom, the Prajna, the Mahaprajna, the Mahakaruna in the possession of which the Buddha essentially consists, do they give one any adequate idea of that? Conze distinguishes, I've said, rather sharply between what is seen by the eye of Faith, i.e. the Buddha as possessing marks, and what is seen by means of wisdom: That is to say the Buddha as not possessing these marks, the Buddha as Dharma. But it does occur to me, I mean Conze implicitly contrasts popular faith with Wisdom in the true sense, so one could say that both faith and Wisdom can be looked at in that sort of way. You could say that is a popular faith which a sort of

unsophisticated belief in Buddhas and Bodhisattvas and believes that they are sort of supernormal beings and if you pray to them you'll get blessings etc. And there's also faith as the emotional counterpart of wisdom In the same way there's wisdom in the scholastic Abhidharma sense of an intellectual understanding and Wisdom in the higher sense of a direct, as it were intuitive perception of the truth of things. So it isn't really fair to sort of play off faith in the popular sense against wisdom in the more philosophical sense. Do you see what I mean? And this is what Conze appears to do, perhaps unintentionally. Faith is not always just popular devotion and wisdom, is not always the actual transcendental faculty of that name,

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: You gave quite a good definition of faith in 'The Survey' - I think you were quoting, is it Phillip Erfan or Phillip Ifan - I can't remember exactly the quote but it was...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, he quotes from Shinran I think, the Shin - Jodo Shinshu tradition that faith is an instant of egolessness - yes. And there, you know, here clearly faith is the emotional counterpoint [104] of wisdom. While we're on this subject of this bit between say devotion and faith, one notices this in the case of the Buddhism of Ceylon, one notices that the monks are very often scholarly learned people with quite well developed minds, but they're also a bit sceptical and they don't have much faith and devotion and they're not spiritually developed. On the other hand all the lay people have got lots of faith, including faith in the bhikkhus, but it's faith of the popular variety and very easily extends over in the direction of Hindu/Muslim goddesses as well. Do you see what I mean? In the Buddhism of Ceylon we've got learning on the one hand, learning as represented by the bhikkhus, and this popular faith and devotion of a not very profound type as represented by the lay people. Well what you really need is to bring the two together and I think that the Mahayana generally with perhaps the help of the Vajrayana does that much more successfully, largely with the help of the Bodhisattva Ideal.

turns.speaker: Jinavamsa
paragraphs: Is it not the case that Nagarjuna apart from writing some of the Prajnaparamita also wrote quite a lot of stuff for the faith schools.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, this is true, he is credited with the authorship of certain hymns but even that I would say is not enough, not that you must be, you know, as it were having intellectual responses, on the one hand and devotional responses on the other, The two must be brought together. I think Shantideva does that much more successfully, and Milarepa does it in his songs; there's great faith and devotion related with great wisdom. One gets this sort of combination of qualities in a rather different sort of way in the Vimalakirti Nirdesa Sutra. Where there's a very strong emotional content, archetypal content, plus very lofty, one hesitates to call it intellectual or even cognitive, we don't really have a term, you might say gnostic element.

turns.speaker: Jinavamsa
paragraphs: Do you feel that Nagarjuna was unsuccessful in bringing those two together?

S. Well if he was the author of those hymns, and it's not certain that he was, but if he was the author of those hymns [105] he seems to have kept his wisdom on the one hand and his faith and devotion on the other, in separate compartments, judging by the evidence of these literary works. There's another point about this not being able to see the Buddha by the possession of his marks. It's as though one cannot or one should not infer the existence of a Buddha. Buddhahood is not something the existence of which is to be inferred merely. Do you see what I mean? Putting it in syllogistic form the argument would be, the argument which as it were is in the balance because the premises are false. That a Buddha is one who possesses the 32 signs, Gotama possesses the 32 signs therefore Gotama is a Buddha. Can you actually reason in this way? Can your knowledge that someone is enlightened be the result merely of inference in this way? Can Buddhahood be inferred, because if it cannot be inferred how can you know that someone is Enlightened or that there is such a thing as Buddhahood? Of course there is the fact that someone can possess the 32 marks and not be a Buddha because he is a Chakravatinraja, but leaving that aside, that would in any case falsify the argument. But even if there was no such thing as a Chakravatinraja, could you even then infer the existence of the Buddha from possession of the 32 marks.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: There are lists that consist of a bit more than 32 marks. Not all lists consisted of more than 32 marks.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes there are other lists where qualities or attributes of the Buddha as the ten powers and there are the eighteen special qualities of a Buddha, these are not included in the 32 marks. So even on this comparatively common sense sort of level Buddhahood is not to be inferred from the existence of the 32 marks. But alright, could Buddhahood be inferred from the possession of any number of external marks of any kind? In other words is Buddhahood to be known through reason, through inference? Because, you know, you've been told that the Buddha possesses these marks (leaving aside the chakravatinraja. So you look at him and you see the 32 marks. You don't actually see that he is a Buddha, but you see the 32 marks, you infer, you have an inferential knowledge, that he is a Buddha, so is that good enough? Well presumably it isn't. Presumably there must be some kind of direct perception.

turns.speaker: Jinavamsa
paragraphs: Only a Buddha could recognise a Buddha. [106]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well one might say only a Buddha could fully recognise a Buddha, but perhaps even someone who's not a Buddha could have an inkling of the fact that another person is in fact a Buddha, by some means other than inferential.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: Well what other means are open to him then?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well what other means of knowledge does one have other than inference?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: There's only experience isn't there?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There's only experience, which will include direct intuitive perception. It does occur to me also that Conze is perhaps a little too schematic perhaps, or his divisions a bit too schematic. In sort of distinguishing between the Sambhogakaya endowed with marks and the Dharmakaya not endowed with marks. And faith by means of which you perceive the Sambhogakaya but not the Dharmakaya. And wisdom by means of which you perceive the Dharmakaya but not the Sambhogakaya and no marks rather than the marks - is it really as neat as that? As schematic as that? Does it really happen like that?

In what sense has the Buddha two bodies, have we been misled by this word body? Maybe we should go into that a little because it has been raised though the Buddha has been (sentence unclear) so it's not quite body in this sense, but it will do. Personality might be better, but you know we use this word Trikaya, the doctrine of the three bodies of a Buddha. But is it that the Buddha has three bodies? What does one mean by this, is it three aspects?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It's three levels of increased experience of the Buddha.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Three levels or three circles? An outer circle and - inner circle and innermost circle? Is it true that faith can only penetrate so far? And that wisdom only can penetrate further.?

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: I think it's another case of inference from your [107] own experience. We see the Buddha, we split up, whereas in actual fact it's much more crude than that.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well there's nothing wrong in that because we are told that we consist of body, speech and mind: unenlightened body, unenlightened speech, unenlightened mind. And the body of the Buddha also consists of body, speech, and mind; in this case they are Enlightened. His Enlightened body is the Nirmanakaya, Enlightened body is the Sambhogakaya and Enlightened mind is the Dharmakaya. So there's nothing wrong in seeing the Buddha in terms of our own self provided that you remember we are unenlightened and the Buddha is Enlightened.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: You could say that body and speech come from the mind.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You could say but what would be the point of it in this connection?

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: Well when you say body, speech and mind you are kind of three separate entities, whereas in others there's quite a lot of interrelations between them and so you can't just put them into compartments and I think you can't do the same thing to the three bodies.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The word body in other words is misleading because you think in terms of three bodies or three heads stuck on the top of the other perhaps. Especially when the three kayas perhaps are represented by three different figures. There is of course a fourth kaya, the Svabhavakakaya which is said to represent the unity of the other three. And then there is another kaya, there's the fifth kaya in the Vajrayana, the Mahasukakaya. The fourth kaya is Svabhavakakaya and then the Mahasukhakaya - body of great bliss. So what does this mean? It presumably represents the different degrees of externality and internality of the Buddha nature but not three different bodies, that perhaps is a misleading, almost theosophical way of splitting things.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: What does Sukhakaya mean?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Mahasuakhakaya is the body of great bliss.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: What do you mean by the degree of externality [108] and internality of Buddha nature?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well it corresponds to relative externality and internality in the case of also a human being. First of all you see their body, that is, the outermost body, the part that is most obvious to you, you see that first and then perhaps you start speaking to them. By speaking they reveal at least something of their mind, something of their ideas, their thoughts. So you're in contact with them on a deeper level, you're in contact with a more interior circle of their being. But then by living with them for a long time you become as it were directly acquainted with their mind, you know what is going on with them, even though you may not actually see them. Sometimes you can have a sort of telepathic rapport with them and then you come to the inner most circle of all, to the mind itself. So, it's as though in the case of the Buddha, seeing his marks, corresponds to seeing the external physical appearance of another human being and then hearing the Buddha teach reveals a bit more to you. You hear his voice, but you still don't really know the mind of the Buddha, to know the mind of the Buddha presumably you must be either enlightened yourself or well on the way to Enlightenment. You can't know the mind of the Buddha so to speak by the external bodily appearance whether adorned with marks or not. You can't even know the mind of the Buddha fully from his teaching, in the sense of just the words of his teaching, just the ideas by which or through which the teaching is communicated. You can only know the Buddhas mind through your own experience of it either because you yourself are in direct contact with that mind or because you yourself develop an Enlightened mind.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Why do you think the other two kayas developed? The Svabhavaka-kaya and the Mahasukha-kaya?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think in the case of the Svabhavaka-kaya it perhaps began to be felt that the three kayas had become too differentiated from one another, as though there were three Buddhas almost rather than three kayas of one Buddha. So the Svabhavaka-kaya idea seems to have been developed to stress the fact that they were in fact one unified system so to speak. As for the Mahasukhakaya well perhaps it represents the Vajrayana's emphasis on the whole notion of bliss. Mahasukha, this is bliss which has gone through what Mr Chen used to call [109] the fires of Sunyata. The Vajrayana seemed to stress, at one stage, at one point, that one's essential being was Bliss. So one had as it were in the deepest depths of one's own self a body of bliss, a body that was nothing but bliss. This is of course vaguely reminiscent of the Upanashadic (Vajracosa) doctrine. We are straying possibly into heresy (laughter) but perhaps it isn't a bad thing to risk that occasionally. Are you familiar with the Upanishadic teachings at all? One of the best known of the Upanishadic teachings is that of the five kosas or the five sheaths. I think this is found in the Taittiriya Upanishads. It is many years, not to say decades, since I read the Upanishads, so I won't be quite certain. But it is almost a standard doctrine, certainly taken up by the Advaita Vedanta, that there are five sheaths, a human being is thought of rather as an onion, consisting of layers, some of which are relatively superficial, others of which are deeper. So, I don't know if I can remember the whole list completely but let me see. First of all there is (unamayakosa) the sheath made of food, that presumably being the physical body. Then there is the (pranamayakosa) the body made of energy, of breath literally, or vitality. This presumably corresponds to the subtle body, the astral body. Then there is the manumayakaya, the body made of mind. Don't forget the Buddha himself spoke of a manu maya body, the manumayakaya he must have said rather than manmayakosa. Then there is the prajnamayakosa, the body made of wisdom. And lastly there is the anandamayakosa or body made of Bliss. So there is the Conception in the Upanishads of a human being as made up of these different, existing in these different levels. But the term kaya is not used, not body, but the term kosa, which is perhaps a little more helpful even one might say. Do you see what I mean? So it's almost as though the dreadful thought occurs to me, it's almost as though Buddhism in its development has recapitulated these kosas. Because the fifth kosa is (anandamaya) and certainly you end up with the (Mahasukakaya) of the Vajrayana. But the basic point is that the individual whether enlightened or unenlightened, whether he knows it or not, exists on different levels, and we're used to identifying ourselves with ourselves as we exist on the lowest level of, identify ourselves with the outermost sheath of our personality so to speak, our grossest body. So you do not know, the list talks in terms of kosas (for a minute that might be helpful, you don't know, say the fifth kosa or even the fourth, or even the third, or even the second, merely by knowing the first kosa. Does it become a little more intelligible [110] now that you do not

know the Buddha by his marks. A Buddha is not just one who possesses marks any more than Subhuti is one who possesses black curly hair. He's a bit more than that, there's a brain underneath his black curly hair (laughter) to say no more than that. And I think that I have more than once before adverted to the fact that it is extremely difficult to know another human being, not to speak of a Buddha, knowing or being able to recognise a Buddha is really quite unthinkable.

It's difficult enough even to know the person with whom you're living even in the same community and sharing the same room, that's difficult enough. It takes years and years perhaps. And not just knowing them externally, not even just talking with them, but really getting to know their minds. Sharing their thoughts and emotions.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: I thought there was another point that came out of this, it's a point you alluded to much earlier is the differentiation between the conditioned and the unconditioned, how you go from one to the other. It seems to be saying here, because you can't tell a Buddha from his marks you can't apply the conditioned to the unconditioned.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well there is a sort of unconditioned depth in the midst of the conditioned you might say. You could even say you don't really know the conditioned itself until you know the unconditioned. It's not as though they:!re two things, two principles, side by side, one existing behind the other. The unconditioned is the true sort of unconditioned depth of the conditioned itself because rupa is sunyata and sunyata is rupa. It's not literally from the Mahayana standpoint or perfection of Wisdom standpoint that you've got to go from the conditioned to the unconditioned. No, stay with the conditioned, fathom the conditioned in its depth and then you will come to the unconditioned, that is the Mahayana point of view. To what extent it's a helpful point of view is another question, but that is its point of view. I'm just saying that this may be a little too much for us at our present stage of development. But isn't it true that it's very difficult to know other people? You don't know them by simply knowing what they look like or what they eat for breakfast, even who their favourite poets are. So how will you know a Buddha from his marks? In any case those same marks are possessed by a [111] universal monarch, so how are you going to distinguish between the two? That's a real puzzler. You might not have found a Buddha after all, you might have found only a universal monarch. Or at least a potential monarch.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Something Conze was talking about in the introduction, he mentioned memories from previous lives. I was wondering how literally can we take that sort of principle of being reborn into the same sort of group that you were originally connected with in previous lives. So I was thinking, if you can take that fairly literally then it's almost like over the course of several lives you can actually get, if you can accept the possibility of being reborn with similar people, you could actually get to know them quite well.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, but suppose every time you were, every time you did, you forgot your knowledge of them. This seems to be the case, but there may be something left because sometimes one does find one does get on with certain people quite quickly, really get to know them, where as somebody else you might be associating with, even living with, sharing a room with for months on end, years on end, you never get to know them. Why is this? Sometimes it's as though you've known someone in a previous life, you can't account for the fact otherwise that you do seem to know them very well, be very familiar with them, get on well with them. Does anyone ever, has anyone ever experienced this sort of thing? Apart from delusions associated with falling in love of course.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: I think I noticed it when I first, originally chatted with Ratnavira. I'd met him previously on a retreat but we had a chat for about an hour and a half or so and I just knew that I was going to live with him for quite some time.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: One could also think of knowing the Buddha or seeing the Buddha in terms of the five eyes that I've mentioned in that talk which I gave in Bombay. You can presumably see the Buddha with the eye of the flesh, or rather if you had been alive and around in Northern India while the Buddha was actually alive, or as Gotama the Buddha was actually alive, well you would have been able to see him with your eyes of flesh. You would have seen his physical body, that is to say his physical appearance, but then there' s a possibility of your seeing him on [112] another level by means of your divine eye, you might then have seen his 32 marks or seen him as possessing those 32 marks. And then when your Dharma eye opened you might have seen the Buddha even more deeply, seen more deeply into his being because in as much as your Dharma eye was opened you would have been seeing the conditioned and the Buddha embodies, been seeing the unconditioned, I mean seeing the conditioned in its depths, and in as much as the Buddha embodies the unconditioned - he having realised the unconditioned - you would be able to see the Buddha more clearly, more fully, more truly, and even more so when your prajna eye opened when you became an Arahant you would have seen the greater part of the Buddha's Being so to speak. But you would have seen the Buddha as he actually was only when your Buddha eye actually opened. For your Buddha eye to open you would have to leave, to abandon the Hinayana altogether. Give up being an Arahant and become a Bodhisattva, become yourself a samyaksambuddha. So we could think in these terms too. It's all a question of, in Lama Govinda's phrase, 'looking deeper', one must just look deeper. The marks can be imitated, external signs can be imitated, people can be misled. So one needs to be able to distinguish the apparent from the real, that means one has to look a little more deeply. Yes, Conze says, he puts it quite pithily, 'Our conception of the Buddha must do justice to him as the unconditioned Absolute'. It's not just a question of our conception of the Buddha's body, though it is to a limited extent. So one might say one mustn't think of the Buddha as an ordinary human being, one mustn't think of the Buddha just

as being a sort of superman in the pre-Buddhist cultural sense. One has to think of the Buddha as a Buddha. I think I've pointed out before how difficult that is, how difficult it is for someone, say, born and brought up in the West, to get into focus this category of Buddhahood, or Enlightened humanity. We either seem to fall short of it, as it were, and think of the Buddha too much as an ordinary human being, or we overshoot the mark by sort of deifying the Buddha or imagining him as a sort of incarnation of some higher principle, along the lines of Jesus Christ.

turns.speaker: Jinavamsa
paragraphs: What can we do practically to try and (define) this category of Buddhahood say in the light of the ?(centres)... [113]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well perhaps to begin with one's approach has to be negative; one has to try and see that the Buddha isn't just an ordinary human being, in the sense of an unenlightened human being, and at the same time see quite clearly that the Buddha isn't any sort of God or incarnation of God or avatar of God. That means one has to understand clearly those two categories which the Buddha is not, then try, as it were to imagine, see in one's minds eye this sort of third category which is quite different. (pause) And of course it is quite important to have a clear understanding at least of this third category, that is to say of Enlightened humanity or of Buddhahood, because after all this is what one is supposed to be aiming at. This is supposedly one's ideal or one's goal. So at least one should have a provisional conception of it. Perhaps it is very difficult, almost impossible, to have an adequate, even provisional conception of it. Perhaps the best thing or at least one of the best things one can do is to read the Pali scriptures and try to get some impression of the Enlightened human being, as it were, in action. Just moving about, teaching and talking with people, rescuing sick monks and so on. Anyway, any further point about that? We have certainly gone, probably, as far as can with the main point, which is that the Tathagata cannot be seen by the possession of his marks, in other words we've got to look deeper. How much more so in the case of the Buddha, we have to look deeper even in the case of an ordinary person who we happen to know.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: What exactly is a 'no possession of no marks'?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: A no possession of no marks is a no possession of no marks. Seems quite clear, (laughter) I don't really see what there is that needs explaining.

turns.speaker: Jinavamsa
paragraphs: Is it the same to say you can't see the Buddha by the possession of the marks, as it is to say that you can him with the no possession of no marks?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: If you say that a Buddha does not possess marks, it's as though you're actually perceiving the Buddha, because if you don't perceive him you could not even say that he does not

possess any marks. Are you actually perceiving him? Actually, when you say that you're perceiving him, even though you say that he cannot be perceived by the possession of marks you're actually perceiving [114] him by the possession of marks. So you haven't really seen him as not possessing marks. You have to see him as not possessing no marks. Marks are not a real thing indicated by the word 'marks'. Marks are not a sign for marks, so it's not that the Buddha doesn't possess marks, he does not possess no marks. It's not that there is no real possession of real marks, the marks themselves are not real, so it is that there is no real possession of marks which do not really exist anyway. Perhaps the Mahayana is becoming a little too much for us.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: - Is it a bit like that it's not really a question that we want to get involved in whether he has marks or not marks.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well one might want to get involved in it but one gets involved in such questions at one's own risk. I think we're better and sort of safer off proceeding along the lines I've indicated before, that it is a question of penetrating deeper and deeper into somebody's nature and encountering them at deeper and deeper levels whether it is another unenlightened person or whether it is the Buddha himself. If you'd been around in the Buddha's day you might have seen the Buddha every day, you might have watched him every day. But unless there was a certain spiritual capacity within you, you would not have been able to appreciate the Buddha simply by seeing his physical body, by seeing his external appearance or even, perhaps, by hearing him. You could have missed the point completely, what the Buddha was and what the Buddha was saying. There were people who did, we know.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Perhaps if you had been around and seen him day in and day out you might feel that he wasn't an old man, that he didn't really sort of fit in with your experience of his, the, whole sort of freshness, of the way in which he approached things all the time, even when he was...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well you might not even have had experienced that, you might have felt that the Buddha was always repeating himself always going on about the Four Noble Truths and the Noble Eightfold Path, the Twelve Nidanas and whyever doesn't he give us something new? (laughter) That might have been your reaction to him. You'd heard it all before. Well we're [115] told according to the Pali Canon there was at least one monk who was pleased when the Buddha passed away. Who said, 'well now that he's passed away, he was always telling us to do this and do that and not do this and not do that, now we shall be free from all that.' [116]
"2da. The Dharmabody or the body of teachings". Would someone like to read that whole section right up to page 33?

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: "2da. The Dharmabody as the body of teachings

"Chapters 4 and 5 expounded a very profound doctrine. They implied a notion of cause and effect which is decidedly peculiar and unusual, in that it had to reckon with the special status of an Absolute which is in itself uncaused and unconditioned. On the other hand, circumstances seem to be rather unpropitious just now for the proclamation of such sublime wisdom. In the present period of history we find ourselves in one of the worst possible cosmic ages, with Buddhism in full decline, and the people everywhere singularly obtuse about matters spiritual, and incredibly dim-wit ted when confronted with the wisdom of the Sages. We may well wonder what point there is in going on trying to teach these things when there seems to be nobody about who can learn them. It is therefore only natural that those entrusted with the Perfection of Wisdom should ask in despair, how anyone in such an age can be expected to believe in this kind of teaching, or to grasp and understand it. The Buddha answers their anxious query by asserting that even now there are Bodhisattvas endowed with the three qualifications necessary to appreciate these doctrines.

"6. Subhuti asked: Will there be any beings in the future period, in the last time, in the last epoch, in the last 500- years, at the time of the collapse of the good doctrine who, when these words of the Sutra are being taught, will understand their truth?-The Lord replied: Do not speak thus, Subhuti! Yes, even then there will be beings who, when these words of the Sutra are being taught, will understand their truth. For even at that time, Subhuti, there will be Bodhisattvas who are gifted with good conduct, gifted with virtuous qualities, gifted with wisdom, and who, when these words of the Sutra are being taught, will understand their truth. And these Bodhisattvas, Subhuti, will not be such as have honoured only one single Buddha, nor such as have planted their roots of merit under one single Buddha only. On the contrary, Subhuti, those Bodhisattvas who, when these words of the Sutra are being taught, will find even one single thought of serene faith, will be such as have honoured many hundreds of thousands of Buddhas, such as have planted their roots of merit under many hundreds of thousands - of Buddhas. Known they are, Subhuti, to the Tathagata through his Buddha- cognition, seen they are, Subhuti, by the Tathagata with his Buddha-eye, fully known they are, Subhuti, to the Tathagata. And they all, Subhuti, will beget and acquire an immeasurable and incalculable heap of merit. [117]

"The last five hundred years: It is well known from the Scriptures of all schools that after the Buddha's Nirvana the Dharma will progressively decline, and that every 500 years a decisive change for the worse takes place. The Pali account of this process can be consulted in BT no. 22, and the theory has been explained in my Buddhism (pp. 114-16). The last 500 years are the fifth 500 years, when the Buddhists will be strong in nothing but fighting and reproving, and the Dharma itself becomes practically invisible. It is of this period that Vasubandhu says that
'The times are come When flooded by the rising tide of Ignorance Buddha's religion seems to breathe its last!'

"'Will understand their truth', literally, 'will produce a true perception', or 'will produce a notion of their truth'. 'True perceptions' are the prerogative of those 'gifted with wisdom'. But there is a paradox hidden here: All perceptions are ipso facto false, and a 'true perception', or a perception of what is really there, is, as we hear in the second part of this chapter, strictly impossible. In fact it is a miracle, and that is why we should seek for it.
"Next four reasons are listed why these Bodhisattvas are capable of understanding the meaning of this Sutra:

"1. In virtue of their spiritual accomplishments, which are of three kinds, and which 2. they have deserved by what they did in the past. (1a) They are gifted with good, moral, conduct, because (2a) they have honoured the Buddhas of the past by doing their bidding, and obeying the moral rules, or precepts. (1b) They are gifted with virtuous qualities, because (2b) they have done meritorious deeds in the presence of the Buddhas of the past, and now they reap the reward of the merit they planted at that time. (1c) They are gifted with wisdom in the sense that they have cut off all false notions with regard to persons and dharmas. This is explained in the second half of this chapter, where eight such false notions are enumerated. (2c) The text does not state the kind of behaviour in the past which enables these Bodhisattvas to have so much wisdom at their disposal. From the large Sutras we know that they have for a long time studied the Scriptures with deep faith and great industry (see A, chapter X), and that also they have 'done their duties under the Jinas of the past' (A iii 79). The possession of these three gifts indicates that the Bodhisattvas have been successful in the three kinds of training - in morality, transic concentration and wisdom. The correspondence is not quite obvious for the second item, but we must remember that aptitude for samadhi is confined to virtuous people with a sound mental and spiritual constitution built up over many lives.

"The times are now so bad that these quite exceptional qualities are needed for even one single thought of serene faith in those doctrines. They enable the chosen Bodhisattvas to believe in the Wisdom teachings, and their hearts are gladdened when they hear of them and think about them. [118]

3. In virtue of their being assisted by the Buddhas. Known they are, etc. This phrase is taken from the large Prajnaparamita. I quote A x 224, with Haribhadra's comments in brackets: 'It is through the might of the Buddha, through his sustaining power, through his help, that all the

Bodhisattvas, the great beings, come to hear of this deep perfection of wisdom, that they bear it in mind, recite and study it, demonstrate, explain, expound, repeat and copy it, and that they progressively train in Thusness. Known they are to the Tathagata (with his heavenly eye), sustained they are by the Tathagata (with his wisdom-eye), seen they are by the Tathagata (with his Dharma-eye), surveyed they are by the Tathagata with his Buddha-eye.' (For an explanation of the 'five eyes' see chapter i8a). And it is of great help to be beheld by the Buddhas. 'The Buddhas will bring him to mind and assist him. And it is quite impossible to impede anyone who has been brought to mind and upheld by the Buddhas' (A x 223).

"4. In virtue of the merit which they have acquired, and continue to acquire. In our present age, with spirituality observably at a very low ebb, the achievement of enlightenment is normally not feasible. All we can do is to lay the foundations for it at a future period, when conditions will again be more auspicious. 'Merit' is the term for these foundations. The Sutra next goes on to explain wherein the 'notion of truth' consists, and enumerates eight reasons why the Bodhisattvas in question are 'gifted with wisdom'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There are several quite important themes here. "Circumstances seem to be rather unpropitious just now for the proclamation of such sublime wisdom. In the present period of history we find ourselves in one of the worst possible cosmic ages with Buddhism in full decline and the people everywhere singularly obtuse about matters spiritual, and incredibly dim-witted when confronted with the wisdom of the Sages. We may well wonder what point there is in going on trying to teach these things when there seems to be nobody about who can learn them".
So how right do you think Dr Conze is here?

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: There might be a little truth in what says but he does seem to be having what feels to me like quite a sort of personal grumble, as if he' s been putting forward these teachings and nobody has [119] appreciated him or taken much notice.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, this is sort of true and not true. To begin with it was possible for his translations to be published and presumably some people somewhere read him, and presumably some of those who read them felt or experienced something as a result of that. On the other hand, he was personally rather isolated, any sort of contact with Buddhists that he had was with people he met at the Buddhist Society mainly and he didn't have I know, a very high opinion of them. In his memoirs, he refers to a band of ladies whom he calls his 'mohas', who did his typing for him - ladies associated with the Buddhist Society who according to him, well, he must have classified them among the incredibly dim-witted because they had no appreciation of Buddhism at all according to him. But anyway, they were faithful and devoted and they did his typing, so they earned a little merit in that way.

Perhaps he encountered too many such people and perhaps he is generalising a little too much from his own experience in this sort of way. There's no doubt of course nonetheless, that the whole trend of modern life - one might say modern civilisation, modern culture - is against these Wisdom teachings. Every advertisement that you see is an advertisement in effect against Buddhism, against the Wisdom teachings because it (develops) greed hatred or delusion, or all three. So certainly these times are not propitious. But are any times really propitious? It's very marginal I think actually, - maybe certain ages are a bit more favourable, others a bit less favourable. But on the other hand some people may be stimulated all the more, united all the more, by the very badness of the conditions under which we live, to do something about them, to find some way out, to seek some higher wisdom. So I think it's very difficult in fact to generalise. (pause)

"It is therefore only natural that those entrusted with the Perfection of Wisdom should ask in despair, how anyone in such an age can be expected to believe in this kind of teaching, or to grasp and understand it. The Buddha answers their anxious query by asserting that even now there are Bodhisattvas endowed with the three qualifications necessary to appreciate these doctrines."

There's no suggestion that Subhuti himself is "in despair". Perhaps Conze is thinking more of himself. But when Subhuti asks: "Will there be any beings in the future period, in the last time, in the last epoch, in the last 500 years, at the time of the collapse of the good doctrine who, when these words of the Sutra are being taught, will understand their truth? -The Lord replied: 'Do not speak thus, Subhuti! Yes, even then there will be such beings. For even at that time, Subhuti, there will be Bodhisattvas who are gifted with good conduct, gifted with virtuous [120] qualities, gifted with wisdom, and who, when these words of the Sutra are being taught, will understand their truth". The Buddha is quite categorical: there will always be some who understand these teachings. What about this question of 'the last 500 years'? Conze says: "It is well-known from the Scriptures of all schools that after the Buddha's Nirvana the Dharma will progressively decline, and that every 500 years a decisive change for the worse takes place. The Pali account of the process can be consulted in Buddhist text No. 22, and the theory has been explained in my Buddhism (pp. 114-16) 'The last 500 years' are the fifth 500 years when the Buddhists will be strong in nothing but fighting and reproving, and the Dharma itself becomes practically invisible. It is of this period that Vasubandhu says that:
'The times are come when flooded by he rising tide of ignorance Buddha's religion seems to breathe its last!'"
So what is one to make of this theory of progressive decline Conze says: "it is well-known from the scriptures of all schools that after the Buddha's Nirvana, the Dharma will progressively decline".

But is that really so? There are certain late traditions, perhaps embodied even in certain late Mahayana sutras but it doesn't seem to have been a tradition from the earliest times. It's very doubtful whether the Buddha had any such idea. There's no doubt it was conceivable from a very early time that the teaching of a Buddha would eventually disappear, presumably that takes place gradually, by stages, one perhaps can even distinguish periods, but it would seem that one mustn't think in terms of an inevitable decline or that it necessarily continues at an ever accelerating pace or that there is decline equally everywhere in all parts of the Buddhist world. I don't think it's quite so simple as that.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: What about this quotation from the Pali Canon? You said the Buddha himself, that there are only late traditions if this is the case.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, what is that particular text? Has anyone got 'Buddhist Texts'? Someone run and get it) and we'll see.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Are we not in the sixth 500 years anyway?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We are in the, well, we've passed (it), if you accept the current reckoning, the 2500 years were up in 1956.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: I was born in 1956.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well that just puts the finishing touch... (loud laughter)

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Thanks a bunch! [121]

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Is there traditionally a specific time between say, the death of the doctrine and the appearance of Maitreya?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Some traditions say a further 2500 years. Anyway, let's have a look at this (enter Buddhist Texts).

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Which one is it?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: 22. Is it from the Anagatavamsa by any chance? (Subhuti: Yes) Oh, well, that's a very late work. It just about crept into the Khuddaka Nikaya. (laughter) Yes, Anagatavamsa. Yes this

is very late indeed. It's one of the very latest works of the Pali Canon. The account in a way seems quite rational - 'disappearance of attainments, spiritual attainment, disappearance of proper conduct, of learning, of outward form and then even the relics.' But it doesn't speak of periods of 500 years. "Here for attainment means for a thousand years only after the Lord's complete Nirvana will monks be able to practise analytical insights." Anyway, Buddhism does accept that the sasana will decline, not the Dharma. One must be careful in what terms one speaks. The sasana will decline, but is it inevitable? I suppose in a sense it is, in the sense that all mundane things do decline, do change, do pass away. But why should that be in the case of the Sasana? Why should that be in the case of Buddhism, let's say, to distinguish Buddhism from the Dharma.
Even if Dharma as a spiritual principle, you know, cannot decline, well, why should Buddhism as a religion ever decline?

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: It's that it's conditioned. As Buddhists therefore, like everything else it's...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: (interrupting) Well is it or is that not sort of to reify too much. I mean is there such a thing as Buddhism - a thing which is conditioned and which therefore can decline after a while. What does it mean, the decline of Buddhism? This is really the question that we are looking to.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It means that individual aren't developing or developing less and less.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, so that is to say individuals are making less use of the Dharma, but why do they do that? Why should that happen at all? Supposing you've got 500 arahants in this generation, why should there not be 500 arahants or even more in the next generation? Why should there be, for instance, only 300 and in the generation after that only 200 and so on why should that happen? [122]
Why should not the number of enlightened beings progress by - what is it called - exponential progression - why not?

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Is it the pull of the conditioned?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, one can speak of the pull of the conditioned, but wasn't that equally operative in the first generation.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: But the Buddha was around then.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, the Arahants are also enlightened.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: But in a different way.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Not sufficiently one would have thought. I mean the Dharma is there. So why should the Dharma decline? Why should Buddhism decline rather? Does seem a bit of a mystery doesn't it? What I'm sort of in a sense emphasizing is, we shouldn't assume that the concept of the decline of Buddhism is something that is completely intelligible, that we know exactly what is meant by the decline of Buddhism. Well, in a sense we do, but in a sense we don't. No doubt Buddhism declines when people no longer practise it, but why do they no longer practise it? Is it just a matter of accidents - accidents happening generation after generation, or is there a sort of accumulation of accidents and the result is hardly anybody in the end of practising the Dharma and therefore, Buddhism practically disappears?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Maybe people's minds have got lazy through things like TV.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But then decline started long, long before TV came. In fact it was accomplished apparently long before TV came in. In the Buddha's day, they could have put it down to the introduction of writing.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: But you could see it on two levels, couldn't you? You could see it on the level of Buddhism as a sort of cultural phenomenon. It's quite easy to see how that could decline because political and social factors could be against it. What's harder to understand is why there's no continuity of experience.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Why there's no renaissance arising out of that continuity of experience. (Subhuti: Yes) I think it has got something to do with what we may call crystallization, that is to say the Dharma as a purely spiritual phenomenon, as a purely spiritual tradition, crystallizes in the form of Buddhism and therefore, inevitably comes to be associated with that, Do you see what I mean? And that may be an expression which is adequate for [123] a certain time and for certain people but not necessarily for all, but it has come to be associated with that because that was the original crystallization.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It doesn't really sufficiently explain the phenomenon does it, because it you've got one enlightened individual teaching another enlightened individual then presumably whatever the... (S: That would be by- passed). Yes it would be fresh because there's a continuity of experience.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But it could be that there are fewer people coming to those enlightened individuals because they see as Buddhism a sort of crystallization which does not appeal to them, do you see what I mean? It's as though in the course of generations Buddhism assumes inevitably more and

more of a set character - the fact that it's crystallized in one way originally tends to prevent a different kind of crystallization in the future. It's as though the options have been limited by the nature of the original crystallization. Do you see what I mean? For instance, the mere fact that the Dharma crystallized in India 500 BC and the phenomenon say of monasticism arose. It definitely historically crystallized in that particular way and it is very difficult to get away from that even now. It's very difficult to get away, say, in India at least, from yellow robes and shaven heads. People really think that this is Buddhism. I think perhaps we have to find an explanation along some such lines.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: You'd think that in so far as the succeeding enlightened individuals were enlightened.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. We understand that, explain it to people. But it is as though people, a broader mass of people, have got this intense loyalty to the old forms. If you remember, to give an example, from another tradition entirely, that of the Russian Orthodox Church. Apparently in the 17th century, a learned archbishop or head of the church or whatever, discovered that when the priest gave the blessing with three fingers, this was wrong, so he discovered that the blessing should be given with two fingers and that this was correct and apostolic or canonical, but he had a terrible task, a terrible job, introducing this reform because people, quite ordinary people, had become quite blind and ignorantly attached to this three finger blessing and they took it to mean father, son and Holy Ghost, so they thought that if the priest only blessed them with only two fingers, they weren't getting the full blessing and something was missing and this - what was his name - this rather strong-minded reforming patriarch, Nikon - he had endless difficulty in forcing his reform and there was one arch priest called Avvacum, who stood against him [124] and became in effect the founder of the sect of the Old Believers who stuck to the three-fingered blessing and other things. So this shows you how very difficult it is even for the enlightened or comparatively enlightened, or at least better informed, to make an impression upon people who've attached themselves to a tradition as it already exists.

Well, I found this when I came back to England in '64. This is one of the things that shocked me that when my hair even grew, you know, it wasn't even half an inch, some people started getting quite upset and quite disturbed even though that length was quite permissible even according to the Vinaya. But they thought it wasn't, and they had quite ignorant sort of devotion to what they thought was the Vinaya. So I think this can be a limiting factor.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: You get this bizarre phenomenon with Zen. A sort of protest against that kind of crystallization - people being really insistent on the forms of Zen being observed.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. So it's as though the mere fact that the Dharma crystallizes in a certain way becomes a limiting factor, and increasingly a limiting factor the more crystallizations take place in

consequence of that original crystallization, more and more along a particular line. It's as though other lines in fact, although not in principle are sort of excluded unless you've got a very powerful personality sort of battling against that sort of circumscription of the tradition. But do you see what I'm getting at? At every stage, you've got the weight of tradition accumulating behind you to deal with.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I suppose that's in a way why we're in a quite privileged position in a sense - we don't have all that as r as Buddhism is concerned.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I mean, for instance, there's an illustration which is sometimes given - I might even have thought of it myself - I'm not sure, - let's say someone throws some ink at a wall and it makes a certain blotch - then he says, to somebody else; "come one, you throw some ink at the wall just as I've thrown it". The person can't do it. It's quite difficult to hit exactly the same spot, make exactly the same mark, but the first person says "Well, I did so easily - how is it that you can't do it?" Do you see what I mean? (laughter)

So what was just a free creative act, when you come to reproduce it becomes very far from being a free creative act. Do you see what I mean? [125] So the original crystallization of the Dharma, say into this or that form of Buddhism was free, spontaneous, actually to help someone, but when you're landed with that sort of crystallization as part of your tradition you try to reproduce it and think in those terms and do things in that particular way. It is very much a limiting factor.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: You could use like the analogy of crystallization, but if you want to recrystallize something you have to put it into solution first. You have to completely dissolve it in solution.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. You have to dissolve it or to melt it down. If, for instance though these beautiful golden Buddhas no longer seem to beautiful to you well, you just have to melt them all down again and recast new ones. It was extraordinary that Zen, which was really so, in a sense, iconoclastic, became in the end such a rigid tradition in many ways, certainly in Japan. It would seem that the mere fact that the Dharma crystallizes into Buddhism, to put it in that way, means that a limiting factor is introduced which is not necessarily a limiting factor but which practically speaking does in fact almost always act as a limiting factor unless very conscious and aware efforts are made to counteract that sort of tendency. We know ourselves, within the EWBO, that we would much prefer people to come along to us, new people, without having read anything about Buddhism before. It's sometimes very difficult to get rid of ideas which they've picked up, you know, more ideas about Buddhism before they came in contact with the FWBO. Certainly that was the case in the earlier days, I think it's less so now. One might almost say - generalizing, perhaps a little sweeping - if you're born and

brought up in a Buddhist country, you cannot but have wrong ideas about Buddhism. So eventually it seems, Buddhism is sort of crushed and smothered under the very weight of its own accumulated tradition. This would seem to be what happens, although of course, sometimes the traditions become merely traditions, merely cultural phenomena and it isn't very difficult for people representing, or forces representing, unsympathetic ideologies to sweep them all away. No one believes in them sufficiently, not deeply enough, to stand in defence of them.

turns.speaker: Chakkhupala
paragraphs: I still can't actually grasp why you say that a factor once introduced becomes a limiting factor.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No. I didn't say a factor. Once the Dharma has crystallized into Buddhism, one cannot but see the Dharma in terms of Buddhism. Do you se what I mean? When I say one cannot - well, ordinary people cannot. In [126] making, say, their initial approaches, one thinks that Buddhism is this or the Dharma is this. One identifies the Dharma with Buddhism. So it means that the Dharma exists in a limited sort of way.

turns.speaker: Chakkhupala
paragraphs: In the case of an Enlightened being would you say that the Dharma existed in an unlimited way? (S: Oh yes, certainly) But then why is there not a succession of Enlightened beings? Because there's no limiting factor in...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: (interrupting) Well, there is a limiting factor historically and socially because along with the Enlightened beings, you have the historical phenomenon of Buddhism which exerts its own pull, perhaps its own influence, at least culturally, quite apart from the pull or the influence of the enlightened beings. Sometimes enlightened beings may even find themselves in a sense, in conflict with the tradition, but people will follow the tradition rather than the lighted being very often. So in that way Buddhism itself may sometimes, in a sense, work against the attempts of the enlightened being to spread the Dharma, you see.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Surely an enlightened being through personal contact with people will be able to say, influence or...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: This may happen to some extent but otherwise it may often happen that all right, the enlightened being is dealing with unenlightened beings. They are not able to recognize him as enlightened, - he's simply someone who is straying away from tradition and they're very suspicious of that. In a sense rightly. How are they to distinguish between an enlightened being who is trying to make a creative use of tradition and someone who is simply misusing and betraying tradition? How are they to distinguish? So they play safe very often, sometimes they don't and they go after not the Enlightened being - who is trying to make a creative use of tradition - but the person who is in fact betraying tradition. They may go after him as
they've no real means of distinguishing between the two. I suggest this as a possible explanation, not that it is the whole story but I think some element of this sort is involved.

As with Christianity, when one starts criticizing Christianity, for this or that misdemeanour, well, Christians will very often say "well, that's not the real Christianity". Well, so what? We have to deal with Christianity as it actually exists in the world. So in the same way there is a sort of distinction between the Dharma and Buddhism sometimes - not a difference in principle but the fact that the Dharma has crystallized into a particular form in the everyday world means that it's taken on a certain limited form and however valid that form was, however valid that [127] crystallization was once upon a time, it may not be universally valid but it has become historically associated with the Dharma and people find it very difficult to separate the two. They don't come to Buddhism with an open mind, they can't. We've sometimes found ourselves that one of the biggest obstacles to spreading the Dharma is the notion that people have got about Buddhism, some of which are justified in the sense that some historical forms of Buddhism do tend in those directions, rightly or wrongly. So it would seem that in a sense decline is inevitable in the sense that Dharma collects, so to speak, all these sort of crystallizations in the form of this and that form of Buddhism and eventually becomes sort of weighed down under them and less and less distinguishable from them so far as most people are concerned. And even the most heroic attempts of the most gifted teachers, sometimes can't make headway on behalf of the Dharma against what passes as Buddhism. So that's apparently why a fresh Buddha has to make a fresh start - start all over again.

Conze has a sort of theory about a tradition becoming sort of naturally exhausted and losing its creativity after a while. That is rather sort of - I don't know if there's a technical term for this - but trying to explain something which is as it were, collective in terms of the personal as when you have a biography of a nation, but perhaps he was getting at something of this sort.

Anyway, despite all that the Buddha does say, "even at that time, Subhuti, there will be Bodhisattvas who are gifted with good conduct", Etc. and who will understand the Sutras when they're taught. So the Buddha is quite careful not to preclude the possibility of there being exceptional individuals at any time who will not be affected by the general decline. This means that in principle, in theory at least, Buddhism could continue indefinitely or the Dharma, the knowledge of the Dharma, could be prolonged indefinitely. It doesn't absolutely have to disappear. Which means that the true individual is not subject to the laws of the group, the laws which govern the group.

And then "Next four reasons are listed why these Bodhisattvas are capable of understanding the meaning of this Sutra: 1. I virtue of their spiritual accomplishments", "They have deserved by what they did in the past" ... "In virtue of their being assisted by the Buddhas"

and "in virtue of the merit they have acquired." These seem to overlap to some extent. It is perhaps understandable that they should be able to understand the teaching even in the period of its decline because they are gifted with good, moral conduct and that they've accumulated merit and so on. But what about, "have honoured the Buddhas of the past"? This is quite interesting. What is the significance of that? Those Bodhisattvas who at the time of the last epoch are able to understand...
(End of Side A) [128]

S:...and those qualities, those endowments accrued to them on account of their various actions, conduct, in previous lives - so that in a general way is quite acceptable, quite intelligible. But why should the fact that they honoured the Buddhas be also mentioned? What is the special significance of this?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It means that they've upheld a spiritual ideal.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: They've upheld a spiritual ideal, yes. They've also been receptive to the Buddhas and to their teaching But this means, that as it were, the emotional or devotional element cannot be left out of consideration. They've not prepared themselves simply by study. They've not prepared themselves simply by the observance of the precepts by also by devotion, by being devoted to the Buddhas, by praising the Buddhas.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Might it not also be that the (see) passed this crystallization, as well?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, I think this is implied. (long pause)

And further of course is the fact that they are assisted by the Buddhas. What does one mean by this? How did Conze's comments go? "It is through the might of the Buddha, through his sustaining power, through his help, that all the Bodhisattvas, the great beings, come to hear this deep perfection of wisdom, that they bear it in mind, recite and study it, demonstrate, explain, expound, repeat and copy it, and that they progressively train in Thusness. Known they are to the Tathagata (with his heavenly eye), sustained they are by the Tathagata (with his Wisdom-eye) seen they are by the Tathagata (with his Dharma-eye), surveyed they are by the Tathagata with his Buddha eye." So what is "being assisted by the Buddhas"?

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Being inspired?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Being inspired. Could be.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Presumably it means to some extent that they're in contact with the Buddhas.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: in contact, yes. The point is made that they are known to the Buddhas and "the Buddhas will bring him to mind and assist him. And it is quite impossible to impede anyone who has been brought to mind and upheld by the Buddhas." It's as though there's a sort of contact, even a sort of communication, between him and the Buddhas, and it's within this sort of communication that inspiration is given. No doubt the Bodhisattva has to [129] think of the Buddha, but if he thinks of the Buddha, the Buddha will think of him. So contact will be established between them, communication will be established between them, and this will be a source of strength to the Bodhisattva, a source of inspiration to him. So this is also why some Bodhisattvas, at least at that time, the time of the last epoch, will be able to respond to the truth of Sutras such as this.

turns.speaker: Conze goes on to say
paragraphs: "In virtue of the 'merit' which they have acquired, and continue to acquire. In our present age, with spirituality observably at a very low ebb, the achievement of enlightenment is normally not feasible. All we can do is to lay the foundations for it at a future period when conditions will again be more auspicious. 'Merit' is the terms for these foundations."
I think he's going rather beyond what the Sutra says or what the Buddha actually says in the Sutra.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: This is more a sort of Theravada view.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, it is very much so.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Does this being assisted by the Buddhas and the contact, communication with the Buddhas that's implied by it also imply Insight? Could you be in any sort of real contact with the Buddhas without it?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I would say that you could not because you wouldn't. To be in contact with the Buddha is surely to be in contact with the Dharmakaya and you cannot be in contact with the Dharmakaya without Insight. Whether it would have to be Insight carried to the point of Stream-Entry, that is another matter.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: What distinction are you making there?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, between Insight which falls short of Stream-Entry and Insight which does not. It goes back to my parallel with little grains of dust on the scales. It's not so much that an individual flash of Insight is not Insight, but there's not enough of those flashes to tip the scale to such an extent that Stream-Entry is attained. So it does seem to me that one could

speak in terms of being in contact with the Buddha and having Insight - that is to say with regard to the Buddha - even though the scale had not been tipped, even though your flashes of Insight were not yet sufficiently numerous and powerful to tip the scale in that particular way. Anyway, I think we'd better get on.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Is quite a bit of it not repetition? [130]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Of repetition? In a sense, no. Sometimes there are subtle differences but where there are repetitions we won't indulge in repetitions. Right! Would someone like to read on? The next section from the Sutra and all the comments after that:
Chakkhupala (reading):

And why? Because, Subhuti, in these Bodhisattvas (1) no perception of a self takes place. (2) no perception of a being. (3) no perception of a soul, (4) no perception of a person. Nor do these Bodhisattvas have (5) a perception of a dharma, or (6) a perception of a no-dharma. (7) No perception or (S) non-perception takes place in them.

A wise attitude is here defined by the absence of eight kinds of misconception, or false assumption. The first four refer to mistaken beliefs in some kind of an individual ego, current among Non-Buddhists. The four terms, i.e. 'self', 'being', 'soul' and 'person', have occurred already in chapter 3, and they are nearly synonymous. Buddhist usage distinguishes them as follows: (i) No perception of a self, i.e. no belief in the existence of an ego as distinct from the five skandhas, an 'ego' being defined as that of which we could say, 'this is mine, I am this, this is myself'. (2) No perception of a being, i.e. no belief in a continuous separate individual identical with himself at different times, who could validly differentiate his internal constituents from what is outside him. (3) No perception of a soul, i.e. no belief in the existence of a unifying and vivifying force within an individual organism which, for the span of a life-time, would persist from conception to death. (4) No perception of a person, i.e. no belief in the existence of a permanent entity who would migrate from rebirth to rebirth.

The last four items refer to misconceptions about the nature of dharmas, current among Buddhists. (5) No perception of a dharma, i.e. the Bodhisattvas know that dharmas do not exist, and they reject the Abhidharmist conception of a multiplicity of separate dharmas, or ultimate entities. (6) No perception of a no-dharma, and that for two reasons: (a) The negation of these multiple dharmas opens up one vast emptiness. This 'no-dharma' is more real than the dharmas, but cannot be the object of an possible perception. (b) The 'no-dharma' envisaged here is not a simple and clear-cut negation, in that emptiness lies somewhere between affirmation and negation, between 'is' and 'is not'. Furthermore, these Bodhisattvas have (7) no perception at all, because perception normally assigns properties to things, and

they know that this arrangement is not founded on fact. They also have (8) no non- perception, because they continue to perceive things as other people do, in spite of their knowledge that these perceptions, and the assumptions they take for granted, have no ultimate reality. [131]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
