paragraphs: That's reasonably clear isn't it? "And why? Because, Subhuti, in these Bodhisattvas (1) no perception of a self takes place, (2) no perception of a being, (3) no perception of a soul, (4) no perception of a person. Nor do these Bodhisattvas have (5) a perception of a dharma, or (6) a perception of a no-dharma. (7) No perception or (8) non-perception takes place in them. A wise attitude is here defined by the absence of eight kinds of misconception, by false assumption an ego being defined as that of which we could say, 'this is mine, I am this, this is myself'."

The five skandhas are rupa, vedana, samjna, samskhara, vijnana, and these are considered to represent an exhaustive classification of the entire ever-changing psychophysical so-called 'being' of the individual; so that there is nothing that we experience which is not one of these - nothing that we experience of our so-called 'self' which is not to be included either in the categories of rupa or vedana or samjna and so on.

So to posit any sort of ego entity lying, as it were, outside the five skandhas or existing within them or lying outside them or existing above them which is you and which is unchanging and permanent his is the wrong perception of a self. The Bodhisattva does not have that kind of perception - he sees with regard to himself that the five skandhas are all that exist. As it says at the opening of the Heart Sutra: "He looked down from on high and he saw the five heaps and he saw that in their own being they were empty." So: "No perception of a self, i.e. no belief in the existence of an ego as distinct from the five skandhas, an 'ego' being defined as that of which we could say, 'this is mine, I am this, this is myself."

And of course, the five skandhas themselves are not regarded as constituting an ego: "(2) No perception of a being" (sattva), i.e. no belief in a continuous separate individual identical with himself at different times, who could validly differentiate his internal constituents from what is outside him."

It's as though the no-perception of a self negates the concept of an ego apart from sort of side by side with the skandhas, sort of coexisting in space with the skandhas. Here what is denied is "a continuous separate individual identical with himself at different times." That is to say, unchanging. "Who could validly differentiate his internal constituents from what is outside him". That is to say differentiate them presumably as changing and unchanging - himself being persisting, unchanging and only the external constituents changing, only what is outside himself changing. So: "No perception of a being i.e. no belief in a continuous

separate individual identical with himself at different times, who could validly differentiate his internal constituents from what is outside him. (3) No perception of a soul, i.e. no belief in the existence of a unifying and vivifying force within an individual organism which, for the span of a [132] life-time, would persist from conception to death." This is slightly more tricky. It would seem that there is a life force holding things together which life force disappears or whatever it may be at the time of death. Perhaps the wrong emphasis is on 'that it persists', unchanged, presumably. That changing life force would of course be included in the five skandhas. And (4) No perception of a person, i.e. no belief in the existence of a permanent entity who would migrate from rebirth to rebirth." That is to say, persisting unchanged again, separate from the five skandhas. So, "The last four items refer to misconceptions about the nature of dharmas current among Buddhists."

We've dealt with misconceptions among non-Buddhists, now misconceptions among Buddhists. That is to say, the Hinayana, or more especially, the Abhidharmikas. (5) No perception of a dharma, i.e. the Bodhisattvas know that dharmas do not exist, and they reject the Abhidharmist conception of a multiplicity of separate dharmas, or ultimate entities." They reject the dharmas as ultimate entities and not as provisional intellectual or provisional (word unclear) constructions.

(6) "No perception of a no-dharma, and that for two reasons: (a) The negation of these multiple dharmas opens up one vast emptiness. This 'no-dharma' is more real than the dharmas, but cannot be the object of any possible perception." It cannot be discriminated as an object 'out there'. That would make it something (...?) (b) The 'no-dharma' envisaged here is not a simple and clear-cut negation, in that emptiness lies somewhere BETWEEN affirmation and negation, BETWEEN 'is' and 'is not'." Is this clear? 'Between' is not to be taken literally of course. It is not that the dharmas absolutely do not exist, they have no ULTIMATE existence. It's not that they are negated altogether and become non-existent. They have a relative existence. So they are negated in the sense that their existence is regarded as a qualified relative existence, not as an absolute existence. So it's not that one goes from regarding them as absolutely or ultimately real as in the Abhidharma to regarding them as absolutely unreal in the Mahayana. No, the Mahayana regards them as relatively real - not as non-existent.

(7) "No perception at all, because perception normally assigns properties to things, and they know that this arrangement is not founded on fact." That is to say, perception assigns properties to things as distinct from the things themselves. This kind of perception, which is wrong perception, distinguishes between attributes and substances - in Western terminology - does not realize that so-called things are only sum totals of their attributes and that the attributes are not in fact sort of stuck onto a separate substance. So "no perception at all" because perception normally assigns properties to things and they know that this arrangement
is not founded on fact, in fact only properties exist. You can see where [133] the nominalism comes in.

"The also have (8) no non-perception, because they continue to perceive things as other people do, in spite of their knowledge that these perceptions and the assumptions they take for granted, have no ultimate reality". it is not that the Bodhisattva doesn't see people, doesn't see trees, and doesn't see houses, he sees them, he has the same perceptions, the same sense impressions as other people, but he doesn't take them as absolutely real, doesn't take them as ultimately real, ultimately valid. He is not misled by appearances, even by the things that he actually in a sense sees. The illustration is just as you look at a stick which is inserted in water, you see that it's bent, but you know that the stick is in fact not bent, you know that the fact that it is perceived as bent is an optical illusion. if you thought that the stick was in fact bent, that would be a delusion So we look around and we see everything as bent but we know that it is not really bent. We look around and we see people, trees, houses, we see them, but we know that there are no people, trees, houses, there are only dharmas, or not even that, the dharmas so to speak are sunyata. (long pause)
All right, we'd better push on.

"And why? If, Subhuti, these Bodhisattvas should have a perception of either a dharma, or a no-dharma, they would thereby seize on a self, a being, a soul, or a person. And why? Because a Bodhisattva should not seize on either a dharma or a no-dharma. Therefore this saying has been taught by the Tathagata with a hidden meaning: 'Those who know the discourse on dharma as like unto a raft, should forsake dharmas, still more so no-dharmas."

Here we have first of all an allusion to the well-known Mahayana distinction between two stages of the comprehension of 'selflessness' (nairatmya). It is not enough to be convinced that there is no 'self' in persons. An impersonal dharma also must be seen as nothing in or by itself, as indistinguishable from any other dharma. The Sutra here claims that the second step is a necessary consequence of the first, and that it alone can prevent a relapse into the 'heresy of individuality'. The reasoning, though subtle, is quite intelligible: No separate dharma can possibly be perceived without a subjective act of perception taking place. Now the Abhidharma teaches that the specific function of perception consists in 'taking up' an object, 'noting and recognizing' it, 'seizing upon' it. Similarly, in our own language, 'perception' comes from per-CAP, and cap means 'to take hold of, seize, grasp'; but to seize on anything. either a dharma or a no-dharma, automatically involves an act of preference, bound up with self-interest, self- assertion and self-aggrandizement, and therefore unbecoming to the selfless. As it is said in the Ashta (A xv 305): 'As contrary to the ways of the whole world has this dharma been demonstrated. It teaches you not to seize upon dharmas, but the world is wont to grasp at anything.' [134]

"The reference at the end is to the Simile of the Raft, which Mahayanists knew from the Sarvastivadin Scriptures. It also occurs in the Pali Canon, and I quote Woodward's translation from Majjhima Nikaya: 'Using the figure of a raft, brethren, will I teach you the Norm, as something to leave behind, not to take with you. If one has crossed with the help of a raft a great stretch of water, on this side full of doubts and fears, on the further side safe and free from fears, one would then not take it on one's shoulders and carry it with one. But though it was of great use to him, he would leave it behind, and have finished with it. Thus, brethren, understanding the figure of the raft, we must leave righteous ways behind. not to speak of unrighteous ways.'

"With a hidden meaning: On the face of it, the word 'dharmas' in this saying of the Buddha means 'virtues', and so have Buddhaghosa, Woodward and I. B. Horner (BT no. 77) understood it. By taking 'dharmas' not as a moral, but as a metaphysical term, meaning 'entities', our Sutra here discloses the 'hidden meaning' of the simile. This method of interpretation assumes that the Buddha, endowed with supreme skill in means, often chose expressions which could mean one thing for the less, and another for the more advanced, thus proving helpful to both.

"The example of the Raft shows that dharmas should be treated as provisional, as means to an end. The same holds good of 'emptiness', the negation of dharmas. This corollary has elsewhere been illustrated by the simile of 'the medicine Agada, which can heal any illness. Once a cure has been effected, it must be abandoned together with the illness, because its further use would only make one ill again. Just so when this medicine, called "emptiness", has brought about a cure of the disease of the belief in existence. Attachment to emptiness is a disease much as attachment to existence. Those who continue to use this medicine of "emptiness" after they have gained possession of full wisdom, only make themselves ill again'" (T 159, 328c).

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Though of course, if one had really gained possession of full Wisdom, one would have hardly continued to use the medicine of emptiness one would have thought. Perhaps gaining possession of full Wisdom is not to be understood literally.

"Both dharmas and no-dharmas." I mean this is sort of quite clear, quite standard, that the Abhidharma analysed so-called persons and so-called things into dharmas, but they stopped there, they regarded the dharmas as ultimate. The Mahayana insisted that you mustn't stop there, you mustn't regard the dharmas as ultimate. The dharmas themselves are Sunyata and you should go a stage further. Well even in sunyata you shouldn't settle down, in other words it is as though at every level both practical and theoretical we know the metaphysical [135] level, you must just go forward, you mustn't settle down anywhere, you must take everything

as a means to an end not as an end in itself, hence the parable of the raft. Whether one understands it more in ethical terms as the Buddha seems himself to have understood it in the Pall Canon, or whether one understands it metaphysically as here, one mustn't settle down at any stage. This perhaps is not unconnected with what we were talking about a little while ago about the decline of Buddhism, not the decline of the Dharma, but the decline of Buddhism. Buddhism declines when it settles down thinking, well, this is Buddhism, this is the dharma, when it is only one particular sort of crystallisation of the Dharma at a particular time for the sake of a particular people.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: I'm not quite clear how having analysed things into dharmas you could then relapse into the heresy of individuality.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Because you transfer the notion of individuality to the dharmas themselves, that would seem to be the norm You think of them as real existent things, existing so to speak in their own right independently.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: So it is not that you'd fall back into the idea of a self or a person or a....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well you would but that would be applied then to the dharmas, would reappear in your attitude towards the dharmas, the way in which you regarded the dharmas themselves. Perhaps the word individuality is misleading here, it is really regarding as self-subsistent entities.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Conze talks about it in terms of you have still got a subtle object, therefore you have got a subtle subject which perceives that so that would be the individuality presumably.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, you could look at it like that. As well as there is an object there must be a subject and vice-versa. (pause) No separate dharma can possibly be perceived though a subjective act of perception can be taking place. So you take up and you note and recognise and then you seize. (long pause) I suppose it isn't easy to regard Buddhism as provisional, only as a means to an end. It is very difficult to regard it in that sort of way and at the same time maintain one's faith. I think this is a sort of practical and in a sense psychological difficulty. People seem to be able to develop faith only when they absolutise something, or to be able to develop faith in something only to the degree that they've absolutised it. It is as though it is a bit difficult to regard something with faith without at the same time [136] regarding it as an end in itself.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: I remember Ratnaketu in a talk saying, well, the Dharma it is a raft but you don't leave it out in the rain.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Not so long as you need it. (long pause) Any more general points arising out of that section? Buddhism as a raft, that is the main theme.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: In the original Pall dharmas translates righteous ways does it?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: What would unrighteous ways be?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Adhamma. But do you see the Mahayana reads this hidden meaning into that parable of the Buddha by taking the dharma in its metaphysical meaning in the sense of an ultimate constituent of existence rather than in its straightforward ethical and religious sense. Whether that is historically justified that is another matter, but this is one of the things that the Mahayana tended to do, to read, so to speak, deeper meanings into some of the teachings or traditions or sayings that they'd inherited from the Hinayana in its Sarvastivadin form. (pause) With regard to this medicine - "Just so when this medicine called 'emptiness', has brought about a cure of the disease of the belief in existence. Attachment to emptiness is a disease as much as attachment to existence. Those who continue to use this medicine of 'emptiness' after they have gained possession of full Wisdom, only make themselves ill again. You could also say, if you use the wrong kind of medicine you don't make yourself ill again, you just never get better at all, or if you use the medicine unintelligently and this would seem to be what happens to Buddhism itself when people don't understand what is really meant or how it really should be used and they use it unintelligent]y, it doesn't help them and in that way decline is accelerated. (pause)

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Do you think there is any, as it were, institutional way in which you could guard against this crystallisation and decline?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's quite an interesting question. In other words how can you have a perpetually self- renewing institution or perpetually self-renewing tradition, because even Zen doesn't seem to have succeeded. I say even Zen, perhaps I shouldn't but Zen has not succeeded in doing this. [137]

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: A perpetual revolution.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well wasn't this the problem of Stalin versus Trotsky? Wasn't this Mao's problem in a way? About three or four years ago some people professed to see a sort of hardening of the arteries in the FWBO itself didn't they, do you remember? I didn't altogether agree with this but some people were thinking and speaking in terms of the good old days, the good old days of Balmour Street basement, there being a sort of constant continuous decline since then.

(laughter) Oh yes, one or two of the people used to sort of look back rather nostalgically to the days when we were just a dozen or so people meeting in a cosy little basement, and when Bhante was around all the time and did everything in the good old days. I sort of observed rather sharply that I didn't myself consider those the good old days at all, when I used to have to take everything myself. Perhaps it could be that things have steadily improved.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Presumably if the Order came to be totally identified with the FWBO then that would be quite a bad step. It would start to lead to that crystallisation. Whilst you've got committed individuals actually outside the FWBO in a sense they wouldn't be totally conditioned by what is happening there, sort of they come back into contact or look at it from outside, they can see the tendencies (which happen).

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes well one must continue to be able to distinguish between the individual and the group, even the positive group. And perhaps sometimes the individual or proto-individual or developing individual has to remove himself from even the group just so that he can see more clearly that the group is just a group even though it is a positive group.
(end of tape) [138]
S:.... spiritual community, with other members of the spiritual community. I remember reading a work of science fiction once by, what was the name, was it Edward Clarke?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Arthur C. Clarke.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Arthur C. Clarke, yes, I think it was "City in the Stars". The theme of this was some city in the future, some millions of years ahead, where a perfect society had been reached. A perfectly regulated society, and there was I think a master computer, a master brain, running the whole thing. But an in-built disturbance was there, a sort of derangement of the system had been planned to take place every few thousand years. Because the derangement would sort of galvanise people to life as it were and make them realise what they actually had, and the value of it, so they would not any longer take it for granted. So there was this built in derangement of the system planned to happen every few thousand years. It's as though things were planned to go wrong and not to work properly. This is the sort of thing you are thinking of presumably. The only solution I have been able to think of is to plan things in such a way that when things do go wrong they can be seen to go wrong and go wrong so badly that someone has to do something about them. Do you see what I mean?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Yes.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: In other words to prevent the possibility of things such as pseudo-success.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Thinking of the decline of Buddhism I can see what you are saying about the crystallisation, but it still seems hard in a way to imagine how an unenlightened person would be unable if he really decided to do so, to find one more enlightened person. So it almost seems what presumably must happen is that people who do develop, they as it were spread themselves too thinly. Perhaps they are in institutions where they have to sort of become abbots and they do a certain amount of administration work and they are dealing with two hundred people. [139]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I would have thought that would have given them golden opportunities!

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: But presumably if they are not enough in a personal contact with individual people, then....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But if they are enlightened and if they are seen as being so, you can presumably see that someone has enlightenment or a degree of enlightenment by the way they tie their shoe laces eh?

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: But presumably you actually have to have a deeper personal communication with that person to really penetrate deeply into them, into their mind.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: One wonders. That doesn't seem to be borne out by Zen stories that one reads of people chopping wood and pounding grain for years on end even decades without exchanging more than a word with the abbot or master, yet they seem to be in touch.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: They do seem to have some very direct confrontations with one another don't they?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Occasionally. At long intervals, it seems, I would have thought, that if there was an enlightened abbot around and he was running things he would be constantly in contact, that way surely something of his enlightenment will rub off. Even if he was only dealing with accounts or things of that sort, because will be dealing with other people through that medium, would he have necessarily to talk about the Dharma as such in conceptual terms, he might do from time to time.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: I'm not thinking that he'll have to talk about the Dharma in conceptual terms but it would seem that he would need to have close contact.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That is one way of looking at it, that is certainly the way in which some masters have worked, but not all have worked in that way. Which would suggest that one must not insist that Dharma crystallises only in that particular way. [140]

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: I suppose one could extrapolate with what seems to happen say with order members in centres who get in a situation where there are mitras who can really benefit from contact. If they had two hours contact they would become order members, but they don't actually get to see the order members because the order members are too busy running the centres.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't think it works quite in that way on the level of relative enlightenment. A little enlightenment will go a long way, and enlightenment contact will go a long way.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: In that case it seems very odd to me that somebody who is enlightened can't find one person who....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: If the enlightened person did allow himself to be elected abbot then surely he should know what would be the best way in which he should operate. I think it's more perhaps that the enlightened person has become an enlightened person, say within a particular tradition, that of Buddhism, and for one reason or another perhaps purely fortuitous in some cases that particular tradition doesn't appeal to a lot of people they therefore no longer contact him through that tradition. you see what I mean? That they don't come near enough to him in that framework. Of course you might say if he is enlightened why doesn't he go out to make himself known to them, what about the Bodhisattva's skillful means? Here it does seem that people from the Mahayana point of view have really fallen the wayside. No doubt it is difficult for one enlightened being to produce another enlightened being, it is difficult on another level for one order member to produce another order member, why not the exponential growth? Why not? Or why shouldn't the world be flooded with enlightened beings by this time?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: You almost end up with a pratyekabuddha.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Enforced pratyekabuddha, because, nobody comes and listens to him, nobody wants to hear him as you might say. But again on the other hand, doesn't one find, one notices, at certain periods there seems to be quite a sort of plethora or swarm of sort of enlightened beings around with, say, the Kargyu school at a certain period, the Nyingma, even the Chan people in China in Tang and Sung times. It does [141] seem that certain masters have the ability to spark off a large number of people rather than not even just one, but others seem to have been able to spark off only a few. It would seem quite difficult to understand that. It's almost that they come in waves. Are some masters more enlightened than others? Are some

Buddhas more Buddha-like than others? Are some Bodhisattvas more skillful in their use of skillful means? Of course there are Bodhisattvas of different status but... Maybe sometimes circumstances do help or hinder as the case may be.

When the FWBO was started some people came along to us because we looked a bit hippy- like, or at least they could see us in that way. If we had not looked a bit hippy-like maybe they would not have come along to us. If we had been solidly identified with asceticism and puritanism and middle-class values perhaps they would not have come. Perhaps they were wrong in identifying us with hippidom, as certain other people were in identifying Buddhism with puritanism and asceticism. But at least that identification, although mistaken, enabled them to come along and make contact with us.

Shantiprabha:: So is it perhaps that with enlightened teachers they perhaps, there is some particular quality which they do posses which does not actually go to really attracting people in that way? When people, you could posses qualities which would produce, what you need to do is actually possess a quite large range of qualities which would attract people from different spheres.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That doesn't help because then people think that you are inconsistent. Well you are behaving in one way with some people, another way with some other people. People don't know where they stand with you. They like to know exactly where they stand with you, that you are definitely following a devotional line, or that you are definitely following an intellectual line, you are definitely ascetic, or definitely hedonistic. It confuses them if you are now this and now that, one thing at one time and another thing at another time. So in a way you cannot but to some extent have a sort of [142] narrow range of appeal it would seem. Do you see what I mean? You can't win, but on the other hand you have to; perhaps we should leave it there.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We got as far as chapter 7 so would someone read that and the following comments right down to but not including 2dc.
"2db. The Dharmabody as the rest of Gnosis

"7. The Lord asked: What do you think, Subhuti, is there any dharma which the Tathagata has fully known as 'the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment', or is there any dharma which the Tathagata has demonstrated? Subhuti replied: No, not as I understand what the Lord has said. And why? This dharma which the Tathagata has fully known or demonstrated - it cannot be grasped, it cannot be talked about, it is neither a dharma nor a no-dharma. And why? Because an Absolute exalts the Holy Persons.

"Mahayanists are fond of saying that the Buddha's enlightenment is not a real fact, and that likewise the Dharma preached by the Buddha should not be misunderstood as a definite teaching of definite facts. In the large Prajnaparamita Sutras the theme of this chapter has been treated at much greater length. A few parallels from the 'Version in 8, 000 Lines' may throw light on it, and show the connection with the basic doctrines of (1) the marklessness of all things, of (2) their emptiness, and (3) of their Suchness.

"(1) At A.viii.192 Subhuti asks: 'All dharmas have therefore really not been fully known by the Tathagata?' The Lord replies: 'It is just through their own essential nature that those dharmas are not something definite. Their true nature is a no-nature, and their no-nature is their true nature; for all dharmas have one mark only, i.e. no mark. It is for this reason that all dharmas have really not been fully known by the Tathagata. For there are not two natures of dharma, but just one single is the nature of all dharmas. And the true nature of all dharmas is a no-nature, and their no-nature is their true nature. It is thus that all points of possible attachment are abandoned.' (2) As for emptiness, we have A.xvi.313-14. Subhuti asks: 'How can the Lord say that full enlightenment is hard to win, exceedingly hard to win, when there is no one who can possibly win it? For, owing to the emptiness of all dharmas, no dharma exists that would be capable of winning enlightenment. If all dharmas are empty, then also that dharma cannot exist which, as a result of the demonstration of Dharma, we are meant to forsake. And also that dharma which would, or should, be enlightened in full enlightenment, and that which would, or should, cognize (the utmost reality) -all these dharmas are empty. In this manner I am inclined to think that full enlightenment is easy to win, not hard to win.' The Lord replies: 'Full enlightenment is indeed hard to win, because (for lack of a cause) it cannot possibly come about, because in reality it cannot take place, because it offers no foothold to discrimination, and because it does not lend itself to the fabrication of fictitious appearances.' (3) The connection with Suchness is explained at A xxvii 453, where Subhuti asks: [143] 'If, O Lord, outside Suchness no separate dharma can be apprehended, then what is that dharma that will stand firmly in Suchness, or that will know this full enlightenment, or that will demonstrate this dharma?' And the Lord replies: 'Outside Suchness no separate dharma can be apprehended, that could stand firmly in Suchness. The very Suchness, to begin with, cannot be apprehended, how much less that which can stand firmly in it. Suchness does not know full enlightenment and on the dharmic plane no one can be found who has either known full enlightenment, will know it, or does know it. Suchness does not demonstrate dharma, and on the dharmic plane no one can be found who could demonstrate it.' These three quotations should suffice to make the teaching perfectly clear.

"This dharma, i.e. the ultimate reality, in both its objective and subjective form, cannot be grasped, i.e. at the time when it is heard one cannot seize upon it as either a dharma or a no- dharma. It cannot be talked about, i.e. at the time when it is preached, one must remain aware that the talk aims at something so high and transcendental that words cannot ever reach it. It

is not a dharma, not a separate thing accessible to discriminative thought. It is not a no- dharma, it is not the negation of a dharma. Psychologically, a negation gives sense only when warding off an attempted affirmation. Where there is no temptation to make positive statements, negations likewise lose their meaning. In other words, dharmas, as strictly empty, cannot even be denied.

"The last sentence of the chapter fittingly concludes this argument by indicating the conclusive reason behind it, i.e. the fact that 'this Dharma which the Tathagata has fully known and demonstrated' is the ultimate and unconditioned reality. Because an Absolute exalts the Holy Persons. The Sanskrit defies translation, and reads: asamskrtaprabhavita hy aryapudga.

"The Holy Persons are traditionally eight, i.e. Stream-winners, Once-Returners, Never- Returners and Arhats (see chapter 9), each one considered either at the moment of entering on his 'Path', or at that of reaping his 'Fruit'. They are called 'holy' in contradistinction to the 'foolish common people', about whom more will be heard at chapter 25 and 3ob. Buddhist tradition, in fact, distinguishes two classes of people, the 'common worldlings' and the 'saints' (aya), who occupy two distinct planes of existence, respectively known as the 'worldly' and the 'supramundane'. The saints alone are truly alive, while the worldlings just vegetate along in a sort of dull and aimless bewilderment. Not content with being born in the normal way, the saints have undergone a spiritual rebirth, which is technically known as the 'winning of the Path'. In other words, they have detached themselves from conditioned things to such an extent that they can now effectively turn to the Path which leads to Nirvana. Only they can really be said to 'tread the path' in any proper sense of the term. The worldling's vision of Nirvana is obstructed by the things of the world which he takes far too seriously. [144]

"Through prolonged meditation he can however reach a state where, each time a worldly object rises up in front of him, he rejects it wholeheartedly as a mere hindrance or nuisance. Once this aversion has become an ingrained habit, he can at last take Nirvana, the unconditioned, for his object, 'he ceases to belong to the common people', and 'becomes one of the family of the Aryans'. So Buddhaghosa, who explains the situation in great detail and with admirable lucidity in chapters 21 and 22 of his Path of Purification. Thereafter the disciple is less and less impelled by the motives of ordinary people like us, i.e. by motives which are a compound of self-interest and a misguided belief in the reality of sensory things, and which contain a strong dosage of greed, hate and delusion. The contrast with the vision of Nirvana reveals the insignificance and triviality of all these worldly concerns, and Nirvana itself increasingly becomes the motivating force behind whatever is done. In this way the Unconditioned seems to become a kind of condition, the essentially Inactive seems to do something, and that which is nowhere seems to become localized in a definite individual.

"The Absolute is literally 'the Unconditioned', and according to Vasubandhu, it means here that which cannot be discriminated. With an obvious and deliberate disregard for logic the Sutra claims that this unrelated Absolute can enter into a relation with certain persons. This is a difficult idea, and a difficult word is chosen to express it. Exalts - the word prabhavita contains a great wealth of meanings, and 'exalts' is the best I can do. One could also say, 'are glorified by', 'draw their strength from', 'owe their distinction to', or 'derive their dignity from'. The idea is that the holy persons have 'arisen' from the Unconditioned, have been 'produced' from it, are 'brought forth' by it. It is as a result of their contact with the Unconditioned that they become mighty and powerful, that they 'thrive'. It is by means of the Unconditioned that they 'prevail' and 'excel'. And finally, they are 'recognized', 'characterized', or 'defined' by it, in a sense they are 'revealed' by it. It is their true nature to be the Unconditioned, and that idea is further developed in chapter 9.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The Lord asked: What do you think Subhuti, is there any dharma which the Tathagata has fully known as 'the utmost right and perfect enlightenment' or is there any dharma which the Tathagata has demonstrated? - Subhuti replied: No, not as I understand what the Lord has said. And why? This dharma which the Tathagata has fully known or demonstrated - it cannot be grasped, it cannot be talked about, it is neither a dharma nor a no-dharma.

The basic point here in a way is quite simple; that when we want to talk about something which transcend s our experience we can still only talk of it in terms of our experience, that is the basic point. We speak of the Buddha as knowing enlightenment. Bu we speak of him as knowing enlightenment on the analogy of our own ordinary knowledge. So far as we're concerned knowledge involves a subject; the knower and an object; the thing known. In other words the whole framework of our knowledge is dualistic. So we cannot but think of the Buddha's [145] knowledge of enlightenment as being of that kind. There is the Buddha; the enlightened knower and there is enlightenment; the object of his knowledge, and it is in that knowledge of that object - enlightenment - that his enlightenment consists. But this is entirely a false analogy, we have taken the wrong model. Because to begin with we can say, in the state of enlightenment there is no distinction, no division of subject and object, as there is in our own experience, as there is in our own knowledge, so how can there be an object which the Buddha knows? You can't really think of enlightenment as an object, because that would mean that enlightenment was party, so to speak, to a duality, or member of a duality which by definition it isn't, it cannot be. So for this reason the Lord asked: What do you think, Subhuti? "Is there any dharma," - is there any object as distinct from a subject - "which the Tathagata has fully known as 'the utmost, right, and perfect enlightenment' or is there any dharma which the Tathagata' has demonstrated? - Subhuti replied: 'No, not as I understand what the Lord has said. And why? This dharma which the Tathagata has fully known or demonstrated - it cannot be grasped" - because it is not an object anyway - "it cannot be talked about" - for the same reasons - "it is neither a dharma nor a no-dharma."

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Is there a play on the meanings of dharma here, dharma as object and dharma as teaching?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Slightly I think, because the Buddha demonstrating a dharma means giving a teaching, but on the other hand it can be understood as making known an object, talking about something conceived of as an object as distinct from a subject, which of course "the utmost right and perfect enlightenment" cannot be.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Does Sanskrit actually use capitals, does it distinguish between Dharma and dharma?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No. Sometimes they use, in Pali also, there is the little word, indeclinable, "iti". That means sort of end of direct quote, so to speak. Yes it's usually at the end not at the beginning: "Iti 'pi so bhagava araham samma-sambuddho" It is an abbreviation of iti because the initial 'i' is elided, because a vowel immediately precedes. Iti - 'thus', end of quote. This is how [146] people described the Buddha or the Dharma or the Sangha. 'Iti' is used in place of quotation marks. In Hindi for instance they say at the beginning of the (?ki) because there is no distinction, no direct and indirect narration in Hindi. You can't say in modern Hindi "I said that he went", you have to say "I said (?ki) he went", anyway that's by the way.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: But there's no distinction, there's no way of telling from the original, whether it's being, dharma is being used with a capital D as in teaching, or small D...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, not even in the written text, in the devanagari alphabet there's no distinction of capital letters there, as I believe in Greek and Latin.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: So it could be that "This dharma which the Tathagata has fully known or demonstrated," that could be Dharma with a capital D. Conze's choice which it is.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. The fact that in English, or in roman characters, we have upper and lower case forces the choice upon the translator which the original does not in fact require him to make. But looking at it even more broadly, I think this chapter is simply saying, as in a way the whole of the "Perfection of Wisdom" teachings say, as they remind us that experience transcends thought, that thought and words are not adequate to experience. This in fact all that these teachings are saying, even the categories of Buddhism itself, the doctrinal categories, are not to be taken literally, not to be taken as ends in themselves, are to be regarded as pointing to experiences. So there is no specific experience known as "utmost, right and perfect enlightenment" we should take or treat as an object and then present as an object to other people. I mean, "the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment" is not that sort of thing, so to

speak, it's not a thing at all. And the quotations from the Astasahasrika (Perfection of Wisdom in 8,000 lines) just elaborate upon that. Or rather, perhaps, this particular chapter is a condensation of what those particular passages say. So in a way that chapter is quite clear isn't it. One mustn't transfer to the unconditioned the various categories that one has derived from one's experience of the conditioned.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Is that what this 'suchness' is, our experience of the conditioned? [147]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: 'Suchness' is a very strange term. It expresses really the indefinability of things, the impossibility of defining anything, the impossibility of describing anything. You can only say it is such as it is, or it is thus.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Tathata

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Tathata, yes. It is just like that. You can only sort of point to an experience. So things are what they are, they are such as they are, they are not to be adequately conveyed by concepts or words.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: The word tathata...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, the abstract noun.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Presumably that is the same sort of word as Tathagata.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, it's the same basic word. Tathagata is the one who has 'thus gone', or 'gone in such and such a way', or come, it can be grammatically interpreted as either. Thus come or thus gone, because he neither comes or goes of course. In the text called "The Awakening of Faith in the Mahayana" the term suchness is used.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: It seems extraordinary in a way that so much of this Perfection of Wisdom literature should have grown up and been necessary. It seems that people have been very, very attached to ideas about the Dharma for...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: ...excessively intellectual emphasis or conceptual approach that needed to be very vigorously counteracted. That's why in a sense it's almost as though the Perfection of Wisdom teaching is a bit irrelevant in the West, at least for English people, who are not very metaphysically prone, or over intellectual. It might be the sort of thing that ought to be given to the highly trained Catholic Theologian who thinks that Catholic Theology, perhaps especially the Theology of St Thomas Aquinas, has got everything cut and dried and tidied up and neatly disposed of once and for all.

Well what about this phrase which is here translated: "because [148] an Absolute exalts the Holy Persons"? This is very interesting: asamskrtaprabhavita by arya-pudgala. He says that "The absolute is literally the Unconditioned" but this is not really true at all. This is quite a slip on Conze's part. It's literally the uncompounded, which might amount to the same thing, but he does say it means "literally the Unconditioned", which it doesn't. The uncompounded, the un-put-together, the literal translation is the in composite or the impartite. "And according to Vasubandhu it means here that which cannot be discriminated." that is to say, cannot be discriminated as an object.

"With an obvious and deliberate disregard for logic the Sutra claims that this unrelated Absolute can enter into a relation with certain persons." Well no, there's no deliberate disregard for logic in this case because, I mean, Absolute means that which is not related. But then asamskrta doesn't mean the Absolute, it means the uncompounded, and there's no logical contradiction in the uncompounded entering into a relation with something which is compounded, or even with something else which is also uncompounded. A little slip on the part of the good doctor here.

So, "This is a difficult idea, and a difficult word is chosen to express it. Exalts - the word prabhavita contains a great wealth of meanings, and 'exalts' is the best I can do. One could also say, 'are glorified by', 'draw their strength from', 'owe their distinction to', or 'derive their dignity from'. The idea is that the holy persons have 'arisen' from the Unconditioned,"

This is perhaps reminiscent of something the Buddha says quite often in the Pali texts, addressing the monks: 'you are my own true sons, born of my mouth, born of the Dharma, the heirs to the Dharma, not heirs to worldly things.' It might be reminiscent of that.

"Have 'arisen' from the Unconditioned, have been 'produced' from it, are 'brought forth' by it. It is as a result of their contact with the Unconditioned that they become mighty and powerful, that they 'thrive'. It is by means of the Unconditioned that they 'prevail' and 'excel'. And finally, they are 'recognized', 'characterized', or 'defined' by it."
There seems to be a little bit of a contradiction here: "In a sense they are revealed by it. It is their true nature to be the Unconditioned, and that idea is further developed in chapter 9."
The force of the "because" is rather difficult to see, it is certainly not the logical...

"And why? This Dharma which the Tathagata has fully known or [149] demonstrated - it cannot be grasped, it cannot be talked about, it is neither a dharma nor a no-dharma. And why? Because an Absolute exalts the Holy Persons." Maybe they want to put an emphasis on

Absolute, "because an Absolute exalts the Holy Persons". They are filled by, inspired by, empowered by something which absolutely transcends thought, which transcends expression. Perhaps you can see it in action in their lives but you can't really grasp it intellectually, you can't categorise it, you can't define it.

turns.speaker: Gerry Corr
paragraphs: Did the Abhidharma not have a list of asamskrta-dharmas as well as samskrta dh armas?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, different traditions have different lists.

turns.speaker: Gerry Corr
paragraphs: It's not the same thing here?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, here Nirvana is meant.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Are all the words in quotation marks on page 39 possible translations of 'exalts',

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, not quite all: 'are glorified by', 'draw their strength from', 'owe their distinction to, or 'derive their dignity from'. All these are possible translations. Prabha is something like 'influence', so asamskrtaprabhavita is more literally ' the holy persons are made influential by.' By the Unconditioned, by their participation in or identification with the Unconditioned they become influential, they exert influence, they exert power, they possess dignity, they are exalted, glorified. The most literal translation I imagine, would be 'that the holy persons are made influential by the Unconditioned, or the uncompounded'. That is to say, by virtue of the realisation of, or to the extent of their realisation of, because they haven't all realised it fully, these holy persons are those at the beginning of the stream. But the idea of asamkrta is definitely of influence, prabha is more like influence than anything else, so exalts, I don't think it's really the closest. 'Draw their strength from' is the nearest, [150] I think, of the equivalents that he has given. Or putting it more in our own terms, one could say that as you pass more and more from the reactive mode to the creative mode, the creative acts through you more and more powerfully.

turns.speaker: Gerry Corr
paragraphs: I suppose it's what you could call 'other power', self and other.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No it doesn't correspond to self and other power, because both are you. First of all you identify yourself with the reactive mode, then you identify yourself with the creative mode, so it is not strictly speaking some other power or force is working through you. Because you -- here again you mustn't think of that Absolute as an object out there which you are in contact with. Conze puts it in a way quite neatly: "The contrast with the vision of Nirvana reveals the insignificance and triviality of all these worldly concerns, and Nirvana itself increasingly becomes the motivating force behind whatever is done. In this way the
Unconditioned seems to become a kind of condition, the essentially Inactive seems to do something, and that which is nowhere seems to become localized in a definite individual."

This raises the questions which I have discussed other occasions, I think in one or two of the question and answer sessions. This isn't easy to express, when for instance someone attains enlightenment it's only too easy to think of enlightenment as some sort of object existing there, waiting to be realised, before someone realises it. So if one thinks in those terms then it does seem as though when somebody realises it an inactive enlightenment or Absolute becomes active or manifests through that particular person. But that is only a way of thinking and speaking, one mustn't take that too literally, you could say using the language of this sutra, well, the holy persons are exalted, but there's no Unconditioned that exalts them. You might say that they exalt themselves, but that could lead the way to misunderstandings. No, I think one is safest dwelling upon the two points that I made at the beginning. That is to say, in the first place one should not think of the Buddha's experience of enlightenment in terms of our own experience of things which we've discriminated as separate objects, and we must all the time remember that experience transcends thought. These are really the two relevant points here. Thought does [151] not exhaust experience, abstract thought does not exhaust t experience.
Alright let's go on to 2dc. "The Dharma body as the result of merit..." Can someone read right through down to the main subdivision 3.
"The Dharmabody as the result of Merit
"The Dharma, it is true, cannot be grasped or taught, it is neither an entity nor a nonentity. Nevertheless, as productive of merit, the hearing and teaching of that Dharma is not useless.

"8. The Lord then asked: What do you think, Subhuti, if a son or daughter of good family had filled this world system of 1, 000 million worlds with the seven precious things, and then gave it as a gift to the Tathagatas, Arhats, Fully Enlightened Ones, would they on the strength of that beget a great heap of merit?-Subhuti replied: Great, O Lord, great, O Well-Gone, would that heap of merit be! And why? Because the Tathagata spoke of the 'heap of merit' as a non-heap. That is how the Tathagata speaks of merit'.-The Lord said: But if someone else were to take from this discourse on dharma but one stanza of four lines, and would demonstrate and illuminate it in full detail to others, then he would on the strength of that beget a still greater heap of merit, immeasurable and incalculable. And why? Because from it has issued the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment of the Tathagatas, Arhats, Fully Enlightened Ones, and from it have issued the Buddhas, the Lords. And why? For the Tathagata has taught that the dharmas special to the Buddhas are just not a Buddha's special dharmas. That is why they are called 'the dharmas special to the Buddhas'.

"The world system of 1,000 million worlds; this corresponds roughly to what we would call a 'galactic system'. Buddhist scholastics distinguish three kinds of universe according to their sire: (1) A universe which comprises 1,000 suns, 1,000 moons, 1,000 Jambudvipa continents, 1,000 heavens, 1,000 hells, etc. (2) A universe which contains 1,000 worlds of the first type. (3) A still larger unit, envisaged here, which combines 1,000 worlds of the second type.

"The merit to be derived from giving depends, among other things on the preciousness of the gift, and the quality of the recipient. The gift consists of the seven precious things, mentioned also at chapter 11, 19, 28, 32a. They are: gold, silver, lapis lazuli, coral, gems, diamonds and pearls. When this most precious of all material gifts is given in abundance to the Tathagatas, who are the most exalted persons of all, then this act of giving produces a quite exceptional amount of merit. This sounds strange to modern ears. The Tathagatas have all they need, they certainly can make no use of all this gold, silver, etc., and so how could this gift benefit them? Would it not be very much more to the point to relieve the sufferings of those who are in want, and to give them what they need, and what [152] they would use so gladly? Is there not a complete and callous disregard of the poor in the Buddhist statement that the best gift is that which is given by the dispassionate to the dispassionate? Common sense as we understand it must withhold - its approval from this statement. Some meditation on the subject is perhaps called for, and it will be all the more fruitful when preceded and accompanied by actual acts of generosity.

"Heap of merit - merit is that which either guarantees a happier and more comfortable life in the future, or, alternatively, increases the scope of spiritual opportunities and achievements. One speaks of a 'heap' (skandha), a 'stock' or 'store', of merit because the merit which people have acquired in the course of many lives is popularly regarded as a kind of accumulation on which they can draw for various purposes. In fact, however, this heap is really no heap, because all its constituents are just empty, and there is no really existing bond which unites them into 'one heap' belonging to anyone.

"The merit derived from material gifts is called great, that derived from the spiritual gift of the Dharma immeasurable and incalculable, 'inconceivable, incomparable and measure less', as A iii 71 adds. To be generous with material things brings a 'great' reward-wealth or reputation in a future life, or even rebirth in heaven. Infinitely greater is the reward of those who teach the Dharma. Their spiritual welfare and stature will grow by leaps and bounds, and ultimately they will become fit to gain the Dharmabody of a fully enlightened Buddha.

"For the peculiar importance of this discourse on dharma, i.e. of the Perfection of Wisdom, lies in that it is the root cause of the enlightenment of the Buddhas, of their very state of Buddhahood. From it have issued, etc. This again is a sentence repeated often in the large

Prajnaparamita Sutras. I quote A xii 254-5: 'The Perfection of Wisdom is the mother and genetrix of the Tathagatas. From her they have come forth. For it is she who has shown them that cognition of the all-knowing. From her the all-knowledge of the Tathagatas has come forth. All the Tathagatas, past, future and present, win their full enlightenment thanks to this very Perfection of Wisdom.' And the perfection of wisdom is both the cause and the effect of Buddhahood. 'From her has come forth the state of all-knowledge of the Buddhas', because, when they were Bodhisattvas, the study of the Prajnaparamita has enabled them to win Buddhahood. 'Conversely, the perfection of wisdom has been brought about as something that has come forth from the cognition of the all-knowing' (A x 210-11), because the Buddhas reveal it to those who are capable of hearing it. In it is possible to say that the Perfection of Wisdom is the true and real body of the Tathagatas. As the Lord has said, "The Dharmabodies are the Buddhas, the Lords. You should not, however, O monks, mistake this individual body for my Body! You should, O monks, see me from the accomplishment of the Dharmabody!" But that Tathagata-body should be seen as brought about by the Perfection of Wisdom' (A iv 94). The perfection of wisdom, and the merit derived from teaching it, are therefore here proclaimed as the 'real decisive cause and condition' (A iv 95) of Buddhahood. But a qualification is necessary wherever the Unconditioned is apparently being subjected to conditions, as we see from the sequel. [153]

"The dharmas special to the Buddhas: The Sanskrit word 'Buddhadharmas' may either refer simply to the attributes which the Buddhas alone possess, or to the well-known list of the eighteen special characteristics of a Buddha (see BT no. 14o), or to the constituents of the Dharmabody. They are, when ultimate truth is considered, not really properties attributable to a Buddha. In the Absolute there can be no distinction between subject and attribute, between the Buddha and his dharmas, and in consequence they are not a Buddha's dharmas. They are also not special to the Buddhas, but common to all things, as we are told later on, in chapter i7d. The Buddhadharmas lie beyond the categories of reflective thought, and each one must realize them in himself.

"In chapters 6 to 8 the consideration of the Dharmabody has led us to the very summit of existence. Up there the air is rather rarefied, and we were bound to feel somewhat dizzy at times. The Himalayas of the soul demand no less of us than Mount Everest or Kanchenjunga."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Mm... (long pause)... the main point here seems to be that any amount of worldly giving, any amount of giving of material objects is not to be compared with, is completely incommensurate with, even the smallest amount, so to speak, of the giving of the Dharma, and especially the giving of this particular Dharma of the Perfection of Wisdom. (pause) So the whole heap, so to speak, of the Conditioned, cannot be compared even with a tiny grain, so to speak, of the Unconditioned. (pause) And it's quite a thought, maybe, to bring it a little

bit down to earth, that if you go and give say one talk on the Dharma to an audience of people who have never heard of the Dharma before, if you disclose those perspectives to them which have never been disclosed to them before, then the amount of merit which you thereby generate is far greater than if you had spent, say, ten thousand lifetimes as a social worker in ten thousand different worlds, do you see what I mean? It's rather like that. (pause Perhaps people don't always realise this, how meritorious is the teaching of the Dharma, even in the ordinary sense, even apart from any direct disclosure, you know, from one's own experience of the Perfection of Wisdom. But even the preaching or the teaching or the propagation of the Dharma in a quite ordinary sense. (pause) Well, I think one has to be especially careful at centres for instance that one doesn't put, say, Dharma classes on the same level, in a manner of speaking, as yoga classes and drama groups and all the rest of it - the Dharma is [154] in a class apart. (pause) These other things are only helpful or useful or meritorious to the extent that they help you to make contact with the Dharma or at least, you know, guide you in that direction. (pause) the Buddha says:

"But if someone else were to take from this discourse on dharma but one stanza of four lines, and would demonstrate and illuminate it in full detail to others, then he would on the strength of that beget a still greater heap of merit, immeasurable and incalculable. And why? Because from it has issued the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment of the Tathagatas."

So, even in the case of the ordinary little talk on Buddhism you give perhaps to some sixth form or some women's institute, well you may be dropping a little seed, sowing a little seed which eventually for this or that person may develop into a fully blown flower.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Could you say that in just giving you're helping people, but in giving the Dharma you're helping people to help themselves?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Mm, I think the whole distinction between helping oneself and being helped by others falls to the ground to a great extent, especially within the context of the spiritual community. It isn't that when you teach people the Dharma well you just give them the tools and then say 'alright, get on with the job yourself', because that leaves out the question of, the whole factor of, the spiritual community. (pause) If for instance you teach people meditation well yes, you show them how to help themselves, you show them how to meditate, but it doesn't mean that they then withdraw into complete seclusion and have no further contact with anybody, so I think one must be - yes, people have to help themselves, have to be shown how to help themselves - one has to be careful to be seen not to preclude this whole aspect of spiritual community.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: It gives me quite a feeling of responsibility that at any moment you could actually, that, you've got the possibility of actually planting a seed.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes. Also the responsibility to implant that seed, not keep all your seeds to yourself. (pause) It sometimes seems to be a little extraordinary that some people who could do a little more just to spread the Dharma seem to be more occupied with purely personal things, not realising the needs of other people. [155]

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Do you think it's because - we talked about this once before - in the question and answers - perhaps they see giving the Dharma very much in terms of actually giving something away. I think you mentioned once...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Possibly, possibly. 1 can't say that I myself have thought of it in this way, of actually seeing people as, you know, being afraid of losing something.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: You know, quite a lot of people think in terms of not wanting to talk outside their own experience... I've heard some people say this about not wanting to teach the Dharma because they feel that they're talking about something which is beyond their experience, so they rather sort of talk in terms of less... (words unclear)...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, they do have some experience to talk about; if they want to talk in terms of their own experience, they can, in a quite simple straightforward way. I mean, are they saying that they've no experience of the Dharma? Are they saying that they've not changed in any respect? Could they not tell people how the Dharma has helped them? It's not expected that they go along to the sixth form or the Women's Institute or whatever and discourse on the thirty-two different kinds of emptiness, with all respect, because admittedly that would be beyond their experience, but is there not some experience which they have upon which they could usefully talk? Can one not talk about ideals even though one has not fully realised those ideals? Can one not talk about one's hopes? Do you think that's an honest reason in all cases - that people are in fear of going beyond their own experience ?

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Not really, I'm...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Do they really feel that every word that they say has got to be actually an expression of their own direct experience?

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: It might be a reasonable argument if they were really using their time and they weren't going out to deepen that experience... (unclear)... wholeheartedly... (unclear).

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Or are they perhaps, well is it a nice way of saying that they are afraid that their inadequacies will be exposed ?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It's a bit more like that, actually. You do put your [156] head on the block, as it were, if you go out and give a talk on the Dharma because a lot of people aren't going to like what you say, not going to appreciate it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That is a useful experience in some ways because it makes one ask oneself how seriously does one take something, does one really believe it? (pause) In the case of Order Members, if it's a case of talking about experience, one can at least speak of one's experience of going for refuge. Presumably that means one knows something about the Buddha, Dharma and Sangha, which means presumably having some sort of glimmering of an idea about Enlightenment. Because if you've no idea about it at all how can you go refuge to the Buddha? Yet this is what you profess to have done. Most people, if they pay a visit to a foreign country, have gone away for a couple of weeks or a month, and have taken some slide pictures, they wouldn't mind coming back and talking about their experiences in that country and showing their slides, they wouldn't think anything of it. So if you've made a sort of excursion into the realms of Buddhism, and you've done more than that, you come back and tell people about it in the hope of interesting them, and inviting them to make that same journey and maybe, you know, spend longer there than you were able to do, or maybe you hope to take them back with you. (pause) There's no doubt that if you go forth and teach the Dharma, you must be well prepared and know what you are talking about - in view of what we were saying - and be very aware of your audience, in a position to meet objections.

Maybe quite a few people do shrink from the effort that that involves, but that effort, in a way, is inseparable from one's spiritual life, especially if one has any sort of regard at all for the Bodhisattva Ideal. (pause) Then what about offering of gold, silver etc. etc. to the Tathagatas? In what sense does one offer gold, silver? I think Conze's perhaps being a bit unrealistic here. They aren't around actually to receive it, so in what way, in what sense, does one actually give it if one gives at all? Perhaps to images, I suppose. (pause) I suppose one could say instead of having those nice golden images at Sukahavati - they're not solid gold but we did have to spend a bit of money on them - well one might have been better advised to spend that money on soup for the poor [156] of the locality but is that really justified? Do you think that is so? Could the money have been better spent on soup for the poor of Bethnal Green? (pause)

turns.speaker: Gerry Corr
paragraphs: There's possibly a balance to be struck.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well we haven't spent any money on soup for the poor of Bethnal Green, (laughter) as far as I know. In fact probably the poor of Bethnal Green don't eat soup any more, (they don't need) soup any more. Very likely there aren't any poor in Bethnal Green.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Better off than the average co-op worker.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Mm. (pause) But there are poor, we could send money to India, plenty of people there would be glad of soup or thick gruel. (pause) I think the main point is that if your offering of gold and silver to the Buddha does represent genuine devotion to, commitment to, the spiritual life, spiritual ideal, that devotion, that commitment, is of such overwhelming importance for humanity at large, that anything that strengthens it is to be justified even though it is to the detriment of certain other things which may well be good in themselves too.(pause) That's why I'm quite in favour of building stupas because, provided they aren't built for the sake of rather worldly objectives like world peace - which is a worldly objective compared with the gaining of Enlightenment - if you just build a stupa for the sake of the Buddha, you know, an act of devotion to the Buddha, I think that's fine because it's sort of, from a worldly point of view, totally useless - preferring the unconditioned at the expense of the conditioned, which I think one should do sometimes - all the time if possible if you're allowed to. But yes, build stupa, as some of our Japanese friends have done, for the sake of world peace, seems a bit of a compromise. Not that I'm against world peace. (pause)

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: In building that stupa aren't they, as it were, trying to invoke something of the spirit of the Dharma?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, I think it would be more effectively invoked, perhaps, if they forgot about world peace and just dedicated it to the Buddha, hmm? (pause) Quite apart from the fact that one is not completely convinced about the genuineness of their desire for world peace, as it gets sort of caught up with Japanese politics. [158]

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Also they aren't very aesthetic.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Although that's a separate consideration, obviously the stupa should be as beautiful as possible, if one does get around to building one. (pause) And as big as possible - Not just a little tiny one, just, you know (two acres in extent). Yes. "The Perfection of Wisdom is the mother and genetrix of the Tathagatas." In some ways it's unfortunate that prajna is grammatically feminine, uh? Because one does then have the conception of the mother of the Buddhas inasmuch as it is, so to speak, the Realisation of prajna, (unclear) prajnaparamita, which makes a Buddha a Buddha. In that way prajna gives birth to the Buddhas. There is room here for, sort of, what shall I say, matriarchal religion to creep in (unclear). (pause) One has to be careful how one approaches this.
Shantiprabha:: What does 'genetrix' actually mean?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: 'Genetrix' is the feminine form of genetor or progenitor: the one who produces, in the sense of giving birth to. (long pause) The fact that 'prajna' is the feminine gender just indicates the

paradoxical, not to say contradictory approach of these texts. (long pause) Any further points arising out of this? (extremely long pause) Hmm... (unclear)... I'm just... "the merit derived from material gifts is called great, that derived from the spiritual gift of the Dharma immeasurable and incalculable." So a material gift is a material thing, it's finite, so the merit which it produces can't but be finite. But in the case of the Dharma, it's not a thing, it's not something material, there's no question of any quantity, mm? Do you see what I mean? It's something qualitative, so the merit deriving from it is immeasurable and incalculable. One could also think in terms of one's teaching of the Dharma, preaching of the Dharma, as producing a sort of chain reaction. You plant a seed in somebody else, well they plant a seed in others, they plant seeds in others still (hopefully). So presumably your merit just goes on increasing and increasing. There's no end to it, no stop to it. (pause) One's again stressing that what is unconditioned is of quite a different order from that which is conditioned. (long pause)

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Where the Buddha asks, whether giving on that scale would [159] "beget a great heap of merit. Subhuti replied: 'Great, O Lord, great.' "And why? Because the Tathagata spoke of the 'heap of merit' as a non-heap. That is how the Tathagata speaks of 'heap of merit." Um, the "And why?" and "Because" are not logical connectors. Why does he drop that in?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think he's trying to guard himself against any possible accusation by the Buddha that he hasn't really understood, hmm? Do you see what I mean? Er "would they on the strength of that beget a great heap of merit?" So, "Great, O Lord, great, O Well-Gone, would that heap of merit be." Yes, indeed, he agrees with the Buddha, but then he hastens to say "And why? Because the Tathagata spoke of the 'heap of merit' as a non-heap." as if to say 'you're not going to catch me out' (laughter) 'I'm not saying it's really great, or it's really even a heap, hm m? All right I'm talking that language if you insist, but I'm not fooled by it any more than you are.' - he's showing that. It's a little bit like a Zen, "That is how the Tathagata speaks of a 'heap of merit' ' - 'that's how you put it isn't it?' You see, there's a constant switching over between, what shall I say, the Absolute point of view and the relative point of view, hmm? Because the exigencies of the actual practical spiritual life are there. And one has to, one has to, in talking about the, in dealing with them, one has to take one's stand, so to speak, on the relative. But again one has, has to constantly bring in the Absolute, the Unconditioned, so that one doesn't settle down in that relative point of view, one just sort of uses that approach.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: So we're using the language really, as we said the other day, so that we crystallise something, and then as soon as it starts to harden we just dissolve it again.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes, yes. In some ways this literature will attempt an impossible task. Because if it's to be true to itself it can't say anything at all. It goes on constantly making statements, making

points which are quite (?hard) and then hastily negating them, lest you should misunderstand them, or take them too literally. It does produce a really peculiar atmosphere. Telling you to do something and then telling you either that you mustn't do it, or haven't in fact done it at all, or should think that you haven't done it.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Or that you don't exist. [160]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes. There's a constant, almost violent oscillation between these two, in a sense, contrary points of view: the relative and the Absolute, the conditioned and the Unconditioned, the provisional and the Ultimate.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: In a way our language doesn't really seem suited to it because it doesn't seem...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well no language is suited actually - no language is suited to it. Sanskrit is no more suited to it than is English.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: I thought always with Sanskrit the implication was that if a word had 'a', like 'subha' and 'asubha', then it wasn't just a negative. The implication was always there that maybe it was talking about something else. Whereas when we say 'no' or 'not'...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well... It's not a matter of whether it's grammatically negative - yes, it can sometimes mean something positive, but that positive is a relative thing, it's positive in relation to a negative, so the language, even Sanskrit, doesn't go beyond that sort of relativism. All you can say is that it exploits its limitations more successfully. In some ways it's a more dangerous instrument. If it is a near perfect instrument, you may be unaware of its essential imperfections. Do you see what I mean? Sanskrit is so articulate, so adequate as a language, it does express subtle differences so clearly, so beautifully. You may be tempted to forget, if you are a Sanskrit (unclear), or if Sanskrit is your mother tongue you may be tempted to forget that even Sanskrit cannot express the Unconditioned. Even the language of the gods, as the Brahmins like to call it, the devapasha, even that falls short. It is not Buddhapasha, it's only devapasha.

turns.speaker: Greg Harman
paragraphs: Bhante, concerning merit, is there any way, any other way in which you could see merit? I mean, I find it a little bit funny - you do something and you get paid so many pounds worth of merit for doing something.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Do you get paid?
Greg Harman:... (unclear)...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, perhaps from that point of view the most useful way of thinking about merit is the effect, the sum total of the effect, that your actions have upon you yourself: your actions modify you and you're left with that modification, that is your merit. It's [161] like if you do exercise it's not that you just do exercise and you're rewarded for that by an increase of your muscles. Do you see what I mean? It's not a reward, it's the natural result with which you are left - you are changed, you are different, your muscles are bigger. So in the same way, if you perform skillful actions that changes you, it modifies your being, you are left with some positive gain, and that positive gain is what we call merit, punya. You become a more positive person, a better person. One can look at it in that way too.

turns.speaker: Greg Harman
paragraphs: It does seem that er, through, um this word merit seems to be like that. I mean if you give um... something in return...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, in a sense there is a return in the sense that there is a result, there is a positive result, something does accrue to you, your actions do affect you, they rebound upon you, so something rebounds to you, to your credit. Obviously one shouldn't think too much in these terms - some Buddhists do. They're keen on their merit. Bhikkhus as potential sources of merit, provided they're good bhikkhus. Some laymen in some Buddhist countries seem to regard bhikkhus as sort of merit-making machines. Put a penny or a rupee in the slot, and out comes the packet of merit. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: I've heard that somewhere they sold their merit.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, that's true. That is an incident mentioned in the 'Survey', where a Jain - not a Buddhist - a Jain ascetic, who practised asceticism for many years, then decided he wasn't going to continue the spiritual life, but he'd accumulated a lot of merit, so he sold it to another ascetic (laughter) and with the proceeds he set himself up in business (laughter). I actually heard this story in India. (pause) The Jains are even more literalistic than are the brahmins, than some brahmins I should say. It's not perhaps beside the point that Bhagavan Rajneesh was born and brought up as a Jain. (unclear)... He's possibly still reacting against his faith. (pause)

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: The popular version of this idea of merit seems to leave aside the state of mind with which you actually perform the action.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. Yes, it seems to be regarded as an almost mechanical process. (pause) There's a curious conception which I've never [162] been able to investigate. I've heard of it in, I think it must be in popular Theravada, of the punyadevata: the merit-god. There's a sort of punyadevata hovering over you. I remember my friend Sangharatana referring to the punyadevata, every now and then. I don't know whether it's a canonical conception, I rather doubt it. But the concept of your merit as calling into being a sort of punyadevata.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: An individual...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
