but one stanza of four lines, and were to demonstrate and illuminate it to others, [206] then they would on the strength of that beget a still greater heap of merit; Immeasurable and incalculable.

"12. Moreover, Subhuti, that spot of earth where one has taken from this discourse on dharma but one stanza of four lines, taught or illumined it, that spot of earth will be a veritable shrine for the whole world with its gods, men and Asuras. What then should we say of those who will bear in mind this discourse on dharma in its entirety, who will recite, study, and illuminate it in full detail for others! Most wonderfully blest, Subhuti, they will be! And on that spot of earth, Subhuti, either the Teacher dwells, or a sage representing him.

"This is what I announce to you, etc. - a formula often used for formal pronouncements by a Buddha. - The saying about the shrine, caitya, is repeated in chapter 15c. It is taken over from the Version in 8,000 lines (iii 5): 'The place in which one takes up, bears in mind, preaches, studies, spreads, demonstrates, expounds, explains and repeats this Perfection of Wisdom, in it beings cannot be hurt by men or ghosts, nor can they be injured or overpowered by them, except as a punishment for their past deeds. Because this Perfection of Wisdom makes the spot of earth where it is, into a true shrine for beings, worthy of being worshipped and adored - into a shelter for beings who come to it, a refuge, a place of rest, and final relief. This is another advantage which Perfect Wisdom confers even here and now'. According to B. C. Law, caitya is the most general name for any sanctuary, and it may refer to a Stupa, a vihara, an assembly hall, a tree, a memorial stone, a holy relic or object, or even an image. A shrine is a sacred place set apart, and it should be honoured with gifts of flowers, and so on. In past ages these shrines were something to reckon with. Respect for them assured the prosperity of nations, and they were inviolate sanctuaries for people in fear for their lives. - The Teacher is the Buddha. A Sage, guru. is a spiritual preceptor.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Is there any new idea introduced in these two chapters?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: The idea of the shrine, that this spot of earth should be a shrine.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. The idea of the shrine. "That spot of earth where one has taken from this discourse on Dharma but one stanza of four lines, - taught or illumined it, that spot of earth will be a veritable shrine for the whole world with its gods, men and asuras." So what does one make of this notion?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Is it stressing the importance of the Perfection of Wisdom?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It's certainly stressing the importance of the Perfection of [207] Wisdom, but what about the way in which it is doing that? What is a shrine? Conze gives this sort of etymological

explanation. What he says is very true. Is there any sort of sense in which what you do, whether it's teaching and illumining this particular text or anything else which is skillful. I mean is there any sort of actual effect on the place itself as such, by virtue of which it is transformed into a shrine? Or is it just a question of association? Is it just skillful means? This seems to be a very strong tendency in humanity, isn't it, to venerate certain places as shrines. For instance even in modern England, I think in most European countries, in America too, places where famous people have lived and worked, famous writers, famous poets, famous politicians, famous statesmen, painters, these are kept up at the national expense. They are sort of national shrines. So is there any particular significance that their particular birthplace or place where they actually did work should be kept up, does some sort of mystic aura cling about it? Has something of them literally rubbed off onto their immediate surroundings so that in a way you are more closely in touch with them when you visit that place, or is it just the association of ideas? Does the fact that the Perfection of Wisdom has been expounded anywhere make any difference to that place, even objectively? I mean in what sense does it become a shrine? The same thing applies to all the places where the Buddha lived and taught, obviously. Or if you, say, make a pilgrimage to Dr Johnson's birthplace in Lichfield do you actually get any closer to him? Does it make it more real to you? Is he objectively present in a sense, there as he isn't say in certain other places.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Well obviously it does by association because we know he came from [208] there, his writing desk is there.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, at least there is that.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: You certainly feel as if you can get closer to him, but whether you can actually…

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes. Well you can envisage him more clearly because you can envisage his surroundings and I think a lot of people feel that going to India and seeing the places where the Buddha lived and taught, some of which are much the same as they were in the Buddha's day. Many of the sights and sounds which you can see and hear in India today are similar to those you could see and hear in the Buddha's own day. A lot has changed, but a lot hasn't changed. In India when visiting the Buddhist Holy Places one does seem to get nearer to the Buddha as an historical personality, but k anything of the Buddha, so to speak, rubbed off onto those shrines so that they're endowed with a sort of sanctity. I mean this is really the point here isn't it? Over and above the mere association of ideas.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I can't see any theoretical reason why not, but I can't actually say that I (unclear) it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Or is this simply what this passage simply what is called prasana, that is to say, a praise, a sort of hyperbolical praise which is not to be taken too literally. 'Moreover, Subhuti, that spot

of earth where one has taken from this discourse on dharma but one stanza of four lines, taught or illumined it, that spot of earth will be a veritable shrine for [209] the whole world with its gods, men and Asuras. What then should we say of those who will bear in mind this discourse on dharma in its entirety, who will recite, study, and illuminate it in full detail for others! Most wonderfully blest, Subhuti, they will be! And on that spot of earth, Subhuti, either the Teacher dwells, or a sage representing him.' These are very, very strong words indeed, aren't they?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I suppose you get some experience of it with a shrine room don't you, because a shrine room builds up an atmosphere. There definitely does seem to be something objective that people....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes. Certainly that people can perceive who don't know anything about what you've been doing there, no understanding at all, maybe don't even know that you've been doing anything there perhaps. Well I don't know whether Adam reported last year he felt any difference in the atmosphere. It could be simply that the very paraphernalia of the shrine helped create that atmosphere; you take them away - does the atmosphere remain? One might think that it did, but perhaps you would have to be quite sensitive to perceive that. One knows that places do have atmosphere of various kinds.

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: Can one also think that, say, a famous person or a holy person actually chooses a place like the Buddha chose the tree, because it is such a … it has these qualities.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes there is that point too.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Have you felt any strong connections with rupas that you've come [210] across?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: In what way?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Well rupas which have been used for long periods of meditation for quite long periods of time. I wondered whether you'd directly perceived any difference between different rupas.

S.. There is the point that some rupas seem to be almost sort of alive. I remember that when I was living on my own out at Highgate in the very early days of the Friends, I had two rupas which I'd brought from India: one of the Buddha Shakyamuni and one of Avalokitesvara, the four-armed Avalokitesvara, and I just kept them in my study-cum-sitting room. I often used to feel that they were two people as it were sitting there. You've been in that room haven't you? Do you remember? There was a bookcase, my big heavy bookcase, at the back of the

room and there were these two images on the thing and when I was sitting and working there especially I would quite literally feel that there were two sort of people just sitting there. Not that they were just two inanimate images, but there were actually two people sitting there. I can't say that I personally worshipped these images a lot, but perhaps they have been worshipped quite a lot in the past. I had that feeling very strongly. But there was another experience I had with a friend's image in Bombay that I've mentioned sometimes, some of you must have heard: a friend of mine who perhaps I met on my last visit, a quite elderly lady who became a Buddhist, she had several Buddha images, and one in particular she had on the window sill - the inside sill- a little greenstone image, some sort of (unclear) [211] semitranslucent. Anyway when I went to see her one day in Bombay, this was about 20 odd years ago, she was quite excited and as soon as I arrived she asked me to take my seat in a low armchair just opposite this image so that this image was on the sill - just about where Chakkupala is sitting, round about that distance, perhaps a little nearer, - and also the window looked out over the Iranian Sea. And she just asked me to sit there and see if I could see anything, so I sat and looked for a while and then I saw around the image a sort of green halo - not a circular halo - but the figure became edged with a band of green light of the same shape as the image itself, and as I watched this gradually expanded. It was vivid green, I've always liked fluorescent lighting, and bright green, and it gradually filled the entire room. So she was asking me, 'Can you see anything, can you see anything? So I told her. She said, 'Yes that's what I saw, the servants saw it too and they were quite scared' because they were (unclear) Christians. Well after a while it just faded. Of course as soon as I started seeing it I looked from side to side to see if there was any reflection, or whether it was the light coming from the sky, but no. It was clearly as though the whole thing had been lit up with florescent lighting very strongly. There was nothing delicate or subtle about it. It was almost crude. So how is one to explain that? The only possible explanation I suppose is that someone must have worshipped it for a long time. It was endowed so to speak with someone's personal magnetism. So a few, a few experiences of that sort I've had. (pause) Of course the Orthodox Hindu idea and the Vajrayana Buddhist idea of images is that once they're established they're endowed with life. I've mentioned this in my memoirs haven't I? [212] There is what we call a (unclear) a life-establishing ceremony. Sometimes it coincides with the painting in of the eyes of an image, but the image is supposed to be brought to life. Popular Indian belief, -this is non-Buddhistic, - describes some images as particularly 'javata' or awake, that is to say, particularly lively, as centres of some kind of energy. It's understandable that an object upon which many people's thoughts have been focused should become sort of alive. I think I've mentioned before the story told, in fact, by Bhikkhu Sangharatana - one of my old friends - that when he went to Burma with the relics of Sariputra and Moggallana, on one occasion he and the other bhikkhus and several thousand people present saw the relics actually circling round inside their little glass capsule, and he himself attributed it to the force of thoughts of all the people present. He didn't regard it as anything supernatural. I don't think he thought anything much of it at all. So it does seem that any sort of concentrated mental activity can have sort of physical effects and therefore, why not, the effect on the surroundings where the activity takes place. Maybe the effect doesn't last for ever, perhaps only for a short period, but
perhaps there is some effect. But even then - can there be such a thing as a transcendental effect?

That is another question isn't it? Is there any perceptual difference between, say, a very powerful mundane effect like that, say, produced by someone in the dhyanas and a transcendental effect, something produced by a transcendental element, as would seem to be suggested here? Can the transcendental produce effects in that sort of way? Well, I mean via the human organism presumably. Conze has mentioned that earlier on hasn't he. Where he says: 'In this way the unconditioned [213] seems to become a kind of condition, the essentially inactive seems to do something, and that which is nowhere seems to become localised in a definite individual'.

One could also say that the whole thing is a sort of skillful means, I mean the Buddha is aware that caityas and shrines are greatly venerated. They become focuses, focal points, of national piety. So he is, as it were, trying to direct some of that piety in a refined but sublimated form towards the Dharma itself. In the Pali scriptures many caityas are mentioned. There seems to be an especially large number of them in the Licivi country in Vaisali. The Buddha seems to have spent many pleasant hours here among them. Conze is perfectly right when he says:

'A shrine is a sacred place set apart, and it should be honoured with gifts of flowers, and so on. In past ages these shrines were something to reckon with. Respect for them assured the prosperity of nations, and they were inviolate sanctuaries for people in fear for their lives.'

In medieval England, of course, there were three great national shrines, that of Thomas Becket at Canterbury and Our Lady of Walsingham at Walsingham in North Norfolk and Glastonbury with its holy thorn down in Somerset.

People went on pilgrimages, as we know from the Canterbury Tales, to all these places. There's something of that sort now in visiting houses associated with great writers and so on. Some people visit the graves of the war dead. The war memorials - it's all the same kind of thinking. In Westminster Abbey there's even Poet's Corner isn't there? So perhaps the Buddha is trying to harness some of this popular devotion in the interests of the Dharma itself to the cause [214] of the Dharma. But, all that apart, there's no doubt that the Buddha is stressing very powerfully, the importance and significance and value of this sort of teaching. That it is so holy, as it were, that something of its holiness apparently rubs off, even on to the place where even a little of it is taught.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It does seem a very valuable (unclear) doesn't it. We have going on a pilgrimage which is going to these caityas.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: What sort of shrines would they have been at Vaisali in the Buddha's lifetime?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, Conze quotes B.C. Law, who is an Indian authority on the subject. "According to B.C. Law, caitya is a most general name for any sanctuary. It may refer to a stupa, a vihara, an assembly hall, a tree, a memorial stone, a holy relic or object, or even an image. Literally caitya means something heaped up, perhaps it was originally no more than a little heap of stones to which some sort of sacredness was attached. I mean someone seems to have been putting up little caityas all around Il Convento - I don't know what the villagers will make of them - they'll probably think there's some kind of magic going on. In India today you often see stones at the foot of a tree - the banyan tree. I mean some trees are considered especially sacred in India, not only the peepul tree, the tree under which the Buddha was Enlightened, but the banyan tree and a tree called the, what is it? The tree sacred to Shiva, the bell tree. There are five sacred trees in Hinduism altogether, [215] the asvatah, that is, the banyan, and a couple of others. So often one finds on the outskirts of village one or more of these trees, sometimes five of them planted as a group and some stones sometimes roughly shaped like a (unclear) at the foot, and then they'd be anointed with things, and people would come and offer flowers. There's just a vague feeling of some kind of sacredness attached to that tree or those trees, but nothing very explicit, nothing very clear, it's all quite vague and almost instinctive, although very much a part of local life, of village life. Sometimes it's formulated that there is sometimes some kind of spirit or devata living in the tree, but more often than not it isn't even as formulated as that. There's a sort of - in other terms - ?mana attaching to that tree. It's set apart. (pause).

Anyway, anything further about that? It does even raise the question of whether a particular spot should be invested with particular, with special sanctity or whether from a spiritual point of view one should regard all places as equally sacred. Can the sacred really be delimited in that way from a purely spiritual point of view leaving aside culture and all that?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I suppose from an ultimate point of view, - no it can't be, but if a particular spot had particularly strong associations for you that could be helpful to you in your development?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, provided it didn't only work there. For instance, you might be greatly inspired by sitting in Buddhagaya, to observe the five precepts while you were there - even meditate a little, but it's not that those things should be associated only with that particular spot, or otherwise it becomes as it is for many [216] so-called Christians, Christianity is associated only with church, with particular hours that they spend in church. You remember that when the Buddha was discussing the conditions of communal stability, he made the point, when he was speaking to the Vajjians, that they should keep up the offerings at their shrines, at their caityas. It's almost like saying they should respect their own traditions and culture. Supposing
for instance, thinking of England again, supposing no one ever bothered any more about Keats' house in Hampstead, or Dr Johnson's birthplace in Lichfield? What would that signify?

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Lack of appreciation of what could be gained from this.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, lack of appreciation of English Literature itself. It would mean English Literature was on the decline, not only itself on the decline but people weren't appreciating it very much. So it would be a symptom of cultural decline generally, national decline possibly. So keep up your shrines (pause). It is no doubt significant that in India today many of the old Buddhist sites have been restored and are being visited again. The interest is not just archeological. (pause) It's very much a living tradition.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Is that a fairly modern tendency - that the old sites are being renovated?

S.. Well I suppose so, because you've got to have old sites to begin with, you've got to have old sites that have fallen into ruin, [217] for one reason or another. In ancient times you didn't have that, you might have had ancient sites but didn't have ancient sites that had fallen into ruins unless it was the result of some invasion or something of that sort. So in a sense it is a modern phenomenon.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: I was wondering whether we could compare it to some specific event, like the Untouchables actually becoming Buddhists.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well no. The restoration of the archeological sites, the sites associated with the life and work of the Buddha, that began long, long before, that began during the last century mainly with Sir John Marshall and General Sir Alexander Cunningham. That was one of the factors responsible for Indian Buddhist revival, those men were connected with Dhammapala. He knew them - or knew of them - they knew him or knew of him. It was all happening at around the same time. At the same time or a little while before that, the inscriptions of Ashoka had been deciphered. (pause) At the time of the Renaissance people started taking an interest in the remains of classical antiquity - Virgil's tomb was rediscovered, or what was believed to be Virgil's tomb. (pause). Anyway, let's go on. 4. The First Ending… [218]
4. THE FIRST ENDING
"The source of this merit is the 'discourse on dharma', which is now named. The Sutra in its first form probably finished at this point.

"13a. Subhuti asked: What then, O Lord, is this discourse on dharma, and how should I bear it in mind? - The Lord replied This discourse on dharma, Subhuti, is called 'Wisdom which has gone beyond', and as such should you bear it in mind!
"It is noteworthy that the title of the 'Diamond Sutra' is here simply 'Perfection of Wisdom'. Kumarajiva, it is true, has 'Adamantine Perfection of Wisdom', but not so the Sanskrit.

"The second part of the Sutra presents the commentator with exceptional and so far insuperable difficulties. It is not impossible that one day someone may succeed in offering a satisfactory explanation. None has yet been found. Even Asanga, Vasubandhu and Kamalasila were often at a loss to account for the logical sequence behind the argument, and their comments are apt to be unconvincing, laboured and overingenious. My own commentary, based on their suggestions, was rewritten a number of times. Even in its final form it failed to ring true, and I must agree with my friends who pronounced it to be unhelpful, inconclusive, tedious, uninspiring and positively confusing. Far from adding to the understanding of the Sutra's spiritual message, this part of the commentary actually obscures it, though it might have some value for philologists concerned with the construction of the Sanskrit - sentences.

"Our bewilderments are perhaps due to invincible obtuseness. It is equally possible that they derive from the state of the text which has been transmitted to us. Far from representing a coherent whole, the second part of the Diamond Sutra may very well be no more than a chance medley of stray sayings. Scholars who are familiar with the conditions of literary composition in Buddhist India, and who have considered the frequent repetitions and violent transitions in this part of the Sutra, are inclined to believe that reciters at various times added a passage here or there, and that, what is more, the scribes at one time misplaced some of the palm leaves, and also added glosses from the margin into the text. In that case the sequence of the argument would be determined by a series of mechanical accidents, and I have been content with a bare translation of chapters 13 to 29, adding a few headlines to serve as tentative indications of the problems discussed, which are nearly always variations on the topics of the first part. Chapters 30 to 32 then again break new ground, and the commentary is therefore resumed when we come to them. [219]
S. So what does one learn from all this?

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: We should be careful when handling spiritual documents.

S.. Yes. Also that we mustn't take things too literally, because the teaching has come down in a form to a great extent determined by the accidents of transmission. It's quite impossible, one would have thought, for the Buddhist to indulge in anything like bibliolatry. The Buddhists don't have just one simple, straightforward, easily identifiable sacred book as the Christians and even the Muslims have. In some ways the Muslims are very lucky, in the sense that things are made very easy and simple for them. They've got one book, The Koran, the text of which is in a fair state of reliability, in fact, it would [220] seem. There are some discussions and disputes, but you've got this simple and compact volume, no bigger than the New Testament of the Bible, and that's the word of God. That's all you've got. You've got all sorts of commentaries, you've got sayings of the prophet - thousands of them - but they're absolutely distinct, absolutely separate. The Koran stands alone and that's your final authority. Even Christians, after a centuries of struggle, taking in and putting out, they settled down with an Old Testament consisting of so many books and a New Testament consisting of so many books, is it sixty-four altogether? Something like that and that's become more or less standard for the whole Christian world. All sorts of new sects arise, there are hundreds of sects, but they all accept virtually the same Bible. So Christians are in a relatively easy position. Whatever difficulties they may encounter with regard to the higher criticism when it comes to considering the origins of a particular text and the development and so on. But look at the Buddhists, I mean they're even worse off in a way than the Hindus, the Hindus have at least got the four Vedas, together with the Brahma-sutras and the Bhagavad-Gita, they're the three main works even though rather popular ones, these are clearly regarded as revealed works. But the Buddhists have got a whole library of books which is in some ways a great disadvantage because it takes time to study these things and it's sometimes quite confusing when you've got Pali canon and Mahayana sutras and tantras and then what about the Songs of Milarepa - are they to be regarded as scriptures or not? This is all quite confusing. But there's one advantage at least that we have: we are in no danger of bibliolatry, in no danger of book worship, in no danger of regarding the whole [221] truth as being written down, the truth, the whole truth and nothing but the truth, in one particular book and in one particular form of words. We are in no danger of that whatever, even the though some Buddhists have tried to commit that mistake. The (unclear), for instance, they've tried to set up the Pundarika Sutra into a sort of Bible but not with any great success so far as the rest of the Buddhist world is concerned. Even they have not been able to ignore at least some of the other Mahayana sutras, they have to give them some sort of position, tradition has been too strong even for them. Even in the case of the Theravadins, even though they do adopt a rather rigid attitude towards the Tipitaka - well it's an attitude they've developed towards a whole series of books, not just one book, almost a small library of literature. So we're in no danger of being able to hold up some small text or tract or little volume - and saying it's all here, this is all you've got to read, this is all you've got to know, this contains your salvation, we re not in that position. We have to piece it all together from many sources, many texts, many works, comparing this with that, which is in a way quite a good intellectual exercise and also helps us to maintain a sense of spiritual proportion and guards against such things as fanaticism and dogmatism. (pause) One may have one's favourite Buddhist text but that is quite another

matter. Even whole schools may grow up who only study particular texts, but that again is another matter. Buddhism has no creeds really - Christmas Humphreys has managed best with his fourteen principles but they haven't exactly caught on, especially as one or two of them are a bit doubtful anyway - especially the one about all life being one, is regarded as very [222] dubious by practically all Buddhists. (pause)

It means that we do get texts like the Diamond Sutra which according to Conze is, in any case, a compilation from the larger works of the same name but it goes along all right for so many chapters and then it seems to come to an end and then for no apparent reason it starts up again. It seems to be repeating itself, the order of ideas doesn't seem altogether clear, and the text perhaps is in a state of confusion, we're not even sure of that. It may be in a state of confusion, but it may be that we're too obtuse to see the connection of the various arguments, the various themes - all of which again does make life rather difficult, but perhaps it keeps the more studious Buddhist at least on his toes. No doubt one should always bear in mind that a sutra like this is not a systematic treatise. It's not really meant to satisfy the requirements of the logical mind. It's just like a series of sledge-hammer blows trying to break through your fundamental delusion. It's attacking you from this side and that, and it's not going to make things easy for the logical mind by putting things in a logical form. Of course it's going to be confusing, of course it's going to be rather irritating and annoying, of course it's going to be rather unsatisfactory to the logical mind, perhaps one can't expect anything else. If it was set forth all neatly and logically and arranged in such a way that the logical mind could understand it practically and clearly and there were no loose ends at all, you might be in danger then of thinking that you'd grasped the Perfection of Wisdom. (pause). [223]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I remember years ago when I was giving some of my lectures at Centre House, one of my old friends came along to listen to the lectures, she used to enjoy them very much. She was an old lady of well over eighty - she's still alive actually, well over ninety now but anyway, at the end of one of the lectures she said to me that she'd enjoyed the lecture very, very much and she said, 'You make everything so clear that we think we have understood'. (laughter) So you might say the Buddha doesn't commit that mistake - he doesn't make things in the Diamond Sutra so clear that people think they have understood. (pause)

Anyway, Conze has left quite a chunk of the Sutra now without any commentary so we shall have to struggle through it as best we can taking up such points as we can here grasp with our feeble intelligences. And when we do come back to a bit of commentary, well we can go through that too. No doubt we shall be able to glean some little points of interest.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: When did this Sutra acquire the title of Vajracchedika?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, certainly by the time Kumarajiva translated it, which I think was in the fifth century. (pause) It does occur to me, and this is only a passing thought, that Part Two can possibly represent an alternative version of the Sutra, if it was a compilation in large part, perhaps the second part represents an earlier, perhaps less successful, effort which came to be tacked on to the later more successful effort as a sort of Part Two. This is of course, in a sense, a quite profane suggestion, quite untraditional. Again it could be that the [224] connection has escaped people so far. I mean Han Shan regarded Part Two as dealing with more subtle doubts whereas Part One deals with grosser doubts. I think he even produces a list of them - eighteen gross doubts and eighteen subtle doubts, which is rather neat. He was a Zen master.
Anyway would someone like to read on. Just read 5a and we'll see if there's anything there that calls for discussion.
5. TRANSCENDENTALITY
"5a. The didactic nature of reality

"And why? Just that which the Tathagata has taught as the wisdom which has gone beyond, just that He has taught as not gone beyond. Therefore is it called 'Wisdom which has gone beyond'. 13b. What do you think, Subhuti, is there any dharma which the Tathagata has taught? - Subhuti replied: No indeed, O Lord, there is not.- 13c. The Lord said: When, Subhuti, you consider the number of particles of dust in this world system of 1, 000 million worlds - would they be many? Subhuti replied: Yes, O Lord. Because what was taught as particles of dust by the Tathagata, as no-particles that was taught by the Tathagata. Therefore are they called 'particles of dust'. And this world-system the Tathagata has taught as no- system. Therefore is it called a 'world system'. - 13d. The Lord asked: What do you think, - Subhuti, can the Tathagata be seen by means of the thirty-two marks of the superman? - Subhuti replied: No indeed, O Lord. And why? Because those thirty-two marks of the superman which were taught by the Tathagata, they are really no-marks. Therefore are they called 'the thirty-two marks of the superman.'

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well is there anything new here or does it cover ground which [225] which is in a sense familiar? - These examples all illustrate the principle of the abrogation of the law of contradiction. Are you familiar with this law of contradiction, a form of logic?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Not that familiar.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well the law of contradiction, doesn't it state that 'A' is 'A', that 'A' cannot be both 'A' and not 'A' at the same time. But the 'logic', inverted commas, of the Perfection of Wisdom asserts the direct contrary. That 'A' is 'A' because it is not 'A'. In other words the principle of

turns.speaker: contradiction is abrogated
paragraphs: 1ogic itself is abrogated or is suspended. In other words when one comes to express translogical matters in terms of logic one can only express them in terms of contradictions or paradoxes. Really one needs to reflect upon this. One needs to meditate upon this - that something is what it is because it is what it is not. Or everything is what it is because of emptiness. Clearly here is matter for reflection. It doesn't become immediately obvious. You have something because you do not have it, for instance the Buddha has the thirty-two marks of the superman - why does he have them? Because he does not have them. His having them is a no-having them. On the level of logical discourse this is nonsense. But does the nonsense convey any glimmer of translogical meaning? Does one have a faint little sort of insight into what the Buddha might be getting at, or is it just a complete blank, just apparently complete nonsense?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Most often.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Most often - Yes, you can understand the form [226] of the words but it doesn't convey any glimmer of meaning usually. Sometimes it does but as soon as you start thinking about it the glimmer of meaning disappears. So perhaps it is something to be taken up in the context of meditation. It can't be sort of reasoned about. (pause) We haven't had 'particles of dust' before have we? But the principle is the same. We haven't had 'world system' before but again the principle is the same. (pause).

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: I was thinking about the comment you made very early on, that first of all you see things as mountains and streams and then after a while you don't see them as mountains and streams and then when you are Enlightened you see them again as mountains and streams. I thought that was maybe - was part of it. (pause).
S.. Anyway, be that as it all may, there's no doubt about what the next section is called: 'The Supreme excellence of this teaching'. So perhaps we'd better go on to that.
"5b. The supreme excellence of this teaching.

"1a. The Lord said: And again, Subhuti, suppose a woman or a man were to renounce all their belongings as many times as there are grains of sand in the river Ganges; and suppose that someone else, after taking from this discourse on Dharma but one stanza of four lines would demonstrate it to others. Then this latter on the strength of that would beget a greater heap of merit, immeasurable and incalculable. [227]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Let's just stop at 13. 1 was just looking at something.

"Suppose a woman or a man were to renounce all their belongings." He's warned us that here he has translated 'personal existence', atma-bhava, as 'all their belongings'. I don't know why, because 'personal existence' would be much better: again so, 'Suppose a woman or a man were to renounce their personal existence' - in other words to sacrifice their life, ''as many times as there are grains of sand in the river Ganges." That would seem to make better sense and to be more literal. "And suppose that someone else, after taking from this discourse on Dharma but one stanza of four lines, would demonstrate it to others. Then this latter, on the strength of that, would beget a greater heap of merit, immeasurable and incalculable." So the original "of all their belongings", atma-bhava. So why he translates it as 'all their belongings' here is not at all clear. 'Personal existence' would seem to be more appropriate. Even the sacrifice of one's life, presumably the sacrifice of one's life divorced from Prajnaparamita is productive of less merit than simply teaching a little of this Sutra.

I think this is one of the major themes of the Sutra in a way - the incommensurability of punya and jnana or punya and prajna or, if you like, quantity and quality. I mean no amount of conventional religiosity can make up for - or can equal - genuine insight, spiritual insight. (long pause). [228]

We're looking at things here from a more general point of view, not so much in terms of the individual, but in terms not so much of the group but the spiritual community also. And it suggests that however great a cultural tradition we may have, however wonderful the cultural tradition, however great the intellectual development may be or however widespread and influential the religion, unless there is present in that tradition some element of insight, unless there are some people at least with insight, then you haven't really got very much. I mean Buddhism without the Dharma in it's true sense isn't really very helpful. I mean Buddhism simply as a cultural phenomenon without any element of actual insight being present. (long pause) Are there any further points arising out of that? I think it is also the first time that the text has mentioned "a woman or a man". Is it not so? Is that not a new thing? Did we not only have kulaputra before?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Son or daughter.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Ah, son or daughter.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Do you think that's just Conze's translation that he's put woman before man. Because it is son and daughter?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It depends on the Sanskrit idiom. I'm not sure. In Pali certainly, mother is always put before father. (mata, pita) so it could well be woman and man rather than man and woman. In literary Hindi or Marathi for instance they always say (?stree, push) woman and man, rather
than man and woman. It just was mainly for the sake of euphony, that they fit together better. [229] (?Stree, push) sounds quite smooth (?push, stree) sounds quite jerky. (pause) [230]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: All right, 14a... Let's go straight on.

"14a. Thereupon the impact of Dharma moved the Venerable Subhuti to tears. Having wiped away his tears, he thus spoke to the Lord: It is wonderful, O Lord, it is exceedingly wonderful, O Well-Gone, how well the Tathagata has taught this discourse on Dharma. Through it cognition has been produced in me. Not have I ever before heard such a discourse on Dharma. Most wonderfully blest will be those who, when this Sutra is being taught, will produce a true perception. And that which is true perception, that is indeed no perception. Therefore the Tathagata teaches, 'true perception, true perception'.

"14b. It is not difficult for me to accept and believe this discourse on Dharma when it is being taught. But those beings who will be in a future period, in the last time, in the last epoch, in the last 500 years, at the time of the collapse of the good doctrine, and who, O Lord, will take up this discourse on Dharma, bear it in mind, recite it, study it, and illuminate it in full detail for others, these will be most wonderfully blest.

"14c. In them, however, no perception of a self will take place, or of a being, a soul, or a person. And why? That, O Lord, which is perception of self, that is indeed no perception. That which is perception of a being, a soul or a person, that is indeed no perception. And why? Because the Buddhas, the Lords have left all perceptions behind.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hm. So does any new point emerge from these sections? Anything that hadn't emerged before?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I suppose first of all there's the emotional impact of the Dharma.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, right. "Thereupon the impact of Dharma moved the Venerable Subhuti to tears." Don't forget he's an Arahant according to the text. "Having wiped away his tears he thus spoke to the Lord: It is wonderful, O Lord, it is exceedingly wonderful, O World-Gone" etc..

So one might think it is a little strange because the form of the teaching is apparently quite abstract, but so far as Subhuti is concerned apparently it isn't abstract at all. It is very meaningful; it's very moving, eh? It's very powerful. Thereupon the impact of Dharma moved the Venerable Subhuti to tears." One could, I suppose, have quite a discussion on this topic, this topic of the - what did Darwin call it? - the exhibition of the emotions in animals and man? There is a book by Darwin isn't there, a very careful study of the way in which animals and men show their emotions, and of course, shedding tears is one of them. Does anybody

know anything about this physiologically? Anything about the physiology of weeping? Doesn't it seem a strange thing that when you're emotionally moved you should weep? [231]; you should weep, say, when you're sad, you should weep when you're happy? You should weep when you're emotionally moved, doesn't it seem strange? Has anyone ever thought about this? Why should one shed tears? I mean, what is happening? What's the meaning of it? Even in the ordinary psychological sense? Why should water start oozing from the corners of one's eyes, you know, when one is emotionally disturbed or stirred, in some way? (pause) Do animals weep?

It is said that there is only an appearance of weeping. I mean moisture may trickle from the eyes of animals but it seems not to be related to any definite emotional state in the way that it is with human beings. Perhaps we should look this up somewhere. I must confess, I don't know anything myself about the physiology of weeping. Perhaps we should look it up in the encyclopedia. What actually happens, one know it does happen, of course, and there are other signs of being emotionally moved like the hairs standing on end. This is often referred to in Indian literature. The word for that is horripilation.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: I thought the hairs standing on end was something to do with - say you get in a fight or if animals get in a fight they grow bigger (unclear) and the hairs stand on end. Maybe weeping in connected with that as well, clear the eyes in battle.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It could be.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: It's the same with blushing - blushing is a defence.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But what about when you weep for joy? Do you need to weep to see more clearly a present someone has given you, or anything like that? Anyway, the fact remains doesn't it? It is important, here, you know, that one should feel moved by the Dharma, and as in cases of this sort one is moved by the Dharma so deeply that the involuntary sort of physiological reactions do take place. Do you think people are less expressive of their emotions?

Yes, in Darwin's book it was the expression of the emotions in man and in animals, wasn't it? Do you think people are less expressive of their emotions now than they were, say in the Victorian period? For instance I've recently been reading a life of Dickens and apparently when Dickens used to give readings, people used to shed buckets of tears at the death of Little Nell and all the rest of it. And even reading his novels, I mean, as they came out in weekly parts or monthly parts, even politicians who you would have thought were tough [232] enough people, used to be found sobbing over the death of little Paul or some such episode, or, you know the misfortunes of Oliver Twist. So is it sort of cultural or is it? Do you think there is less accepted weeping in British social life now? is it a less recognized

expression of the emotions? I mean, do people, especially women, still go to the pictures to have a good cry? I remember my mother and her friend going off to the pictures when I was a boy every Thursday night and coming back saying how much they had enjoyed it, that they'd had a really lovely cry... (laughter) Has this gone out of fashion? Have we become tougher, more cynical, in a sense, less emotional? (pause) But is crying less acceptable than it was? The ancient Greeks seemed to have wept freely. Some people find it difficult to weep even when somebody dies. Tears don't seem to flow very freely. What does that mean? Does it mean that we have hardened within? Are people very often moved by the Dharma so to speak, by sort of spiritual things, spiritual issues? I think they are to some extent, but they don't show it very freely.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: I think it's partly that weeping nowadays is associated with just being in a very bad state - weeping at (unclear). All those soap operas on TV.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, I haven't watched any soap operas, but then perhaps someone who has can tell us, is there any weeping in these soap operas?

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: It's not usually the (unclear)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Perhaps it's become more associated with sorrow nowadays - or more exclusively associated.

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: It seems also that people are very afraid of being sentimental.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm, Yes. The Victorians it seems were not afraid of being what we call sentimental. Dickens certainly wasn't. But why are we afraid of being sentimental, of being what we call sentimental?

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: Presumably it's a reaction against... (unclear)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But why should we react against it? Why should we not be as happy to be sentimental as they were? Were they sentimental in a strict sense? [233]

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Sentimental is really a sort of assumed emotion.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. it seems to have been genuine as far as, I mean in the case that I've mentioned. I mean Dickens and his audience and readers seemed to have felt genuine emotion. They were genuinely moved by death scenes that tend to leave us cold, Though I must say, literary critics are beginning to revise their opinion of this aspect of Dickens' work which used to be dismissed out of hand as just weakness, even literary weakness, and nothing more. And

Dickens himself, when writing, used to laugh and cry and apparently behave rather like a madman, quite carried away by what he was writing. I don't know whether authors are still carried away in that sort of fashion. I must confess that when preparing a lecture and inserting one of my little jokes, sometimes I'm unable to repress a quiet chuckle - (laughter) especially when I think of the possible reaction from my audience.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Maybe we are not so sentimental now because we are supposed to be more secure.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That could be.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Maybe it's more connected with there being nothing sure into which emotions can flow. As if there's been a sort of attrition of values, so in a way, you don't really know what you can respond to.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. What you can weep at and what you can rejoice at.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Yes, and you're sort of numbed, you don't dare move.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well maybe for Dickens the fact that a small child should die was definitely and unambiguously tragic, but maybe we can't see it in that way. So children do sometimes die young, so what? Yes? It does seem to indicate an incapacity to respond directly in a natural sort of human way. Of course, we must be careful not to generalize too much. We are thinking presumably more of people in England, not even Scotland. I mean people are carried away by emotion, are people carried away by emotion or have I been grossly misinformed, at rock festivals? Or is it something else that happens?

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Even that doesn't seem to really be wholehearted emotion. [234] It's more cerebral. …a tingle, than a real heartfelt....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Or just something physiological? Like just jiggling around, you know, moving or shaking your limbs about. (laughter) I remember when I came back from England I just happened to see on somebody's TV, before it was hastily switched off, some sort of dance - and there were eleven year olds you know, jiggling around and doing, what was it in those days? What would it have been in '64?

turns.speaker: A Voice
paragraphs: The Twist... (laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But my impression was, I noticed their eyes, little boys and little girls, their eyes were glazed as though they were in a sort of trance. They were like little zombies, you know, just jiggling around the room. It clearly wasn't a question of expressing emotion, they were in the
grip of something, something which had affected them: more as a sort of mass phenomenon. There didn't seem to be very much in the way of emotion.

Also another factor perhaps is that in order to feel very strongly you've got to have a sort of reservoir of emotion, in other words, emotion has got to accumulate. You haven't got too many, to have too many avenues of easy discharge, hmm? I mean the Victorians were supposed to be, or Victorian society was supposed to be rather repressive, so it was as though emotion tended to get accumulated, tended to get banked up, so that when it was released it was released in a quite forceful, quite powerful sort of manner. Do you think there is anything in that? I mean probably there was much more social self-control more self-discipline. I mean mainly in the middle classes, which sort of set the standards, the norm. You couldn't always say what you thought or what you felt, so emotion needed perhaps to accumulate.

turns.speaker: Khemapala
paragraphs: I used to notice sometime on retreat if there was a lot of silence or something like that, then something would happen that would spark off a lot of people sort of giggling or something like that.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But that is not exactly emotion, that is more like repressed vitality. It is even sometimes just as simple as repressed sexuality, or at least sexuality without any definite outlet. You get it in girls' schools, a whole form, you know, goes off into hysterical giggles for no apparent reason. But do you think, thinking more of people in England, that people have lost something of their capacity to be emotionally moved, moved in a deeper sort of way as here Subhuti seems to have been moved? [235]

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: It seems also that people are afraid of, it's not that they can't but that they really try to almost avoid situations where they could be moved, they're frightened...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. That still raises the question why should they be, or why should they have become afraid in that sort of way?

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: It does seem to be the case that a lot of people even when they do actually feel things, they quickly try to - they don't accept that, well, they're going to have a good cry or (unclear). They try to stop.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: In the case of men, of course, crying has come to be considered effeminate and that is the last thing that the majority of men wish to be considered - crying is womanish, eh? Despite the Greeks, and despite the Elizabethans, not to speak of the Victorians.

turns.speaker: Khemapala
paragraphs: It does seem in some cultures it's more (unclear). I know for instance, like for example the Maoris are a lot more (unclear), I wouldn't say so much burst into tears (unclear) the Scots and the Irish seem to be more...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: A bit more emotional?

turns.speaker: Khemapala
paragraphs: Well, yes, I mean less inhibited, say.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Also 1 think people nowadays particularly under the influence of TV let other people have their emotions for them. (S: Ah, yes) You see that in those big games programmes where everybody starts jumping up because they've won a thousand pounds or something.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Also maybe because on TV, you see so much and in newspapers, you read so much, that almost we've become unable to respond. It's too much, it demands too much of you. Whereas in Dickens' day there wasn't that sort of thing.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: This might be due to. You talked about Victorians' emotion accumulating, maybe this constant entertainment enables you to have sort of little discharges of emotion all the time.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, Hmm. [236]

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: I have the impression too, that people now.. The distinctions between being a child and being an adult are more sort of blurred. Some people seem less secure in their adulthood and therefore - so if you're secure then you can quite happily cry which you could associate with childishness, and it doesn't actually threaten your basic feeling of adulthood, but if that isn't very strong then if you indulge in crying as you did when you were a little boy - big boys don't cry - then it's quite threatening.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Even women don't seem to cry so easily as they used to. They snivel sometimes but they don't seem to have a good cry, or at least if they do so, they do it on the quiet.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: They don't faint and require smelling salts. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, that was admittedly a social ploy. (laughter) Even smelling salts one doesn't often see. When I was a boy there was a bottle of smelling salts on every lady's dressing table, just in case, I can remember very vaguely when I was a boy smelling salts being applied to a lady's nostrils on certain occasions. (laughter) That's nearly half a century ago. Women do cry sometimes nowadays, I mean usually when they are not allowed to be engine drivers or something like that. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Because they cry it's very often taken as evidence that they shouldn't be engine drivers.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, indeed. (pause) I think a lot of it is to do with what I meant that there is no possibility for the accumulation of emotion. It's as though emotion flows in lots of very tiny channels so it flows very shallowly. It doesn't sort of cut out just a few rather deep channels. This shows itself in our relationships too. Perhaps we are more mobile, we have lots of more casual relationships with lots of people, even sexually speaking, people are much more promiscuous perhaps than they used to be.

They don't have just a few close and deep and strong relationships with just a few people, as I think tended to be more the case formerly when people were less mobile. I did discuss with a few people last year, or it might have been earlier this year, earlier this year, this question of fidelity. You remember don't you? And I think this is part of what I was getting at, and there's of course fidelity to the Dharma. I mean that's one aspect of commitment, isn't it? that [237] you are faithful. Tara, in Tibet is called the Faithful Tara, the Faithful Dolma, which is quite interesting. She keeps faith; this keeping faith is a very important quality.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Do you think this affects the movement to quite a large extent still?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, I mean like being faithful to your Centre, devoted to your Centre? One particular person told me recently, I think it was an Order Member, that he'd happened to be at one of the women's communities, I forget exactly in what connection, but anyway, he happened to be there and he heard a discussion going on among the members of this women's community, and he was quite horrified, he said, to hear the casual way in which they talked about leaving that community, as though they didn't feel any loyalty to that community.

So I think here this is to some extent a sort of by-product of our sheer mobility. We can go around so easily, we can change, we can switch over, we can make new friends. So we tend, perhaps, I hope I'm not generalizing too wildly, we tend to be perhaps lacking in emotional depth and therefore not to feel so strongly and therefore not to be capable of being moved in the way that people were perhaps more in the past, hmm?

I mean at the very beginning of the Movement we had this problem almost as it was then of so many people sort of shopping around, and even though they were going along to the FWBO, they'd be going along to the Sufis too maybe, or to a Zen group or to a Tibetan group. They seemed to find it difficult to settle down and give their loyalty to one particular group or one particular tradition or one particular teacher. I think perhaps, in modern times, again generalizing a little wildly, they commit themselves, or they give all their loyalty or

their faith to a particular ideal or even to a particular person they are sort of tying themselves down, Hmm? They are limiting their options. Perhaps they have a sort of wrong or misguided conception of freedom. ((long pause)

But I think it is essential that one should feel strongly, feel deeply, not only about the Buddha, Dharma and Sangha, but about your Centre, about your community, about your mitras and Kalyana Mitras. About your work. (pause) But what happens when one is deeply moved in the way that Subhuti was? "Thereupon the impact of the Dharma moved the Venerable Subhuti to tears." We have discussed the outward manifestations, the why and wherefore and all that, but what is happening when one is moved emotionally in this way, by the impact of the [238] Dharma or by any other similar impact?

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Well, you are literally moved, it has an effect.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, but what is the nature of that effect, what is happening, what is it doing to you?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: It's transforming you.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: it's transforming you, yes, or at least the possibility of transformation is there.

turns.speaker: Khemavira
paragraphs: (unclear) liberation of energies.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes. I mean it's as though something in you which has not been touched before is touched, hence you know, the tears, because that seems to be the expression of hitherto blocked up energy or at least, accumulated energy. It's as though the Dharma touches some spring in you, opens something up so that energy is discharged in this particular way; you are touched, you are moved, you are stirred.

In Pali there is a very expressive word for this sort of stated called samvegha: the state of being stirred up and moved by the Dharma. It's a quite powerful word. You are shaken by the Dharma. But I think there are two occasions on which I've seen large numbers of people deeply stirred, and on each occasion it was by somebody's death. The first was when Mahatma Gandhi was assassinated and the other was when Dr Ambedkar died. His own followers were very, very deeply upset. That was not quite the same thing because there was an element of doubt and fear in their minds, not knowing, you know, what course they would be able to follow without him. How they would manage without him, in the case of Dr Ambedkar. Because with Mahatma Gandhi, it was already clear that the country was moving in a direction other than that in which he wanted it to move, moving towards a rapid wide scale industrialization.

What sort of things move people? I mean, things of a more sort of Dharmic nature? What do you think was moving Subhuti here? What had the Buddha said? "And again Subhuti, suppose a woman or a man were to renounce all their belongings as many times as there are grains of sand in the river Ganges, and suppose that someone else, after taking from this discourse on Dharma but one stanza of four lines, would demonstrate it to others. Then this latter on the strength of that would beget a greater heap of merit, immeasurable and incalculable. [239] Thereupon the impact of Dharma moved the Venerable Subhuti to tears." Well, what was it that moved him in, what the Buddha said?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Is it that in what the Buddha said he has in fact talked about himself in a sense? He's made Subhuti realize what he himself has just done. Because he goes on to say here "how well the Tathagata has taught this discourse on Dharma" and" through it cognition has been produced."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: "Not have I ever before heard such discourse on Dharma. Most wonderfully blest will be those who", etc. It's as though what the Buddha says about, you know, grains of sand in the River Ganges and all that bit, makes him realize the overwhelming greatness of the Dharma. He's impressed and stirred and moved by the glimpse he has had of the sheer greatness of the Dharma.

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: Especially if one takes the alternative translation, actually someone who sacrificed themselves this many times, that is moving...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Even that is very stirring, yes. The fact that someone should sacrifice his own life again and again for anything, for any cause that is itself deeply moving. But then you are told that his merit is nothing in comparison with the merit of someone who teaches just a little of this Dharma, so presumably that (words unclear) even more deeply stirred and moved, and this is perhaps what happened to Subhuti. Perhaps one could generalize a bit and say one of the things that stirs and moves one is the spectacle of greatness or sublimity. Beauty doesn't move or stir one in this sort of way, it's sublimity. In aesthetics at one stage in the 18th century there was quite a discussion about the difference between beauty and sublimity. There is a treatise by the young Burke on the sublime and the beautiful. Kant also took up this question. And the sublime moves you and stirs you, I think they sort of concluded, because it represents something grand, something overwhelming, something infinitely powerful, and in comparison with that in the case of obvious sort of natural sublimity you realize your own sort of littleness in comparison with them. In a way you feel overpowered, at the same time you realize that on another level they are not greater than you. And that gives one a sort of sense of exhilaration. It moves one. [240]
It seems that the spectacle of something sublime, something lofty, something great, something overpowering has the capacity to move one in this sort of way.

So here one could say it's the sublimity of the Dharma which moves Subhuti. He has a new glimpse of how great the Dharma is, and this enables him to see things in a new way. He realizes how important it is to see things in this new way. But then he goes on to say, "It is not difficult for me to accept and believe this discourse on Dharma when it is being taught. But those beings who will be in the future period, in the last time... well again, it's the same question as before, exactly the same, but again with a difference in view of what has just preceded it. Do you see what I mean? There's a subtle difference in this topic that is now raised about the beings of the last five hundred years. A slight difference of emphasis, just because of that has immediately preceded it. "It is not difficult for me to accept and believe this discourse on Dharma," - I have been so greatly moved by it, but what about these beings in the last five hundred year period, will they be moved in this way? Will they be able to see the Dharma and be able to appreciate the Dharma in the way that I am seeing it and appreciating it?

He doesn't actually put it in the form of a question as he did before. He takes it as read, as it were, he takes it that some at least of them will be able to take up this discourse, but only because there is no perception of a self arisen in them. (pause)

There's another aspect of this whole question of being emotionally moved and that is in modern times it's very easy for us to get hold of something new. That tends to very often blunt our appreciation of things, we can change over so often, so freely, so easily, - we don't give ourselves a chance to get into anything or to expose ourselves to anything sufficiently, to enable us to be moved by it. It's the same with people, and that's the whole point of what I was saying earlier, some moments ago, on this question of fidelity. (long pause)

turns.speaker: Jinavamsa
paragraphs: Could you explain the difference between beauty and sublimity. I don't quite understand that.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. That's quite difficult. Beauty is generally considered to be that which charms and attracts and appeals, and which does not arouse any feeling of terror or awe; but the sublime is that which impresses one as more powerful, as overwhelming, as impressive, as grand, even as terrible. For instance, if there was a storm with black clouds and thunder and lightning you could say that was a sublime spectacle, - you wouldn't say that it was beautiful. [241] You certainly wouldn't say that it was a pretty thunderstorm. Do you see what I mean?

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: The Sanskrit word or Pali word 'subha', does that have exclusively the meaning of beauty, or does it also...?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No that is much more beauty... pure beauty. No, it doesn't connote anything like the sublime. I don't know that Sanskrit has exactly any word for sublime. There's something like it in the various ragas connected with music. There is the (vebhara) which is the terrible, that is a bit like the sublime. For instance among artists, Michelangelo is said to be celebrated for his sublimity. His figures are sublime more often than they are beautiful. I mean, even where one would think that perhaps beauty was more appropriate he's introduced sublimity.

Perhaps one could think in terms of the Tibetan Book of the Dead. There's the peaceful deities as beautiful and the wrathful ones as sublime. That might make the whole thing, the distinction more accessible. (pause) And also one could say that it is one of the signs that one has, say, taken this teaching or understood this teaching not merely intellectually, that it does move one emotionally - even that one sheds tears in the way that Subhuti did. I think in the Songs of Milarepa, or the life of Milarepa, there's quite a lot of tear-shedding, isn't there? In Tibetan Buddhism generally, perhaps, the Tibetans are relatively free with their tears.

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: In the Parinirvana it's said that when the Buddha passes away, only the monks were, not the arahants, were crying...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Then?

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: Subhuti was crying in this scripture (unclear).

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So? But it's a different thing, it's a different situation, isn't it?

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: Yes, it is.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Because what is happening at the time of the Parinirvana is simply that the Buddha's physical body is beginning to be removed, beginning to disappear. The arahants presumably did not feel any separation from the Buddha thereby. Only those who were relatively unenlightened felt any separation, any loss. I mean, the general sort of Hinayana or Theravada view of the arahant is that the arahant is sort of impassive, incapable of emotion. That doesn't seem to be the Mahayana view though, judging by this passage. In any case he's moved by something beyond arahantship, yeah? That's why he's moved. There's a revelation of something even more sublime than the Nirvana that he so far, has presumably attained. It's as though he stood on a sort of peak and he thought that was [242] the highest peak of all, but then the mist clears and he looks up and there's another peak still towering above him, of course, so he's moved by the sublimity of the spectacle. You may also remember if you've read it that towards the end of the Astasahasrika - the Perfection of Wisdom in 1,000 Lines - there is the story of the Bodhisattva Sadaprarudita. Do you remember that? Has anyone read

it? Oh dear! Sada is 'always', prarudita means weeping, greatly weeping. It's the ever-weeping Bodhisattva. And what's he weeping for? It's because he's moved by the Dharma. So Subhuti is only an arahant so he just sheds a few tears but his Sadaprarudita is a Bodhisattva so he goes on weeping all the time. Anyway, let's go on to 14d.

"14d. The Lord said: So it is, Subhuti. Most wonderfully blest will be those beings who, on hearing this Sutra, will not tremble, nor be frightened, or terrified. And why? The Tathagata has taught this as the highest (param) perfection (paramita). And what the Tathagata teaches as the highest perfection, that also the innumerable (aparimana) Blessed Buddhas do teach. Therefore is it called the 'highest perfection'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So why should one 'tremble' or be 'frightened' or 'terrified' on hearing this sutra?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: The sublimity.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So how does this differ, I mean one isn't simply frightened or terrified by the sublime. So if one is simply frightened or terrified, well, one isn't seeing the sublime as sublime or isn't seeing the Dharma as sublime. I mean what is one seeing it as?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Threatening.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Simply threatening, simply destructive of oneself. It's just as with the wrathful Buddhas, if you yourself are not in a very skillful state of mind you see them merely as wrathful, merely as threatening. But if you are in a more positive frame of mind you see them as awe- inspiring, as wonderful, sublime, heroic, impressive and very inspiring, huh?

There's the story about Turner the painter. Turner often depicted in his paintings sea scenes including thunderstorms, and apparently he used to get himself taken out in a boat, you know, during a thunderstorm and have himself lashed to the mast so that he could make notes on his sketch pad. No doubt there would be danger of being drowned on that sort of occasion, so one could have merely been overwhelmed by fear, but Turner was not overwhelmed with fear. He was just impressed and inspired by the sublimity of the scene. So that is the difference. If you are merely concerned about your own destruction then the sublime appears as the destructive, the threatening, but if you are not concerned about your own destruction, if you can rise above that, then you can experience the sublime as sublime, you can experience it as inspiring. [243]

So if you are over-preoccupied with the safety of your own existence, your own continual existence as you are, then teachings like that of the Diamond Sutra will merely terrify you out of your wits, if you manage to understand them that is. I mean sometimes you are too stupid

to understand them, just as some people are so stupid so that even if there's a thunderstorm at sea they are not afraid because they haven't got enough imagination even to think what might happen, or blind faith in the ship or the ship captain. So it's quite a healthy sign in a way if one is terrified by the teaching of the Sutra because it shows one has understood it to some extent. (pause) But if you are moved by it, as Subhuti was, well, so much the better because you've started to appreciate its greatness and sublimity. If the Dharma appears to you as beautiful, then it means you have just risen to the level of a Buddha. (laughter)

So you could say that to worldly people the Dharma appears terrifying, to Bodhisattvas it appears sublime and awe-inspiring and to Buddhas it appears beautiful, hmm? (pause) You could apply this even on a much lower level to the question of living in communities, especially men's communities. To the ordinary man in the street the idea of living in a men's community is just, well, frightening, terrifying even; maybe to a sort of regular friend or someone who has just become a mitra, who hasn't perhaps even left home, who has still got a job, well, to him maybe the idea of living in a men's community is rather awe-inspiring, but at the same time it's a bit attractive, though he feels a bit afraid too, still. But to someone who is actually living in a men's community and has been there a long time, it's just beautiful, because he realizes from his own experience it's the best possible way to live.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Or even the prospect of coming to Il Convento for three months.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, that too. It can be a little threatening. Yes, Indeed. But once you're here... (unclear) (laughter) Well that's threatening in a different way for different reasons entirely.

There is a bit of a play on words here also that "The Tathagata has taught this as the highest parama," perfection, paramita. "And what the Tathagata teaches as the highest perfection, that also the innumerable (parimana) Blessed Buddhas do teach. Therefore is it called the 'highest perfection'." These are not very scientific etymologies, but they do throw some light on the term 'paramita'. [243]
In principle all Buddhas teach the same thing because they have achieved or realized the same reality, the same truth, the same absolute, the same unconditioned. (pause) Anyway, let's go on to 5c.
"5c. Selfless patience and perfect inner freedom.

"14. Moreover, Subhuti, the Tathagata's perfection of patience is really no perfection. And why? Because, Subhuti, when the king of Kalinga cut my flesh from every limb, at that time I had no perception of a self, of a being, of a soul, or a person. And why? If, Subhuti, at that time I had had a perception of self, I would also have had a perception of ill-will at that time.

And so, if I had had a perception of a being, of a soul, or of a person. With my superknowledge I recall that in the past I have for five hundred births, led the life of a sage devoted to patience. Then also have I had no perception of a self, a being, a soul, or a person.

"Therefore then, Subhuti, the Bodhi-being, the great being, after he has got rid of all perceptions, should raise his thought to the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment. He should produce a thought which is unsupported by forms, sounds, smells, tastes, touchables, or mind-objects, unsupported by dharma, unsupported by no-dharma, unsupported by anything. And why? All supports have actually no support. It is for this reason that the Tathagata teaches: By an unsupported Bodhisattva should a gift be given, not by one who is supported by forms, sounds, smells, tastes, touchables, or mind-objects.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. So a new subject has been introduced, a new paramita, which is that of ksanti paramita, and the Buddha is referring to an incident in a previous life when he says: "the King of Kalinga cut my flesh from every limb, at that time I had no perception of a self, of a being, or a soul, or a person. And why? If, Subhuti, at that time I had had a perception of self, I would also have had a perception of ill-will at that time." So the Buddha is, so to speak, saying that there are two kinds of ksanti, two kinds of patience. One being where you grit your teeth and just endure in a stoical sort of fashion. The other in which you are not in a sense conscious of anything being done to you by anybody. You've gone beyond the subject- object duality. You don't cherish the notion of a self or of a non-self, or of the other person. So that you don't think anybody is doing anything to you. Therefore there's no possibility of anger. The Buddha doesn't say that there's no possibility of suffering, but there is no possibility of anger as a result of that suffering.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: So in the first case you're biting back your anger. [245]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. you're not manifesting it, you're not acting in accordance with it, not acting upon it. (pause) It seems a very sort of lofty level indeed, doesn't it? One can imagine perhaps by some stretch of imagination the Bodhisattva practising dana in this sort of way, but patience, to that extent? It's quite difficult to conceive.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: When you say in a sense, you're not conscious of anything being done to you by someone, I'm not quite sure what I want to ask but....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You see somebody doing something but your mental attitude is not that "he is doing this to me!" Again this is quite difficult for us to conceive, isn't it? Especially under those sort of circumstances. It's difficult to even get a glimpse of it.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: So it would just be a number of experiences, as it were, taking place and you couldn't identify with any particular...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, perhaps, one could look at it in a slightly different way - a sort of analogy, rather unsatisfactory but perhaps a bit helpful, in the case of the mother and child. I mean the child might get angry with the mother, might hit the mother, but the mother doesn't really, well, there might be a bit of pain, but the mother doesn't think "Oh, he is hitting me" and become angry, very often. Do you see what I mean? Yes, the mother notices or knows that the child is hitting her, but at the same time there is a feeling of sort of oneness with the child. It's her own child, her own flesh and blood so to speak, so she doesn't feel that, well, "he is hitting me" and consequently become angry, at least that is conceivable I think.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Sometimes, the opposite seems to occur. It's almost like the opposite, before a child is born when it's actually in the womb, the mother can perhaps get a lot of joy from feeling the baby kicking.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. Well, in a way she knows everything's going alright.
