But sometimes one can feel like this when one can see very clearly why somebody is doing something, maybe someone sawing the flesh from your bones is somewhat extreme, but sometimes someone might be behaving quite unreasonably and causing you a lot of trouble and inconvenience, but you can see why it's all happening.

So this knowledge, this fact that you can see why it's all [246] happening, and why he's in that state prevents you from really reacting. Do you see what I mean? You don't think "Well, why is he doing this to me?" You can see the reason for it all, at least psychologically. (pause)
So this is ksanti as a paramita, as distinct from the mundane virtue of that name, where you just turn the other cheek even though you'd like to give the other person a sock on the jaw.

And then the Buddha goes on to say of the Bodhisattva that he should produce an unsupported thought, 'all supports have actually no support'. That can be better translated, 'that which is supported has no support: one of the basic teachings of the Perfection of Wisdom. If you think about it, it's quite true, it's even quite obvious rationally. If something is supported, if it depends upon being supported by something else, then it isn't really supported because it is supported by something outside itself, and that support can be at any time taken away. So if that is your position, you certainly aren't supported. So that which is supported is unsupported. Real support is absolute support; relative support is only relative support.
Relative support is your absolute support, so that which is supported, i.e. is relatively supported, is unsupported in absolute terms.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: So an unsupported Bodhisattva is actually supported in an absolute sense.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes. If one uses that terminology at all, yes. That's the only way to be supported, to be unsupported. (pause) So, "He should produce a thought which is unsupported by forms, sounds, smells, tastes, touchables, or mind-objects etc", in other words, which is not derived by his ordinary sense experience, which is not even derived from his mental experience, not derived from his dualistic thinking, which is not derived from anything. One can only say which arises from deep within him, from the deepest possible level within him, quite spontaneously. (long pause) Alright let's go on to Sd.
"5d. The existence and non-existence of beings

"14f. And further, Subhuti, it is for the weal of all beings that a Bodhisattva should give gifts in this manner. And why? This perception of a being, Subhuti, that is just a non-perception. Those all-beings of whom the Tathagata has spoken, they are indeed no-beings. And why? Because the Tathagata speaks in accordance with reality, speaks the truth, speaks of what is, not otherwise. A Tathagata does not speak falsely. [247]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. This in a way echoes a passage or passages in the Pali scriptures.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Which ones were you thinking of?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I was just trying to think of the precise wording. There's one that somewhat slips my memory, but there's another one.. ah... here the Buddha says words to the effect that he uses ordinary language. He speaks, that is to say, in terms of I and you, but he is not misled by it. Of course again there is that passage in the Lankavatara Sutra where it is said from the night of his enlightenment to the night of his parinirvana the Buddha had not uttered a single word, because no word that could he uttered could do justice to Reality. So in reality the Buddha had not said anything, - that is another way of looking at it. But in one way or another the point is that the Buddha does speak from a higher standpoint, he does give expression to his inner state of enlightenment. He does speak out of his experience of reality. So everything that he says, if one takes the real meaning of it, is in accordance with Reality. He speaks the truth; speaks of what is, not otherwise; does not speak falsely. But nonetheless there is a sort of corollary or rider in the section immediately following, so just read 5e.
"5e. Truth and Falsehood
"14g. But nevertheless, Subhuti, with regard to that dharma which the Tathagata has fully known and demonstrated, on account of that there is neither truth nor fraud."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: In other words one must apply one's dualistic notions of truth to the fact that the Buddha speaks the truth. He doesn't speak the truth as opposed to falsehood, he speaks in terms of ultimate reality which is beyond truth and falsehood in the dualistic sense. That doesn't mean that the Buddha might tell a lie, because truth and falsehoods are a duality, but truth in the case of the Buddha here must be understood in a deeper non-dualistic sense. In other words, there isn't, on a metaphysical level, a distinction between truth and untruth to which the Buddha conforms (pause). Anyway, go on with the remainder of that section.

"In darkness a man could not see anything. Just so should be viewed a Bodhisattva who has fallen among things, and who, fallen among things, renounces a gift. A man with eyes would, when the night becomes light and the sun has arisen see manifold forms. Just so should be viewed a Bodhisattva who has not fallen among things, and who, without having fallen among things, renounces a gift. [248]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Here the Buddha is contrasting two ways of practising dana. One co-joined with Prajnaparamita, the other not co-joined. The first is blind, the second alone truly seeing. So a Bodhisattva who gives something, thinking that there is a real thing that he gives, that he himself who gives is a real thing, and the person to whom he gives is a real thing, he is "fallen among things", he is in darkness, he is blind, he is spiritually blind, blind to the Perfection of Wisdom. But not so is one who does not act in that way. The point that has already been made has been repeated.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It's quite an interesting expression, isn't it, 'fallen among things'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. It's almost like the Biblical 'fallen among thieves'.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I was thinking of the Gnostics... fallen into the world.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes. We haven't got the Sanskrit text, otherwise we could see how closely that reproduced the original. I imagine it does. It sounds like a Sanskrit idiom, "fallen among things". Fallen is ?pattita.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Is there any significance in the term, 'renouncing a gift'? Up to date, it's been giving a gift that's been used.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. You are giving up something, he gives away something, renounces it. I don't know whether there is any significance, or whether it's just a variation for the sake of a variation.
To renounce something means you give it for good, you, give up any claim that you have on it.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It's connected with ksanti.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: In what way?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Renunciation is in a sense an aspect of ksanti?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Renouncing one's own body, one's atma-bhava. Also the comparison is "A man with eyes would, when the night be comes light and the sun has arisen, see manifold forms. Just so should be viewed a Bodhisattva." So here the language is the language of vision and sight with regard to the Perfection of Wisdom. (pause) Go on to 14h, that contains a new point which we could go into, before we close. [249]
"5f. The Merit acquired, its presuppositions and results.

"14h. Furthermore, Subhuti, those sons and daughters of good family who will take up this discourse on Dharma, will bear it in mind, recite, study, and illuminate it in full detail for others, they have been known, Subhuti, by the Tathagata with his Buddha-cognition, they have been seen, Subhuti, by the Tathagata with his Buddha-eye, they have been fully known by the Tathagata. All these beings, Subhuti, will beget and acquire an immeasurable and incalculable heap of merit."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So what's the new theme here. I think it's a new theme. I don't think we've come across this.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Recitation study and illumination. I think before he's only talked about - just demonstrating it to others, just repeating it. Now there seems to be an emphasis on actually remembering it and looking at it quite closely, understanding it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's true, but I wasn't thinking of that. But that is a variation on a theme we've had before, or a development of it.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: Well, the fact that those who recite then study it will be seen by the Tathagata.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. So what does that mean? What does it mean be seen the Buddha - by his Buddha- eye? Was one not seen before? Was Subhuti not seen before? Was nobody else seen by the Buddha? Even with his Buddha eye, his Buddha-cognition? Why only those who take up this discourse on the Dharma? What does that signify?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: This did actually come up before, didn't it?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Oh, has it?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Yes. The idea then was that...Conze translated it as 'assisted' by the Buddha, and we talked about then being in contact with the Buddha.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That's true. Yes. But being seen by the Buddha.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Recognized?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Recognized, yes, but going into it a little more deeply, what does it sort of imply? That you are seen by the Buddha? Because [250] presumably you have been seen by the Buddha before? Let me give you an analogy. Supposing there were two people: supposing one of them is looking at the other. Let's say A and B. A is looking at B, but B is not looking at A, so what happens when B does look at A to some extent in the same way as A was looking at B?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Communication takes place.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. So isn't it a bit like that? Whereas before the Buddha was looking at Subhuti, but Subhuti was not fully looking at the Buddha. But when Subhuti looks at the Buddha, then the Buddha can look at Subhuti, yes? (Subhuti: Yes.) So the Buddha looking at you, is an aspect of your looking at the Buddha. Do you see what I mean? And therefore, you see the Buddha when you see the Dharma, presumably. So the Buddha sees you when your Dharma-eye opens. I mean, in a sense, he can only see you with his Buddha-eye because he is a Buddha. He can't really close his Buddha-eye; it's always there - but when the Dharma eye opens in you then you really look at the Buddha, and when you look at the Buddha, the Buddha looks at you, hmm?

So there is a much more direct communication between you than when the Buddha was simply looking at you, and you were at best looking at sorne thought or idea or even image of the Buddha, but not at the Buddha himself. Now you are looking at the Buddha, you are really looking at the Buddha, so now he can really look at you. Hmm? So that means in a way, there is a duality but there is also the beginning of a non-duality.
So one could say that this is another aspect of going for refuge. Do you see what I mean? I mean one has spoken in terms of going for refuge; one has spoken in terms of entering the

turns.speaker: Stream
paragraphs: one has spoken in terms of the Dharma eye opening, one has spoken in terms of going forth, - these are all different aspects of.... (End of Tape 12) [251]

S:.. all different aspects of one and the same basic spiritual experience. Could one not also speak in terms of other aspect still - being seen by the Buddha? There is, of course, another one I haven't mentioned or didn't mention before, though I've given a lecture on it in the past, which is the paravritti, according to the Lankavatara, the turning about. This is an aspect of the same thing.

So it seems as though one is discovering more and more different aspects of the Going for Refuge. So another here is that you become seen by the Buddha with his Buddha-eye, which is, in a way, a very strange experience. The Buddha is seeing you all the time, is looking at you all the time, and you are looking at the Buddha. It's just like in the case of those eyes, you know, painted on the sides of the harmikas, of those stupas in Nepal. If you look at them you find they are looking at you. (laughter) But why do I say a sort of duality, but at the same time, a sort of non-duality?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Because there's you and the Buddha, but because of communication there's a link between you...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. You're much closer. The division between you is less. You are not one with the Buddha, because after all, he is looking at you and you are looking at him, but you are looking at each other. In a way, he sees himself reflected in you, because if you look another person in the eyes, you can see your image reflected in the pupils of their eyes, don't you, so you are in them and they are in you.

So when the Buddha looks at you he sees himself reflected in you, and when you look at the Buddha you see yourself reflected in the Buddha. So there's duality, but at the same time there's non-duality. You are moving in the direction of non-duality. Not that non-duality excludes duality- one mustn't forget that.

So here's another aspect of Going for Refuge one could say. That one becomes seen by the Buddha with his Buddha-eye. It does seem that the Going for Refuge is including more and more of Buddhism, doesn't it? I was thinking, this is quite incidental and by the way, let me say it so that it can be jotted down and then someday something may be done about it. It wouldn't be a bad idea to have a series of talks, a symposium, maybe spread over two days, on the different aspects of Going for Refuge. We've now got at least seven of them - say with one person speaking on Going for Refuge itself, another speaking of the opening of the Dharma-eye, another speaking of Going Forth, and so on. That would be quite a good
symposium, wouldn't it? (mumbled agreement) You could have different order members speaking on each aspect. (long pause) Anyway let's do one more little section. [252]

"15a. And if, Subhuti, a woman or man should renounce in the morning all their belongings as many times as there are grains of sand in the river Ganges, and if they should do likewise at noon and in the evening, and if in this way they should renounce all their belongings for many hundreds of thousands of millions of milliards of aeons; and someone else, on hearing this discourse on Dharma, would not reject it; then the latter would on the strength of that beget a greater heap of merit, immeasurable and incalculable. What then should we say of him who, after writing it, would learn it, bear it in mind, recite, study and illuminate it in full detail for others?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So, "on hearing this discourse would not reject it", well, the demands are becoming less and less and the merit becoming more and more. So why is the merit of not rejecting this Dharma so great?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Because of the possibilities it opens up for you.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Because of the possibilities - well, it's not rejecting. It's as though the temptation to reject it is so great, because it is so threatening. So if you manage to resist that temptation, well, already that is a great achievement; you've done a great thing simply not to reject the teaching. I mean, here, not to reject it is tantamount to accepting it one might say. If you get as far as not rejecting it, well, you're very very close indeed to accepting it to say the least. Is there any further point arising out of this, or anything else we have done this morning? We seem to have got on quite well without Dr Conze's help, (laughter) notwithstanding.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: I've got a bit confused about the basic usefulness of the Sutra. It's like at times we've said it doesn't seem to be relevant to the West, especially to people who don't have highly developed intellects, but at other times we've said it would be good for people to learn it off by heart and meditate on this sort of Perfection of Wisdom, and I can't quite reconcile the two.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, I think its very utility is in its inutility. It is relevant because it is so irrelevant. (laughter) That is to say I think one needs to occupy oneself sometimes with something that has got no bearing whatever on one's immediate concerns, and no doubt the Perfection of Wisdom fulfils that admirably. I think we shouldn't be over... well, on a certain level we have to, but again on another level we should not be over-concerned with what is relevant to me, interesting to me, applicable to my situation, in the West, living in Bethnal Green. Do you see what I mean?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: I'm just trying to work out if it's relevant to my spiritual [253] development here and now.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think there needs to be some element which is not relevant to one's spiritual development at all.. because only then will one sort of transcend conditions. It must be gloriously irrelevant, and then it will be sort of most relevant of all, you know, in a deeper or higher sense. I think actually people do enjoy things like this, partly for this reason; their sheer irrelevance. There's not a word about co-ops, not a word about centres, not a word about communities (laughter), not a word about practising meditation (laughter). You can just have a complete rest from these things. Sometimes you'd like to have a complete rest from some of these things however good they may be. On the other hand, you may have a complete rest on, one might say, to some extent a very much higher level. You are still concerned with the Dharma, but you're not concerned with any of the things that you usually think of as constituting the Dharma. So you don't get a chance to sort of settle down in them, to rest in them. This is quite a refreshing change. Here's something that's completely irrelevant - a bit like nonsense poetry, a bit like Lewis Carroll or Edward Lear with characters taken to a very much higher plane. I won't go as far as Christmas Humphreys and say that Alice in Wonderland is a Zen scripture or anything like that, but there's a sort of analogy if you see what I mean?

It's alright to expect Buddhism to appeal to one where one is, here and now, but where is here and when is now? Is one only a conditioned being? (long pause) In some ways it's quite refreshing to have something which in a way you understand but which in another way you don't understand at all. Perhaps an exclusive diet of Perfection of Wisdom wouldn't do one any good, but a touch of it is I think, very useful. It's just like salt, you can't, you know, eat a meal of salt (laughter) but without salt the meal is a bit tasteless. You could almost say that one needs a little grain, or a few grains, of Perfection of Wisdom in one's spiritual diet all the time. It gives it a little flavour, the flavour of irrelevancy, otherwise it all becomes too relevant, or one takes it all too seriously, and one thinks too much in terms of oneself as one at present is.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Is this flavour of irrelevancy rather like the 'taste of freedom'?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, quite. Perhaps I'll give a talk on that one day, on the flavour of irrelevancy or perhaps somebody else will. (pause) [254] After all, those monks for all those hundreds of years in monasteries in Japan and Lamaseries in Tibet who were reciting the Diamond Sutra, they must have got something out of it.. or perhaps they didn't get anything out of it. Maybe that was the point! (laughter) Perhaps they just recited it, perhaps they liked reciting it, liked the sound of it, even though they didn't understand it. I was just thinking that maybe all of those women who in the early days of the Friends declared that the Diamond Sutra was their

favourite scripture might have had lurking at the back of their minds that famous saying that "diamonds are a girl's best friend". (laughter) Diamonds in that sense are certainly not irrelevant. Or to speak of that other saying, "diamonds are forever". I suppose there is the point that if one repeats something over and over again, the meaning of which one understands, but the purport of which one does not understand, let us say. If one repeats those words over and over again a sufficient number of times, long enough, then something of the purport in addition to the verbal meaning of the words will begin to dawn on one. But it may sometimes be difficult to explain the actual connection between that purport and what I've called the meaning.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: What does the word purport mean? (laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, I'm using the word 'purport' to avoid using the word 'meaning' again which would be ambiguous. The sort of general drift of the statement, let us say, the direction in which it is pointing above and beyond whatever one can get out of the meaning of the words or the meaning of the sentence, or the meaning of the statement as a statement. It isn't difficult sometimes to get at the meaning but to get at the purport is difficult. I think one can only get at the purport by constantly bearing in mind the meaning; well, one of the ways of bearing in mind the meaning is by constantly reciting the words, as one does in some of the visualization practices, with certain short verses which one repeats or recites over and over again. (pause)

Anyway, I think we have left Dr Conze trotting along behind today. Perhaps we should leave it there. We've got two more sessions so we are getting on quite well. As far as I can see we will certainly get through the text, so at least we'll have an overview of it.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: The difficulty with this, the second part, is not so much with the individual passages but with relating them, is that the point? We don't seem to have had that much difficulty in.. [255]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It is somewhat more condensed that the first part. But the sequence of the sections, even the connection of the argument is not clear, perhaps it's not meant to be. What I think is that if the 'argument', inverted commas, is inconsequential, why should one expect consecutiveness in the arrangement of the paragraphs? (agreement)

turns.speaker: One might sort of attempt an image
paragraphs: one usually thinks of a book, say, as a linear thing, that the pages could be laid side by side, or in one long strip or scroll, and that is to say the page connects linearly with the next one. But supposing one was to imagine an arrangement of pages like the letters on a golf ball in a typewriter, in such a way that every page could be read as continuous with the pages on each side. Do you see what I mean? That would mean,

perhaps, really the Vajracchedika is to be thought of in that sort of way, and it only seems confusing because convention compels us to put one page or one section after another, and say, only one other, - do you see what I mean? Whereas they should be arranged, in a way, in relation to each other like the letters on a typewriter golf ball, so you could read them in a number of ways rather like dice - the different faces of a dice.

We mustn't forget there are conventions, linguistic and typographic conventions. We mustn't expect Reality to conform to those conventions. You could even work out how many sections there were, whether one could a multifaceted figure (few words unclear) one could (engrave?) one section of each of these facets (unclear). Then it could be read off either as leading to the facet there or the facet there. Do you see what I mean? That might give one a more comprehensive view of the Perfection of Wisdom. I mean Conze seems to have assumed that there must be one correct linear arrangement of the sections of the text. Is that necessarily so in the case of a text like this?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: My impression is that the tempo has been turned up. With the first one there is some thread of linear development you could hang on to...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes. There's even a more constructive derangement... with fewer compromises than before... Well, this is perhaps why (Han Shan?) regarded section one as eliminating gross doubts and section two as eliminating subtle doubts. Perhaps he sensed a certain increase in tempo as you've called it. Because were the sages and editors of old so obtuse that they couldn't see something that seems pretty obvious to us, you know, that part two is in a mess; is it in a mess? Or is it we who are in a mess? even Dr Conze who is in a mess? (pause) You know, if I find anybody sort of surreptitiously [256] experimenting with multi-faceted figures, I shall know what they are up to. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: If you wanted to learn the Diamond Sutra off by heart, is this a good one to go from?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I think this would be the best. It is the closest to the original Sanskrit in idiomatic English... I think this probably would be the best translation to learn off by heart. It is clear, not that that helps very much. (unclear)... Alright, let's leave it there until tomorrow.
(Day 6)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We got as far as 15, I think. Would someone like to read 15b right down to 15c.
"15b. Moreover, Subhuti, (1) unthinkable and (2) incomparable is this discourse on Dharma. (3) The Tathagata has taught it for the weal of beings who have set out in the best, in the most

excellent vehicle. Those who will take up this discourse on Dharma, bear it in mind, recite, study and illuminate it in full detail for others, the Tathagata has known them with his Buddha-cognition, the Tathagata has seen them with his Buddha-eye, the Tathagata has fully known them. All these beings, Subhuti, will be blest with an immeasurable heap of merit, they will be blest with a heap of merit unthinkable, incomparable, measureless and illimitable. All these beings, Subhuti, will carry along an equal share of enlightenment. And why? (4) Because it is not possible, Subhuti, that this discourse on Dharma could be heard by beings of inferior resolve, nor by such as have a self in view, a being, a soul, or a person. Nor can beings who have not taken the pledge of Bodhi-beings either hear this discourse on Dharma, or take it up, bear it in mind, recite or study it. That cannot be.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So does any new idea emerge from this section?

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: (unclear)... have to be a Bodhi-being to hear this discourse.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No. That isn't what the Buddha says. The Buddha says you hear the discourse you are a Bodhi-being - at least I think that is the implication.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: The last sentence almost seem to be saying that if you haven't taken the Bodhisattva vow you won't hear the discourse.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. So if you do hear it, then it means you have to infer [257] that you must have taken the Bodhisattva vow. So what is the idea implied here?

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: Presumably hearing this discourse on Dharma doesn't just mean hearing the words, you have to understand the meaning of it.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But, as a more general sort of principle. It actually says "Because it is not possible, Subhuti, that this discourse on Dharma could be heard by beings of inferior resolve". I mean if one takes it literally, well, if you haven't some sort of spiritual resolve, you don't even hear the discourse. It doesn't say actually anything about understanding it - it just actually does speak in terms of hearing it.

What seems to be the general principle underlying these lines? (long pause) What does in fact one find actually happening very often? (long pause) Well, the general principle that seems to be underlying all this, what in fact one does find happening is that you are drawn to a teaching when you are ready for it. It is as though the teaching is moving toward you at the same time that you are moving towards the teaching. You may not know it. One often does find that you encounter a situation where you read a book just when you happen to need it
and it seems an extraordinary coincidence sometimes, as though there was some sort of law at work, some sort of magnetic attraction on the spiritual plane. (long pause)

This verse, this section, suggests that if you can come into contact with the 'Perfection of Wisdom' teaching, that means that you've got some affinity for it, otherwise you could not even have heard it, not even have listened to it or read it. (long pause)

turns.speaker: Gerry Corr
paragraphs: What does it mean by "equal share of enlightenment"?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: "All these beings, Subhuti" that is to say, all those that the Buddha has known with his Buddha-cognition because they have taken up this discourse, "all these beings, Subhuti, would carry along an equal share of enlightenment". Presumably they would all be equally advanced on the spiritual path, not that enlightenment would be literally divided among them, I imagine. The expression does seem rather odd, not to say clumsy, at least in English. They all partake equally in enlightenment, I suppose one could also say. (silence)

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Talking about a magnetic attraction on the spiritual plane between you and the teaching. I can get a vague sort of feeling for that as a poetic image. Sometimes I sort of feel as if it might be happening, but do you have any more sort of models for what actually happens? Or how that [258] works? What goes on?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, clearly it isn't a case of sort of linear causality. It's as though there's a web of interrelationships, you know - I won't say transcending - but at least spanning both time and space. They constitute a sort of web, and you touch one part of the web and another corresponding part is affected. Do you see what I mean?
There is a response from another corresponding part. (pause)

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: This equal share of enlightenment: why should they all carry along an equal share of enlightenment?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You notice it says, "and why? Because it is not possible, Subhuti, that this discourse on Dharma could be heard by beings of inferior resolve", which seems rather inconsequential. It's as though these beings have all much the same spiritual status. They have all done the same things. They've all taken up the discourse, borne it in mind, studied it, illuminated it, "in full detail for others." They've all been seen by the Tathagata with his 'Buddha-cognition', 'his Buddha-eye'. The Tathagata fully knows them, they are all blessed "with an immeasurable heap of merit, unthinkable, incomparable, measureless and illimitable" and it does seem that they are all much on the same spiritual plane, on the same level, that they carry along an
equal share of enlightenment, they have a lot in common. It seems to me no more than that, though again, it is rather an odd way of putting the matter.

And the reason of course, which is given is no reason at all - not logically speaking. Perhaps it is an encouraging thought that if one has encountered this Perfection of Wisdom teaching well, one could not even have encountered it if there hadn't been at least some glimmerings of spiritual life in one, hm?, to take it no further than that. (long silence)

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: I can understand what you're saying about it being a principle, but does he not go a bit far when he says that those who have not taken the pledge of Bodhi-beings cannot hear this discourse on Dharma?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. He's going quite far. So if one has full faith in the Sutra one has to conclude that the mere fact that one is able to hear it in this life means that he must have taken the Bodhisattva Vow in a previous life and that on account of that you've been brought in contact with this teaching again in this life. (comment unclear) (loud laughter) [259] Otherwise, there's so many thousands, so many millions of people in the world, hundreds of millions, so many people, even in Buddhist countries, people in even Mahayana Buddhist countries; there are only a few thousand copies of this Sutra in circulation in one form or another. How is it that you should come in contact with it, huh? What mysterious affinity have you for the Sutra that has brought you actually in contact with it? And be reading it and be studying it, huh?, etc. etc.

But does one not find that some people do take to things immediately? Very readily? They plunge right in as though in their natural element like the proverbial duck taking to the water. Doesn't one find that? Other people, you just have to work on them so much and so hard before they show even a glimmering of interest, but to others, it's as though it's already quite familiar to them. One can't help wondering why. One does start wondering - well, could they not have had some contact with this kind of thing, this kind of teaching, this kind of ideal in a previous life? Maybe they are coming back, as it were, to something they have known before, taking up the threads again. Sometimes one is tempted to believe this, or rather I should say sometimes one is tempted not. (pause) Why should certain people be reading and studying an extraordinary text like this, which apparently doesn't make any sense at all. (laughter) (unclear)... baffling and bewildering. I noticed when looking, I couldn't help looking at the beginning of the study at the front, and I see that it was first published in 1958, and the second edition was 1975, which means that there was an interval of seventeen years. So it means that, well, there wasn't a very brisk demand, they probably didn't print more than 2000 copies, at the most 3000, so that they were only selling at the rate of about 100 a year. Perhaps it is not surprising, eh? But I noticed also that though there was a second edition in 1975, there's been a second impression in 1980. 1 don't know if that's due to the influence of

the FWBO, but there certainly are quite a few copies around in the Movement. Perhaps a few more Bodhisattvas have been reborn in the interval, clamouring for their copies of the... (loud laughter) I can remember my own experience, reading this Sutra, when I was sixteen, when I immediately felt that this was it. I can't say that I felt familiar with it in a way. I can't say that I had the feeling that, well, I've studied this before, but I certainly had the strong feeling that the content of the Sutra was quite familiar to me - howsoever one might explain that. It certainly didn't seem anything strange or outlandish, even though as far as I can remember the translation wasn't a very adequate one, from the Chinese anyway? [260]

So that means one does have a sort of affinity with this teaching or that. I mean, whether that affinity is to be explained in terms of previous contact with that particular material or whether it is simply that there is a certain level within oneself, irrespective of the whole birth and rebirth process, which has an affinity with the text itself. It can't be just a matter of psychology or temperament because teachings like this go far beyond that.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Is there any specific reason why you read it at sixteen?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I just wanted to read as my Buddhist texts as I could. I was always on the lookout for works on Buddhism, translations of Buddhist texts. I happened to come across this, I seem to remember picking it up second-hand - it might have been in Foyle's which at that time was only a second-hand bookshop, it was very likely there. And then I must have read Max Muller's translation of the Sanskrit and also there a translation from the Chinese by Arnold Price who was a friend of mine in those days. (long pause)

So perhaps it is a sobering thought that the mere fact that one has come into contact with the Dharma at all, the mere fact that one has come into contact with teachings like those of the Perfection of Wisdom, means not perhaps that one has reached a high stage of spiritual development but at least one has a certain spiritual capacity, that at least the ground has been prepared to some extent, that one has a certain spiritual potential, not just a potential in a abstract sense but even a sort of spiritual preparedness and readiness that should not be wasted and should not be thrown away. I mean having come so far it's a pity that one shouldn't complete the journey. You've actually got as far as hearing the Diamond Sutra. That's a very long way actually. Perhaps one shouldn't dwell too much on the fact that one is a beginner and one has all sorts of weaknesses and imperfections and drawbacks, and that one backslides very often. Perhaps one should reflect, you know, that, well, one in a sense has not done too badly, at least as far as contacts are concerned, at least as far as opportunities are concerned and facilities are concerned. So you can't be in such a bad way after all. Perhaps you have got some .. of merit lurking in the background somewhere. You might even be a fallen incarnate lama reborn in the West. (laughter) You might even be a backsliding Bodhisattva or a runaway Arahant! (laughter) But one doesn't know, so why not take the

more optimistic view? (laughter) The present birth may simply be an episode in an otherwise quite distinguished spiritual… (laughter) [261] It might be the spiritual equivalent of just being sent to stand in the corner for being a bad boy.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Now you're making things worse, Bhante! (laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It might be the equivalent of a hundred lines, you know, go and set up 200 FWBO Centres in a lifetime.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: There is a tradition that this present age or world system is quite a dodgy one for a Bodhisattva to encounter, that he's advised to stay clear of it. Do you know where does this come from?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't remember. Perhaps it's only novice Bodhisattvas who are advised to stay clear of this particular world system where the influence of Mara is dominant, where relationships are very strong. But these lines suggest a quite different spiritual perspective, because if one thinks of the corresponding or rather the not-so-corresponding Christian perspective what does one find? What are one's spiritual antecedents?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: You're a miserable sinner!

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, there's Adam and the sin of Adam, the Fall. Behind you is the fall in which you participate, in which you are involved. No doubt we are baptismally regenerated only for a very short period indeed. You start sinning again inevitably. Well, it's not that Buddhism asserts that you do definitely have a wonderful spiritual heritage behind you, but at least there is the possibility of that. It can even be inferred by the fact that you've come into contact with the Dharma in this life, and in this particular Sutra the Buddha even goes so far as to say, if you come into contact with the Sutra, if you hear this discourse, well, then it must have been that you've taken the Bodhisattva vow, otherwise you could never have come into contact with such a profound and abstruse teaching as this. So there's a great difference of perspective. It's much more encouraging, though of course at the same time it gives one much more to live up to. There's no cause for complacency. One has got no reason at all for being dull or apathetic or in an unskillful mental state or bad way of any kind.

It reminds me, this is something I may have quoted before, that someone who was in Kalimpong, and who knew a lot of the people I knew, including Dhardo Rimpoche in the early days. He wrote of him in an article - I think it was published in a German magazine - words to [262] this effect - he believed - he was speaking of Dhardo Rimpoche - believed that he was an incarnate Bodhisattva and acted accordingly. (laughter) So, one might say, if you
really believed the words of the Buddha in this Sutra that you could not have come into contact with the Sutra if had not already taken the Bodhisattva vow.

If you believe that, then you must just believe it and act accordingly (laughter). That makes everything very simple. You are told who you are so you know exactly what you have to do. You remember this passage in the Saddharmapundarika where the Bodhisattva Never- Disparaging goes about telling people that they are all due for Buddhahood. Well, what sort of response does he meet with? Not a very favourable one. It's almost as though people don't want to contemplate their glorious spiritual destiny. Reminds me of a passage from a Chinese Opera translated by Lin Yu Tang. There's a sort of long song, a piece of declamation, put into the mouth of a young girl who is running away from her nunnery and she, among other rather strong things, she says she not only doesn't want to be a nun she doesn't want to be a Buddha, and she's not going to become Buddha, and she's going to run down the hill away from the nunnery and find herself a handsome young lover, and she's not going to become a Buddha, she doesn't want to become a Buddha.

So some people seem to contemplate the prospect facing a Buddha or even of being a Bodhisattva, or being told that they have in fact taken the Bodhisattva Vow, with a certain amount of dismay. (soft laughter)

Certainly we should perhaps get more accustomed to this more positive, more encouraging really, perspective of the Mahayana. I think anyone born in the West, practically, is bound almost to be affected by this tradition of Christian view of man as descended from Adam and participating in Adam's original sin, and having to be purified of that by baptism. But then, of course, being unable to maintain this baptismal purity. You know, the whole idea that you can be free from original sin only for a few short weeks perhaps, as a baby, isn't really very encouraging, is it?

I don't know whether it's ever been discussed - it must have been - how long that state of purity lasts. I mean perhaps it doesn't last any longer than the first time the baby loses his temper or something like that, or clutches his little fists, or gives mother a nasty look - even cries while he's being baptized.

One thing - this is a little bit of a digression - but one thing that has surprised me is how many people brought up as Christians, whether Protestant or Catholic, know very little about actual [263] Christian teaching. It's as though it's a sort of general atmosphere, a sort of gloomy presence. Do you see what I mean? It's like a sort of black cloud almost. Some people no doubt did go through their catechism, but not much of it seems to have stuck - (unclear) - Why do they reject it? Perhaps one deliberately, well, not deliberately, but represses the memory of the whole business. But certainly, as I say, the Buddhist perspective, the
background that Buddhism envisages for beings is much more positive. Anyway, let's go on to 15c. Would someone like to read the whole of that section right up to....
"6. The Bodhisattvas.

"15c. (1) Moreover, Subhuti, the spot of earth where this Sutra will be revealed, that spot of earth will be worthy of worship by the whole world with its Gods, men and Asuras, worthy of being saluted respectfully, worthy of being honoured by circumambulation, - like a shrine will be that spot of earth.

"16a. And yet Subhuti, those sons and daughters of good family, who will take up these very Sutras, and will bear them in mind, recite and study them, they will be humbled, - well humbled they will be. And why? The impure deeds which these beings have done in their former lives, and which are liable to lead them into the states of woe, - in this very life they will, by means of that humiliation, (2) annul those impure deeds of their former lives, and (3) they will reach the enlightenment of a Buddha. - 16b. With my superknowledge, Subhuti, I recall that in the past period, long before Dipankara, the Tathagata, Arhat, fully Enlightened One, during incalculable, quite incalculable aeons, I gave satisfaction by loyal service to 84,000 million milliards of Buddhas, without ever becoming again estranged from them. But the heap of merit, Subhuti, from the satisfaction I gave to those Buddhas and Lords without again becoming estranged from them compared with the heap of merit of those who in the last time, the last epoch, the last five hundred years, at the time of the collapse of the good doctrine, will take up these very Sutras, bear them in mind, recite and study them, and will illuminate them in full detail for others, it does not approach one hundredth part, not one thousandth part, nor a one hundred thousandth part, not a ten millionth part, nor a one hundred millionth part, nor a 100,000 millionth part. It does not bear number, nor fraction, nor counting, nor similarity, nor comparison, nor resemblance. -16c. (4) If moreover, Subhuti, I were to teach the heap of merit of those sons and daughters of good family, and how great a heap of merit they will at that time beget and acquire, beings would become frantic and confused. Since, however, Subhuti, the Tathagata has taught this discourse on Dharma as unthinkable, so just an unthinkable karma-result should be expected from it. [264]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Any new themes here?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: The humbling.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. This is quite important. This is why, it is on account of these few lines, that this Sutra is sometimes recited in connection with, so to speak, purification of sins. You remember the little ceremony which Chi-i refers to in Dhyana for Beginners. If one becomes conscious of any transgression, any breach of the precepts, one of the things one can do is to confess it in

front of an image of the Buddha, burn incense, light candles and recite sutras, recite Mahayana sutras until such time as one feels that one has freed oneself from the ill effects of those particular unskillful actions.

16a "And yet, Subhuti, those sons and daughters of good family, who will take up these very Sutras, and will bear them in mind, recite and study them, they will be humbled, well humbled they will be!" It's as though the Buddha is seeking to redress any imbalance that might have been created as a result of the preceding section. Maybe one has come into contact with these sutras because one is in fact a Bodhisattva, a novice Bodhisattva. Perhaps one has taken the Bodhisattva vow in a previous life, but nonetheless, one is by no means perfect yet. One may also have committed unskillful actions in previous lives. So if that is the case, then when one takes up the study of these sutras, one will be humbled; as the result of being humbled one's impure deeds will be annulled. Do you see the connection? Well, why should there be that sort of connection, do you think?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Humbling is sort of confession, isn't it? It means that you've recognized your position.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: 'Humble' I take it to mean, - I mean I have no grounds for so saying, except that this is what occurs to me as I read the text - "they will be humbled" almost in the sense of being humiliated and made to suffer - not just that they will feel humble on account of their previous unskillful deeds although no doubt this is included, but when they come into contact with the Perfection of Wisdom teachings, as a result of that contact, things will happen: circumstances will so develop that they will be humbled. They will in a sense be made to suffer, and by virtue of that humiliation, by virtue of that suffering, unskillful actions which they have committed, or the effects of unskillful actions they have committed, will be purged away. So why should that happen? What is the sort of raison d'etre of that process? [265]

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: In a way, by that humiliation one leaves these things behind one.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That is true, yeah, but why should all this process be set up when you come into contact with the Perfection of Wisdom teachings?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I can see it in psychological terms, in that the Sutra is a sort of assault on the ego. (S: Yes) But you seem to be suggesting that it is actually objective. Is that what you're suggesting, that circumstances will arise (S: Yes) - I'm not sure...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It would seem like that, as though, well first of all when you come into contact with the Sutra, when you come into contact with the Perfection of Wisdom teaching, you come into contact with something, so to speak, very powerful, let us say. So that must have a tremendous effect on your whole being. There will be an incompatibility between not just the

sutras themselves, but the effect they have upon you, the way that they affect you and the way that you normally are, the way you usually are, however prepared you may be, even though you may have taken the Bodhisattva vow, there's still a tremendous discrepancy between the level represented by the sutras and the level on which you stand. And to the extent that you come into contact with the sutras and to the extent that you are affected by them, there will be a tremendous discrepancy, so to speak, within you - between those parts of you, so to speak, which have been affected by the sutra and the rest of you sort of coming over from the past. So that sort of discrepancy can be even dangerous. I mean it can be almost schizophrenic. Here you are on the one hand in contact with the Perfect... (End of Tape) [266]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: … for one's sanity. So, the situation has to be remedied as soon as possible. So, it's as though, by virtue of the fact you've come into contact with the Perfection of Wisdom teachings, you generate on the mundane level an immense amount of punya. So you need to be brought out of that situation where there is that tension between yourself as affected by the Perfection of Wisdom teachings and yourself as not affected by those teachings. In other words, the rest of you needs to catch up very very quickly, something drastic needs to happen. So what happens? You're humiliated, you're humbled. So how does that come about? Well circumstances are set up, your punya, by virtue of your punya, circumstances are set up which cause you to be humiliated and to suffer, and in this way, very quickly, much that is unskillful in you is purged away, the tension is lessened, more of you so to speak is brought into line with the Perfection of Wisdom teachings and with that part of yourself, so to speak, which has already been deeply affected by them. (pause)

So in a sense, it could be, one could say, that after you have come into contact with the Perfection of Wisdom teachings things could start going badly for you, especially in such a way that you are humbled, that you are humiliated. But one must be very careful here and in a way understand what is happening, and even if you don't understand it quite in this way, obviously you shouldn't react in such situations. The process of purgation is at work. One does find this in a way with the Vajrasattva practice, I don't know whether any of you have actually done this, but it is quite common to find that when one does the Vajrasattva practice, which is a purifying or purificatory practice, one has various experiences, sometimes in dreams, which indicate that the process of purification is actually going on, and you are actually expelling, in a manner of speaking, whatever is unskillful within you.

"And yet, Subhuti, those sons and daughters of good family will take up these very sutras and will bear them in mind, recite and study them, they will be humbled, - well humbled they will be. And why? The impure deeds which these beings have done in their former lives and which are liable to lead them into the states of woe - in this very life they will, by means of that humiliation annul those impure deeds of their former lives, and they will reach the enlightenment of a Buddha."

In a little sort of Zen publication which reached me the other day, there was a quote from a Zen master, a modern Zen master from America, who says something like, "the good situations are the bad ones and the bad situations are the good ones". Well you can see [267] the sense of that from this point of view? The bad situations are situations in which you are being humiliated or being humbled. But there is a direct assault to use this Subhuti's phrase, on the ego. You don't mind being told by the Perfection of Wisdom teachings so to speak that you are egoistic or your ego is a delusion and you have got to get rid of it, but when actual circumstances and other people give painful blows to your ego, to your self-esteem, pride, conceit, and so on, that is quite another matter, that is felt much more painfully, so that is what you really need in a way. It's easy enough to read a book about it and say, "oh yes, that is all very true, perhaps we can get rid of the ego, etc, etc", but that is not so easy. You need the belabouring of actual circumstances or the ego needs the belabouring of actual circumstances.
This is one of the reasons why begging, for bhikkhus, was regarded as such a good spiritual practice because, well, one couldn't be other than humble while begging for food from other people.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: I had this experience on flag days.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right. yes... or even doing Aid for India. You're asking for something. You're in a way having to put yourself in, not just a receptive position, at least in ordinary social terms, in an inferior position. I think part at least of someone's success with Aid for India, is the ease or naturalness with which they can do this. Because they ask without feeling sort of humble and inferior because there is less egotism in fact there. Of course in traditional Buddhism especially in Zen, it is the guru who is supposed to humiliate you, humble you, but most people in the West and I suspect even in the East nowadays, cannot really any longer take such treatment, maybe because of lack of faith.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: I wasn't clear of the part that punya plays in this process.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, punya is productive of good consequences, helpful consequences, so if what you need is to be humiliated, if that is what would be the best thing for you, if you have any punya to your credit at all, that punya will bring about that state of affairs. You've earned your punya so to speak, by coming into contact with the Perfection of Wisdom teachings, by hearing them and maybe explaining them. So you have a great stock of punya. But you've also put yourself in a perilous situation from which you need to be extricated as quickly as possible. So your punya comes to the rescue and sets up a situation in which you will be humiliated so that your unskilfulness is purged and your whole being [268] brought more into
line with that very profound experience that you have had as a result of your contact with the Perfection of Wisdom teachings. Do you see the sort of process, the sequence?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I'm not sure I can understand it mechanically (or can or not) understand it mechanically how it happens. Why should your merit engineer apparently detrimental situations?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Because it is good for you. It is merit. Merit can only bring about good. Of course it really raises also this question of what is good. ?Arnold Price raises this question in connection with the law of karma generally, because it is generally held that to be reborn into, say, a wealthy family is the result of good karma, of punya, (not ?harpa). But is it necessarily? You could perhaps be born into a wealthy family that didn't respect the Dharma etc., as a result of bad karma. So one has to be very careful about identifying certain situations as good or bad, and therefore necessarily the result of good or bad karma. The fact that you are in a situation which humiliates you, may be the product of your good karma, not your bad karma.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: On a much lower level, could you look at this in terms of say initially coming into contact with the Movement. In a sense you derive quite a lot of punya from coming into contact, but then you actually have to do quite a lot of changing to actually....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, indeed. And situations may set up or situations may naturally arise one can invoke karma or not as one pleases, which are quite painful, in which you suffer. I mean, I think you have to be careful to avoid thinking that suffering, any old suffering, just does you good. It isn't quite like that. You may not even suffer acutely, certainly not physically, but there may be a situation set up, or a situation arises, in which you are humbled, you are humiliated, that is to say, your ego comes up against obstacles, this is essentially what it is. Your ego comes up against the fact of its own egohood. It is forced to recognise itself for what it is. It is force, as it were, to do something about itself.
Can one think of situations in which one might be humbled in this sort of way, or humiliated in this sort of way? Have any of you been so lucky as to have been involved in these situations?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Failure, quite often....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Failure, yes.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: When you don't succeed.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Or when you can see the possibility of failure, you can see your ego being threatened, you can feel it sort of almost crumbling within you or crumbling beneath you that you might fail, because you've identified yourself, [269] your ego has identified to such an extent with the success of that particular project. So very often a humbling situation is a situation of failure. Maybe in the case of some people who are either very unlucky or very lucky it's a failure of their whole life work. I was reading a few months ago about Milton, read in the life of Milton, how in a sense Milton saw his whole life work, everything that he stood for, practically and politically fail with the Restoration, but also apparently he did manage to come to terms with that and to be at peace with himself nonetheless, and it resulted in a great shift in his own outlook.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Perhaps he couldn't have made it if the Revolution had as it were succeeded.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: yes, yes.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Criticism is often a....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Criticism, yes. Yes you are humble because you don't like to be criticised by other people, because it is as though other people are better than you, they're acting superior to you, they are looking down on you, and people don't like that. So failure and criticism, all very positive situations in a sense one might say for the growing individual for the aspiring Bodhisattva.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Making a fool of yourself in public.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, making a fool of yourself in public (laughter). While you're at a meeting to do a lecture that you been looking forward to giving and finding that you have forgotten your notes (laughter) or even that you are at the wrong meeting. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: I find telling people you are a Buddhist creates humility.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, because of what they think a Buddhist is. So failure and criticism, these two do seem to be almost the two main sources of humbling and humiliation. There surely must be others? I think or some people illness. Illness is very humbling, especially if they thought of themselves as fit and healthy and able to do whatever they like. It is a very humbling experience or even a humiliating experience, to be forced to recognise one's physical limitations and to what an extent one is dependent upon the proper functioning of the body if you are laid up for a few weeks you don't like to feel humbled and humiliated even angry when you're subject to that limitation.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: (?Byron and his corpulence) [270]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well that is rather different. That's sort of physical deformity. Yes, people can feel humbled on account that, physical deformity, ugliness. Some people are very conscious of their personal appearance, that they are not handsome, they are not attractive, people don't seem to fall for them very easily. They have to work at it (laughter). That is not exactly a situation that you fall into. It is something you have been in all your life. You don't even see it as a situation in that sort of way. But it can be humbling for a lot of people to feel that they are not attractive to other people.

So there is failure, there is criticism, there is illness, there is physical deformity, and ugliness. Even worse than criticism perhaps is ridicule. Maybe Dhirananda touched on that in a sense because in the eyes of some people to be a Buddhist might be (so) ridiculous. Also there are situations like being accused of something you didn't doo, maybe being accused of stealing something which you didn't steal, even finding yourself in the position of being accused of committing a murder which you didn't commit and of which you know nothing. A few people have found themselves in that sort of situation. I don't know whether that is just a situation of humiliation. It is a sort of situation where you experience the meaninglessness of life, the injustice of it all.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: You experience it quite lot in competition if you lose, as a sort of tinge of ?jealousy.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well as a failure. So this suggests that in situations of this sort, in situations of failure, criticism, illness, you should be very much on your guard so as to derive the maximum benefit from that very positive situation.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: It seems (few words unclear) commitment is quite important. It is quite easy after you feel this to give up, but if you have got commitment you keep going.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It is very easy to ride on the crest of the wave. What about when you find yourself in the troughs and hollows? You have to be able to keep on going then, to bob up again when another wave comes along.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: Would being incapable of understanding something have…

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. That again is a failure. Where maybe other people are understanding things and you are not. It is as though you are duller than other people or in some way inferior. Yes one can be humiliated by consciousness of lack of talent in comparison with other people. Other people are so bright, other people are so clever, other people can play [271] the guitar, write poems, give lectures, type, manipulate computers, fly aeroplanes, and you can't do any of these things, you're really pretty stupid, (laughter) that can be a source of humbling.

turns.speaker: Khemapala
paragraphs: I have seen some things have sometimes happened in karate: the higher grade one will take someone who's going up the dance out in front of his class and actually beat him up in front of the class, to just humiliate him. So you can actually go on to break his ego.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I did see a karate master once get beaten by one of his pupils. He got so mad, that he really humiliated him, he really gave him a rough time after that, and did beat him on one occasion. (laughter) I'm not so sure that that was a very skillful thing to do. I think one should be very careful about deliberately humbling other people. No doubt humbling is good for people sometimes but I doubt very much whether one shou1d ever go out of one's way deliberately to humble somebody even for their own good. I think that is very suspect. I think one can leave that to the Buddhas and Bodhisattvas. Yes, voice an honest criticism because that is what you think, that is what you feel, but not with the deliberate intention of humbling the other person. You've voiced that honest criticism in the interests of good communication so far as you're concerned, and friendship, spiritual friendship, not because you think that he needs to be humbled and so you're going to do the humbling.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: So punya is very much of a process, once you've actually started it, it sort of carries on.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I wouldn't say that one can think of it as having a life of its own. It needs to be constantly replenished. It is finite. But on the other hand, the fact that you have accumulated a certain amount of punya makes it easier for you to accumulate further punya. So in a sense punya does breed punya, just as money makes money. But we need to be careful about what actually are the results of punya and what actually are the results of (?harpa). Not too readily assume that a nice comfortable situation must be the results of our good deeds in the past and a rather unpleasant, uncomfortable situation must be the result of our unskillful actions.

One might say that punya is always productive in happiness, but not necessarily in the short term. Punya is productive of good rather than productive of happiness, and under certain circumstances the good may be the happy, pleasurable. I have seen in the lives of some people that all seems to be going well until they took up [272] the spiritual life. I think I've seen that more before I started the FWBO than afterwards, I think now it is a bit easier because a spiritual community exists; whatever difficulties do arise after one has take up the spiritual life can be more easily, or at least more systematically handled. Probably I used to see or used to encounter these people on their own. And sometimes people complained that before, when they were living a worldly life everything seemed to go on alright, but now they've taken up the spiritual life everything seems to go wrong, as though they expected that since they've taken up the spiritual life things should go better, things should be easier, smoother. But that certainly doesn't always happen. It is almost as though for some

inexplicable reason things start going wrong in one's environment, even after one takes up the spiritual life, this is what a lot of people used to find. When I say a lot I mean a high proportion of those individuals of this kind with whom I was in contact, maybe just not more than a dozen or so I can think of. So the spiritual life may be a happy life, but it is not necessarily an easy life, or a life free from difficulties or free from suffering, not to speak of humbling, of humiliation.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: That last passage suggests that it would be quite dangerous for someone to study the Diamond Sutra in complete isolation.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: One's just said that, yes. Well another sort of humbling situation is a situation in which your knowledge or your learning is of no avail. Do you know the sort of situation I mean? Where, for instance, you find yourself in a foreign country and you can only speak the language to a limited extent so that, however intelligent you may actually be, you appear as a fool to the people you are trying to contact or to speak to. Do you know what I mean?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Yes, you like a child.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, that is right. You behave almost like a child, speak like a child and then they often speak to you accordingly and think you must be very stupid, sometimes shout at you or speak very loudly as though you were rather deaf as well as stupid. This can be a humiliating situation.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: It is a very frustrating situation.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, yes indeed, yes. Have you also been in it?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Yes.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well if you cannot speak the language at all that is better, they begin to think you are a foreigner, but if you can speak it a little [273] they probably think you're just stupid. So, therefore, I think it isn't being altogether a good friend, if you sort of salve people's wounded pride, when they are humbled and humiliated and try to assure them they haven't been humbled or humiliated. Well in a sense they haven't but in a sense, certainly psychologically, you shouldn't try to sort of minimise the experience. That doesn't really help. Just supposing someone has made himself ridiculous by forgetting his notes and having to speak without them, speaking quite badly. Well, don't say, 'What a good thing you forget your notes, you spoke really well, you know, much better than you would have done with them, it is a good thing you forgot them, you probably did it deliberately anyway, a skillful means.' (laughter)
Don't sort of console them in that sort of way. Let them experience their humiliation. Don't deprive them of that benefit.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Could you not say though that you could have too much humiliation?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Oh yes, of course you could. But then, I don't think that would be the humiliation that sprang out of the punya that you have accumulated as a result of studying the Perfection of Wisdom. That would be perhaps quite fortuitous, not the result of karma at all, or just due to other people's unskilfulness or even malice.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: So this sort of humiliation is always nicely calculated as it were, it won't break you. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well yes. It breaks what needs to be broken, it breaks what can be broken. But it cannot break what cannot be broken. It does it, yes, one could say in a positive and constructive albeit painful fashion.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: I was thinking of the story of Milarepa, building the towers and that....

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, well he was humbled by Marpa, and for much the same reason, that he needed to do something quick, because in the course of this life, he had committed so many sins that if he died, when he died, he would go straight to hell, so the only thing he could do to prevent that was to gain enlightenment itself so that he wasn't reborn at all anywhere within the samsara. So to gain enlightenment in one life time, especially after having committed those sort of actions, is a very very difficult business indeed. So Marpa could see apparently no other way of purging him of his sins, than that of humbling him, of humiliating him. So it is as though Marpa took the place of the Perfection of Wisdom. His wife, by the way was called Nairatmya. I don't know whether that gives [274] one a clue. Dakmema which is Tibetan for Nairatmya. So she was Nairatmya. Who was he? Perhaps he was the Perfection of Wisdom, even though grammatically that is feminine, but that seems to have been the way he functioned, like the Perfection of Wisdom itself, setting up a very positive situation for Milarepa as a result of which he'd be truly humbled or well and truly humbled. He'd be purged of all his sins and be able to gain enlightenment in this life. It is sometimes said that the hard gurus are actually in a way better and more effective at least with a few people than the soft ones. The soft gurus are good for larger numbers of people, but the harder gurus are better for small numbers of people, even for one or two. It is not easy to be a hard guru and a soft guru at the same time because then some disciples say, well, why does he let so and so have such an easy time and why do I get such a tough time? So usually gurus are either of this type or that type. They'll either have quite a large band of disciples to whom they'll give a pretty easy time or a small band of disciples to whom they give a pretty tough time. So
paradoxically you could say that the fewer disciples a guru has, probably the better guru he is. And the more disciples he has, probable the less he is doing his job.

I think this is an aspect of things that is not sufficiently considered or hasn't been sufficiently considered yet in the Friends: the beneficial effects of this humbling or humiliating situation. I think most people in the Friends would tend to try to avoid such situations if they could rather than sort of go half way to meet them or accept them in a positive spirit. Maybe a lot of people don't even stretch themselves sufficiently to encounter a situation of this sort. You cannot fail unless you have really tried. I mean failure has got meaning for you only if you have made a tremendous effort to succeed. So some people are sort of beneath success and failure. They cannot be in this positive situation of being humbled. They don't run any risk, they don't even try, no risk of failure then. so you can come into this positive crucial situation only if there is a certain momentum in your spiritual life, and if you are a quite vigorous sort of person. I mean some people don't even mind being ill, in fact they are rather glad of it because there's an excuse for not doing anything, but if you have got a lot of things to do and you really want to get on and you have got a burning ambition, it can be very frustrating to be ill, very humiliating. But that again is a positive situation if you use it in the right way. I am sure that some people felt in a quite existential situation [275] a certain stage in the history of Sukhavati when it seemed as though there might not be enough money to complete the work, or we might fail. That was really quite a terrible moment for some people I know, I could see it by look in their eyes, it was a sort of haunted look (laughter) a look of utter amazement, a sort of stunned look. In one case someone had that look for about two weeks and then it cleared and things started going well again, a little money trickled in, I was going to say rupee by rupee but you know what I mean. But it was touch and go for a while, practically speaking. Some people had firm faith and were not disturbed but from an ordinary mundane point of view it looked a very tricky situation, really chancy. With failure looking sometimes more likely than success. (pause) I think some people put themselves deliberately into that sort of situation in a way when they go mountaineering, especially when they do rather risky sorts of climbs. They put themselves on the line so to speak. Or they go motor racing perhaps, but I think that is a bit madder. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: This ties up here with what you were speaking of yesterday about having a deep emotional life.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right, yes, well that usually goes along with it, a powerful and vigorous and impulsive nature perhaps. But one does get the impression that so many people nowadays are quite anaemic as it were in their approach to things, not very full-blooded, either if they become villains or if they become something else. Do you see what I mean?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Would prostrations or prostrating be seen as a humbling sort of process? The whole of your body is lying flat on the ground in front of the guru.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't think in India and Tibet it is quite felt in this way. To some extent, yes, but it is much more acceptable and therefore much less of a humiliation than it would be in the West. Though you may remember that, in my memoirs, I speak about that strange guru in South India who made his disciples do a special almost slow motion full prostration because according to him, people just sort of hurried through this sort of prostration when meeting a religious person, and according to him again, that was due to some kind of lurking pride. Just to do it, yes, that is alright to do it. But get it over it as quickly as possible. So he made them do a very lengthy and elaborate form of prostration which took several minutes, just to make sure they did it properly and got duly humbled. His method of course was, too, to reduce the ego so to speak. He was quite remarkable and quite unlike any other Hindu teacher I ever met. [276] Yalahankar Swami, you may remember, the one who said, 'however high you go I shall be higher and however low you go I shall be lower.' He seems to be some belated Madhyamika survivor. Perhaps he was several hundred years old.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Isn't there in Sufism the idea that (unclear) humiliation? You must deliberately court situations where you are going to ...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, I think that is when you court situations of ridicule almost by not keeping up any external appearances of being a holy man, not doing anything that would invite people's respect, avoiding things of that sort. I mean that reminds me of a story in the life of Hakuin. Apparently Hakuin, the great Zen master, was accused once of being the father of an illegitimate child, and the indignant parents of the girl actually brought the baby to him - "It is yours, you look after it." And all he said was, "Is that so?" And he looked after it for years. And because of that, he was very badly spoken of by the other monks. Pious Buddhists generally scorned and in a sense humiliated him, But after some years the girl felt so, what shall I say, remorseful at the fact that she'd falsely accused Hakuin of being the father to protect somebody else, it preyed on her conscience so she confessed. And so her parents went and took back the child and apologised to Hakuin and said, well, we're sorry, we know that you weren't the father after all.' And he said, 'Is that so?' (laughter) He made no attempt to excuse himself or to justify, and this is quite extraordinary because one the most difficult things is to accept being falsely accused because it seems so unfair and so unjust and right is so much on your side. But the ego is also on your side. This is Hakuin's (song), that he accepted that situation.
