It must have been a very very difficult one, you know to be scorned by people who had hitherto respected you. But he accepted it, he seemed not to mind, and it did come out right in the end so to speak, or right for the other people, not in sense for Hakuin himself, he wasn't affected. But he was able to do that, he accepted this situation of humiliation. In a sense for him there was so little ego perhaps that it wasn't much of a
humiliation to lose his reputation in that way as a result of that slander, Anyway let's stop there, because it is tea time….

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Let's go back just a little bit to this question of humiliation. It does seem that failure is the humiliation par excellence. But why is it that failure is so important? Therefore, why is it that success is so important? I mean this is something that really needs looking at. I mean what does success mean? [277] Is the pursuit of success invariably unskillful, or is it skillful or skillful under certain circumstances? What do we mean by failure anyway? What do we mean by success?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: You've set yourself some end which you either achieve or you don't.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But why should you do that? why should you derive such satisfaction from setting yourself ends and achieving them? Is it part of one's very nature as a conditioned being? Is it sort of essentially the way in which one functions as a time-conditioned being?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Could be an attempt to define yourself, by attracting other people's attention to you, if you actually do something quite well, then people invariably look at you and say, ah, you've done that quite well.' So in a sense you can see it as a sort of process of definition.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It's to do with self-conciousness, isn't it? That you can cast yourself into the future in another way and that can either be skillful or unskillful, you can either be casting yourself, as it were, ahead of yourself, onto a new level, or you can be simply seeing yourself, you know, very popular and everybody looking up you and your ego sort of swelled and strong.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So which do you think you'd feel more, say, a failure which was based on a sort of unrealistic, unskillful kind of ambition, or a failure which was the failure of something well grounded?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I think the unskillful. The other sort you just sort of pick yourself up, dust yourself down…

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: In a way it's connected with sublimity, isn't it? In a way the sublime humbles you.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, but doesn't crush you.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: But it doesn't crush you.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You recognise your own insignificance in relation to the sublime object but you're also conscious of a certain affinity with it. The very fact that you can appreciate it as sublime shows that. (pause) Also when you are seeking to achieve something for some good and worthy purpose, you're concerned with the success of the project itself. You're not concerned with your success. In the more neurotic version, you are more concerned with your success, that you should be successful, not that what you are doing should be successful. Do you see the point of the distinction? (pause). Very often the two are mixed up to some extent and not very easy to distinguish, but one can in principle at least, distinguish. [278]

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Also I've been very struck by the extent to which one is, I am, determined by the desire for success.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Motivated.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Motivated. It's quite strong.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But success in the sense of desiring to achieve certain objectives which one sees as good, or motivated to achieve for the sake of personal self-satisfaction?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Well both I think.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: A mixture of both. (pause) But I think this is, in a way, necessary, in a sense is healthy, because unless you function in this way, you don't ever face the possibility of failure, you don't ever face the possibility of humiliation and therefore of growth. Some people are beneath ambition rather than being above it. (pause).

Sometimes I wish that people in the FWBO were a bit more ambitious. You know, I would really welcome it if someone had the ambition to set up, in the course of his lifetime, one hundred centres. Why, I would consider that to be very positive and healthy even though there might be a taint of worldly ambition in that, almost inevitably there would be, but no doubt in the course of setting up one hundred centres you would experience many failures which would purge away that worldly element in due course. So I wouldn't worry. (pause) But, you know, perhaps a lot of people wouldn't even have the ambition of setting up one centre, not to speak of a hundred, or even of helping to set up one, which is rather a pity, unless of course, they are getting on with something else equally vigorously such as full time meditation or literary work or something of that sort. (pause).

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I think if one's very concerned with success that actually stops you sometimes from even making an attempt, because you're so afraid of failure. It's not that you're not ambitious, it's just that you don't have the courage of your ambition, as it were.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, quite, you're over ambitious almost. You cannot risk the possibility of failure because so much is, or would be, invested in the achievement of success. Yes, you never risk giving a lecture in case it should not be absolutely outstanding and impressive. (pause)
In this way if you're not careful you become a young man who's had his future behind him, eh? (laughter).. (pause).

turns.speaker: Some people are like this with people
paragraphs: they cannot risk making advances to others in case they should be rebuffed. They'd feel the rebuff so keenly, so they never venture to make the approach. Success or social success, success in communication, is so important [279] to them. (pause) As the proverb says: 'Nothing ventured nothing gained'.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: It's more like the less you venture that way, the bigger the potential humiliation becomes. It's quite daunting. It's really hard to do anything, as it were, to change, actually, if you do get, it seems a lot less worse than you think it is.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Or you do survive, you find that you come through.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Life goes on.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Life goes on. You pick yourself up, dust yourself down and....

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: …start all over again.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: ...and stagger on. (laughter) Anyway, I don't suppose people were thinking of the Perfection of Wisdom as a source of humiliation but, yes, this is what it is, and that's why, as I said, or that's what it is amongst other things, and that's why as I said, this particular text is used in connection with confession. (pause). Alright, let's go on to 6. The Bodhisattvas, and would someone read all of that right down to the next section on Buddhas.
"6. The Bodhisattvas

"In chapter 17 the Sutra now veers back to its beginning. The question of chapter 2 is repeated, and so is the answer of chapter 3. 17 successively considers three stages of the Bodhisattva's career, just as chapters 3 to 5, and again chapter 10 did. With the absence of a real entity for its leading idea, chapter 17 once more goes over the old ground. 17a corresponds to 3; 17b to 10a; 17d to 7, 14g and the end of 8; 17c to 10c, - and 17g to 10b. I have marked the literal repetitions by placing them into double brackets.
"6a. The Bodhisattva's Vow

"17a. ((Subhuti asked: How, O Lord, should one set out in the Bodhisattva-vehicle stand, how progress, how control his thoughts? The Lord replied: Here, Subhuti, someone who has set out in the Bodhisattva-vehicle should produce a thought in this manner: 'all beings I must lead to Nirvana, into that Realm of Nirvana which leaves nothing behind; and yet, after beings have thus been led to Nirvana, no being at all has been led to Nirvana'. And why? If in a Bodhisattva the notion of a 'being' should take place, he could not be called a 'Bodhi-being'. And likewise if the notion of a soul, or a person should take place in him.)) And why? He who has set out in the Bodhisattva-vehicle - he is not one of the dharmas." [280]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Perhaps we should do these little sections separately. There's a slightly new theme: "He who has set out in the Bodhisattva-vehicle - he is not one of the dharmas." What do you think this means? All else is repetition but this is a new bit. "He who has set out in the Bodhisattva- vehicle - he is not one of the dharmas." (pause).

It's simple. He comes into a different category. He just doesn't belong among the dharmas. A new element has been introduced, a new note has been struck with the arising of the Bodhicitta, a purely transcendental element in the highest sense has been introduced, - a seed has been sown and the seed is not of the soil so to speak, the seed does not belong to the soil, it has not been produced by the soil, it's come from elsewhere, it is only planted in the soil. In gnosticism, there is the conception of the spiritual person, that is to say as the stranger, even Stranger with a capital S. He doesn't belong, he's a foreigner, he comes from a foreign country, he belongs to a foreign country, he doesn't belong here, he's not a native of this clime. It's a little bit like that: "He who has set out in the Bodhisattva-vehicle he is not one of the dharmas". He doesn't belong here. (pause). It's a bit like being a spiritual community in the midst of society. The spiritual community doesn't belong to society. It's not part of the group, even though externally it may appear to be so. It represents the introduction, not to say the invasion, of a different element, a foreign element, a foreign body in the strict sense. (pause) One can't really think of the Bodhisattva also as an individual in the ordinary sense. The individual is a mundane category, at least as we normally use the term. (pause)
Alright, let's go on to 6b.
"6b. The Bodhisattva's state of mind when he met Dipankara.

"17b. What do you think Subhuti, is there any dharma by which the Tathagata, when he was with Dipankara the Tathagata, has fully known the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment? Subhuti replied: There is not any dharma by which the Tathagata, when he was with the Tathagata Dipankara, has fully known the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment. - The

turns.speaker: Lord said
paragraphs: It is for this reason that the Tathagata Dipankara then predicted of me: 'You, young Brahmin, will be in a future period a Tathagata, Arhat, fully Enlightened, by the name of Shakyarnuni!'

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So does any new theme emerge here?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: The prediction took place because he had no perception of a dharma.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Because of his state of mind then. Also he is addressed as a young Brahmin. He was a Brahmin ascetic in those days (in Nagpur). (pause) [281]

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Dipankara gives him the name of Shakyamuni for the first time as well. I don't know if there's any real significance in that.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: By the name of Shakyamuni - a muni of the Shakya tribe. It's a sort of patronymic.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: What does 'muni' mean?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: 'Muni' means 'one who is silent' and also 'one who is wise' - sometimes translated as 'silent sage'. It basically nans 'one who is silent'. Because he is silent he is assumed to be wise, like a wise old owl, who sat in an oak, in the poem, you know it don't you?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Yes. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The little verse about the wise old owl, you must know it, it's a nursery rhyme: A wise old owl sat in an oak, The more he heard, the less he spoke, The less he spoke, the more he heard, Wasn't he a wise old bird. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You haven't heard that one? Well you miss all these nursery rhymes going to public school. (laughter) One of the disadvantages of a public school education. You do the classics these days. (laughter). So a 'muni' is perhaps a little bit like that. The more he hears the less he says and the less he says the more he hears, and in that way he becomes very wise indeed. (pause) Anyway let's go on, if there isn't any query, and do 6c.
"6c. The Bodhisattva at the end of his career
"17c. And why? 'Tathagata', Subhuti, is synonymous with true Suchness (tathata). - 17d. And whosoever, Subhuti, were to say, 'The Tathagata has fully known the utmost, right and

perfect enlightenment', he would speak falsely. And why? ((There is not any dharma by which the Tathagata has fully known the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment. And that dharma which the Tathagata has fully known and demonstrated, on account of that there is neither truth nor fraud.)) Therefore the Tathagata teaches, 'all dharmas are the Buddha's own and special dharmas'. And why? 'All-dharmas', Subhuti, have as no-dharmas been taught by the Tathagata. Therefore all dharmas are called the Buddha's own and special dharmas. - 17e. ((Just as a man, Subhuti, might be endowed with a body, a huge body. Subhuti said: That man of whom the Tathagata spoke as 'endowed with a body, a huge body', as a no-body he has been taught by the Tathagata. Therefore is he called, 'endowed with a body, a huge body'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So is there anything new here?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: "All dharmas are the Buddha's own and special dharmas"

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. So what does this mean? - "All dharmas are the Buddha's own and special dharmas"? (pause). It's as though anything belongs to the Buddha, or everything belongs to the Buddha. You can't sort of tie the Buddha down to being this or that specific thing. The Buddha is, as it were, identical with emptiness. All dharmas are empty. Therefore the Buddha as empty, possesses all dharmas. If one wanted to put it in more logical form, one could say that. The Buddha possesses, so to speak, all dharmas by virtue of his realisation of that sunyata which all dharmas essentially are. You may remember that one of the consorts of one of the so called dhyani Buddhas [282] is called Mamaki. Mamaki means, roughly speaking, "one who considers all as mine". Here of course in a positive sense. All things are hers because she does not claim anything as hers. (pause).

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: I don't know if it said before that "Tathagata is synonymous with true Suchness"?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't think it was said before. It's like saying that the Buddha is synonymous with Enlightenment, the Buddha is the embodiment of bodhi, in as much as he has realised it. In the same way, the Tathagata is no different from tathata, the ultimate, or the absolute, inasmuch as he has realised that. You can interpret Tathagata not only as the one who has thus gone, or thus come, that is to say gone like that or come like that, but one who has gone to or come from a state of suchness, tathata, or thusness. (pause).

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: I know we have touched on this before, but I am not clear on this, on the truth or fraud. "And that dharma which the Tathagata has fully known and demonstrated, on account of that there is neither truth or fraud."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It is as though the demonstration goes ultimately beyond the extremes, so to speak, of truth and falsity. If think of truth as related to existence, and fraud as related to non-existence, then
that which is beyond existence and non-existence, as the transcendental is and the Perfection of Wisdom is, must be beyond truth and falsehood, or truth and fraud at the same time. (pause).

It is truth presumably in the more dualistic sense, mundane truth. (pause). When we spoke about changing the subject slightly, but not altogether, when we spoke about the different ways in which Perfect Vision might arise, was humiliation included among them?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: No, not really.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Not really no. One could consider including it perhaps.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: In a sense the nearest you get to it is talking about death…

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, well, and we talked about illness also didn't we?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Humiliation seems to be quite tied up with the Bardo. When the Bardo experience opens up, in a sense you experience a humiliation.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Though the emphasis seems to be more on sort of experience of being overwhelmed. Yes, humiliating in a way, cut down to size. It's as [283] though reality cuts you down to size. (pause) Let's go on then to 6d.
"6d. The Bodhisattva's attitude to his tasks

"17f. The Lord said: So it is, Subhuti. The Bodhisattva who would say, 'I will lead beings to Nirvana', he should not be called a 'Bodhi-being'. And why? Is there, Subhuti, any dharma named 'Bodhi-being'? - Subhuti replied: No indeed, O Lord. - The Lord said: Because of that the Tathagata teaches, 'selfless are all dharmas, they have not the character of living beings, they are without a living soul, without personality'. - 17g. ((If any Bodhisattva should say, 'I will create harmonious Buddhafields')), he likewise should not be called a Bodhi-being. ((And why? 'The harmonies of Buddhafields, the harmonies of Buddhafields', Subhuti, as no- harmonies have they been taught by the Tathagata. Therefore he spoke of 'harmonious Buddhafields'.)) - 17h. The Bodhisattva, however, Subhuti, who is intent on 'without self are the dharmas, without self are the dharmas', him the Tathagata, the Arhat, the fully Enlightened One has declared to be a Bodhi-being, a great being."

S,: A slightly new theme emerges here at the end. "The Bodhisattva, however, Subhuti, who is intent on 'without self are the dharmas, without self are the dharmas', him the Tathagata has declared to be a Bodhi-being."

There are the two kinds of nairatmya. There is the pudgala-nairatmya and the dharma- nairatmya, the second of those being peculiar to the Mahayana. So it is as though the Buddha is saying that it is this preoccupation with dharma-nairatmya or dharma-sunyata that makes a Bodhisattva a Bodhisattva. The Bodhisattva is preoccupied with this deeper level of sunyata which goes even beyond the pudgala-nairatmya.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: What is it that makes the danger of the dharma-nairatmya? Is it that through that one - I can't really phrase it very well - is it that you're still clinging to a subtle form of self or the building blocks out of which you can return to the (unclear) self? Or is it that you're still stuck in concepts and words, sort of separate from experience?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, according to the traditional interpretation, the traditional way of looking at things, you've simply transferred from the pudgala to the dharmas, the so-called ultimate constituents of the pudgala, your notion of an entity. You've broken down the pudgala; you've seen that the pudgala, the self or the atma or whatever, is not something solid, is not something impartite. You see that it's composite, [284] therefore that it's conditioned, therefore that it's transitory and unsatisfactory. But you've broken it down into a number of elements, a number of psychophysical constituents, which you now proceed to regard as ultimate, in the same way that formerly you regarded the pudgala as ultimate. So you've transferred that concept of ultimacy from the pudgala to the dharmas. So you're back where you started from, in a way, but in a subtler sort of way.

So one has to realize that the dharmas just as much as the so-called pudgala are just almost arbitrary conceptual constructions. You need not stop there, you can go further, you can analyse further. The dharmas are not ultimate, you can go on and on indefinitely and that possibility of going on and on indefinitely is represented by the idea - it cannot be other than an idea to begin with, of dharma-nairatmya or dharma-sunyata. So it would thus seem that we're going over pretty much the same ground in this section as earlier on in the Sutra. Let's press on then to 7.
7. The Buddhas
7a. The Buddha's Five Eyes

18a. What do you think, Subhuti, does the fleshly eye of the Tathagata exist? - Subhuti replied: So it is, O Lord, the fleshly eye of the Tathagata does exist. - The Lord asked: What do you think, Subhuti, does the Tathagata's: heavenly eye exist, his wisdom eye, his Dharma- eye, his Buddha-eye? Subhuti replied: So it is, O Lord, the heavenly eye of the Tathagata does exist, and so does his wisdom-eye, his Dharma-eye and his Buddha-eye.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So we wonder what the significance of this is. There's not a word about them existing because they don't exist. (laughter) It's almost as if Subhuti has been let off lightly or is letting himself off lightly. Here the statement that the five eyes exist is simply accepted. Do you think there is any significance in this? Is the Buddha letting up a little or is Subhuti letting up? Or does it simply illustrate that mundane reality has to be allowed to exist on its own level? For its own purposes? That the transcendental does not negate the mundane? "So it is, O Lord, that the heavenly eye of the Tathagata does exist, does his wisdom-eye, his Dharma-eye and his Buddha-eye". A slightly different order from that which is sometimes given, you notice. Wisdom-eye and Dharma-eye are reversed in comparison with the list I gave on some other occasion. Did we go into this question of 'eyes'? I think we did on some occasion. [285]

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: In that lecture you (read)…

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, wasn't it sort of discussed - 'eyes' as representing different levels of perception, or maybe it was just when I was explaining Chakkhupala's name.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: We talked about it as well with the 'Five Sheaths' and the Upanishads and then went into the five 'eyes'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Ah yes, right, that's true. There was clearly the five 'eyes' represent(ing) different levels of knowledge, different degrees of awareness, of vision even, in the fuller sense. That is a quite concrete way of looking at it. The Buddha has five 'eyes' where we have only one, that is to say one pair - both on the same level. In fact, we have horizontal vision, not vertical 'vision'

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: When I looked up chakkhu in the Pali dictionary (laughter), I found that the Dharma- eye, by their references anyway, occurred most frequently in a combination of three. The list of five didn't include Dharma-eye, but rather Buddha-eye was the fourth and the fifth was samanta-cakkhu.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. At the same time, Buddha-chakkhu and samanta-cakkhu are sometimes identified, regarded, as the same thing. There are several lists, I think there's another one in the Mahavastu. But in the Pali, the Dhamma-cakkhu seems to be regarded as coming immediately after the Divine eye because it is that Dhamma-cakkhu which arises as the equivalent of Going for Refuge when one does have an actual Insight into truth. I think it is the panna-cakkhu and even the Buddha-cakkhu which are more indeterminate. They do vary somewhat. But the content of the Dhamma-cakkhu is always the same, at least in Pali. The general idea is quite clear, of successively higher levels of vision.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: (unclear) say that these eyes exist? Is that because they understand in what sense they exist, just take that as understood? (S: Say that again.) The fact that they don't say that the Tathagata's flesh now exists because it doesn't exist and that's why it exists. Is it just that they've understood with what sense it exists?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes it could be that it doesn't need to be made explicit, it could be that, yes. [286]

turns.speaker: Same Voice
paragraphs: I mean they do go back to contradiction afterwards. (pause)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. Perhaps that little refrain is no longer necessary. Perhaps the lesson has sunk in. That's why it is really seeing mountains as mountains.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: But the force it has when you come across it in a list of all negation is to give you the impression that the Buddha is the only reality, if you like, that his attributes are allowed to stand.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, because presumably for devotional purposes, they have to be allowed to stand, yes.
(End of Tape) [287]
"7b. The Buddha's superknowledge of others' thoughts.

"18b. The Lord said: What do you think, Subhuti, has the Tathagata used the phrase, 'as many grains of sand as there are in the great river Ganges'? - Subhuti replied: So it is, O Lord, so it is, O Well-Gone! The Tathagata has done so. - The Lord asked: What do you think, Subhuti, if there were as many Ganges rivers as there are grains of sand in the great river Ganges and if there were as many world systems as there are grains of sand in them, would those world systems be many? - Subhuti replied: So it is, O Lord, so it is, O Well-Gone, these world systems would be many. - The Lord said: As many beings as there are in these world systems, of them I know, in my wisdom, the manifold trends of thought. And why? 'Trends of thought, trends of thought', Subhuti, as no-trends have they been taught by the Tathagata. Therefore are they called 'trends of thought'. And why? Past thought is not got at; future thought is not got at; present thought is not got at."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm, we went into that a little in the course of one of the question and answer sessions, didn't we? It is not only past, present, and future thought that one cannot get at, one cannot get at past, present, and future anything. It is a sort of commonplace of Buddhist thought, especially earlier Buddhist thought, that the Buddha does know the trends of thought, as it were, the psychological characteristics, the tendencies, the temperaments of all other living beings. But here, this is being related to the well known ?refrainments, so to speak, that the
Buddha knows those trends of thought, because they are no-trends. (But) he knows them through the void, knows them through sunyata.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Is not got at? What...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Cannot be apprehended as a real objectively existing thing. (pause) I was thinking it might be a good exercise or a practice, if one hasn't done it before, in having gone through the Sutra, to read or to have the whole thing read aloud straight off as it were and just sit and listen to it. And see what sort of impression it then makes. It isn't very long after all. I don't know how long it would take.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: About an hour.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That is quite a reasonable length. [288]

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: We could do it the day after the end of the study.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: You could either get one person to read it - choose someone who is a good reader - or just take it in turns to read it, a section at a time.
"7c. The Buddha's Merit is no Merit

"19. What do you think, Subhuti, if a son or daughter of good family had filled this world system of 1,000 million worlds with the seven precious things, and then gave it as a gift to the Tathagatas, the Arhats, the fully Enlightened Ones, would they on the strength of that beget a great heap of merit? - Subhuti replied: they would, O Lord, they would, O Well-Gone! - The Lord said: So it is, Subhuti, so it is. On. the strength of that, this son or daughter of good family would beget a great heap of merit, immeasurable and incalculable. But if, on the other hand, there were such a thing as a heap of merit, the Tathagata would not have spoken of a 'heap of merit'."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm, this is the same, in a sense, line of thought: that something is what it is because it is not what it is, here applied to the Buddha's merit. Perhaps we should just go on until we encounter something new, something we haven't come across before.
"7d. The Buddha's Physical Body

"20a. What do you think, Subhuti, is the Tathagata to be seen by means of the accomplishment of his form-body? Subhuti replied: No indeed, O Lord, the Tathagata is not to be seen by means of the accomplishment of his form-body. And why? 'Accomplishment of

his form-body, accomplishment of his form-body', this, O Lord, has been taught by the Tathagata as no-accomplishment. Therefore is it called 'accomplishment of his form-body'. - 20b. The Lord asked: What do you think, Subhuti, is the Tathagata to be seen through his possession of marks? - Subhuti replied: No indeed, O Lord. And why? This possession of marks, O Lord, which has been taught by the Tathagata, as a no-possession of no-marks this has been taught by the Tathagata. Therefore is it called 'possession of marks'."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Have we come across the form-body before? This is the rupakaya. Even in the Hinayana and certainly in the early Mahayana, the rupakaya of the body - that is to say his phenomenal personality, phenomenal psychophysical personality, is distinguished from his Dharmakaya, which in the Hinayana does not seem to have a sort of metaphysical meaning but simply to mean the sum total of the teaching itself. But in the Mahayana, the Buddha's as it were transcendental personality is distinguished [289] from his mundane or phenomenal personality. In the Madhyamika one finds only this twofold distinction, but it seems to have been the Yogacarins who introduced or who popularised the threefold division into Dharma- kaya, Sambhoga-kaya, and Nirmana-kaya. In other words, Rupakaya is subdivided into Sambhogakaya and Nirmanakaya. I have gone into this, I think it is in the Survey. So "the accomplishment of the form-body" presumably means the attainment of the form-body as a result of previous skillful deeds, skillful deeds performed in previous lives as a result of which the Buddha has the particular psychophysical organism that he does have in his last life. Then of course from form-body to marks is not a very difficult transition; what is said about the 'form-body', much the same thing is said about the 'marks', as has been said before. Well let's go onto 7e.
"7e. The Buddha's teaching

"21a. The Lord asked: What do you think, Subhuti, does it occur to the Tathagata: 'by me has Dharma been demonstrated'? Whosoever, Subhuti, would say, 'the Tathagata has demonstrated Dharma', he would speak falsely, he would misrepresent me by seizing on what is not there. And why? 'Demonstration of dharma, demonstration of dharma', Subhuti, there is not any dharma which could be got at as a demonstration of dharma."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Let's stay with that for a little. Well clearly there is the usual "reason" inverted commas: well one cannot say that the Buddha demonstrated Dharma because demonstration of dharma is a non-demonstration of Dharma. But can one look at it in any other way, can one sort of bring down what is said to a more accessible level, that is to say, can one, when Subhuti says of the Tathagata that he does not think by me Dharma been demonstrated? Can one look at that less, as it were, metaphysically?

turns.speaker: Dhirananda
paragraphs: Maybe there is no difference between what one is and what one teaches.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Mm, yes, it is in a way a natural process, not that one is self-conscious about it. Perhaps the Buddha doesn't think of himself as teaching something called the Dharma or teaching Buddhism. He just thinks of himself, if he thinks at all in those terms, just talking, talking to other people, saying what he thinks, saying what he sees, sharing or describing his vision, he's just his life so to speak, nothing special in a way. It is like, in a way, the child's view of the adult: the child [290] thinks the adult as engaged in all sorts of very special and wonderful and mysterious activities, like for instance, in the case of adult men shaving, or going to work. Whereas (to) the person who has to shave every morning and to work, it is just part of his life - nothing special or wonderful or glamorous about it. But for the child it isn't like that; it's very special, something extraordinary.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: You've got upper case and lower case dharma used. It seems rather inconsistent.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Where is this?

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: In the first part of that, he says "by me has Dharma" capital D "been demonstrated", "The Tathagata has demonstrated Dharma." But "demonstration of dharma" is small D.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Ah, yes. That would seem to be rather arbitrary, but "any dharma which" suggests a plurality of dharmas, whereas "demonstrating Dharma", is actually teaching the Dharma. But no doubt, when being forced to choose between capitals and small letters by the exigencies of the Roman alphabet one is departing somewhat from the original text in the characters, even from the spoken tradition. (long pause) Let's go on then to 21b.

"21b. Subhuti asked: Are there, O Lord, any beings in the future, in the last time, in the last epoch, in the last 500 years, at the time of the collapse of the good doctrine who, on hearing such dharmas, will truly believe? - The Lord replied: They, Subhuti, are neither beings nor no-beings. And why? 'Beings, beings', Subhuti, the Tathagata has taught that they are all no- beings. Therefore has he spoken of 'all beings'."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. This seems to tie up a little with, "He who has set out in the Bodhisattva-vehicle, he is not one of the dharmas." In other words, to paraphrase, there will be even in the last 500 years those who " on hearing such dharmas will truly believe". But they will be able to believe because there is, so to speak, a transcendental element in them, something that transcends existence and non-existence, and which will give them an affinity for the teaching, and on account of which they cannot be classified themselves as existing or not-existing, or as beings or no-beings. They're not individuals but they don't at the same time totally not
exist. They belong to a new category, a category which is represented in its fullness by the Buddha himself.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: I was thinking a bit earlier on, the Buddha mentions how he goes through 84,000 million, millions of Buddhas and then, after that, it says that the good Dharma just lasts 2,500 years. It seems a bit... [291]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Ah, no. He is serving different Buddhas in different world epochs, in different periods of the promulgation of the teaching. In any particular given world period, it doesn't last, but taking the cosmos as a whole, it does last because it's discovered and rediscovered again after being lost.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Just seems that for all the effort he has put in for it to only last 2,500 years…

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But never mind, there will be other Bodhisattvas who will rediscover and make it known again. You might say, what's the use of combing your hair in the morning? You are only going to have to comb it again at night. (laughter) There are some things that have to be just kept up by repeated effort. The Dharma, so far as that survives in the world as Buddhism, just has to be kept up, revived and restored and brought back again and again and again. One could say that's what Bodhisattvas are for.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: In fact, it isn't just that those who believe in the Sutra and so on are neither beings nor non-beings, - all beings are neither beings nor non-beings.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hm, yes, yes that's true, from, in a way, another point of view, in a sense, an even more profound point view, because all are equally identical with, that is to say non-different from, sunyata. It's as though some beings are more empty than others, at least provisionally speaking. (long pause) Now let's go on to 7f then:
"7f The Buddha's Dharma

"22. What do you think, Subhuti, is there any dharma by which the Tathagata has fully known the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment? - Subhuti replied: No indeed, O Lord, there is not any dharma by which the Tathagata has fully known the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment. - The Lord said: So it is, Subhuti, so it is. Not even the least (anu) dharma is there found or got at. Therefore - is it called 'utmost (anuttara), right and perfect enlightenment'. - 23. Furthermore, Subhuti, self-identical (sama) is that dharma, and nothing is therein at variance (vishama). Therefore is it called 'utmost, right (samyak) - and perfect (sam-) enlightenment'. Self-identical through the absence of a self, a being, a soul, or a person, the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment is fully known as the totality of all the
wholesome dharmas. 'Wholesome dharmas, wholesome dharmas', Subhuti - yet as no- dharmas have they been taught by the Tathagata. Therefore are they called 'wholesome dharmas'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. There's a play upon words here. With regard to 'utmost, right and Perfect enlightenment', that is to say, 'anuttara samyak Sambodhi', the Buddha says: "Not even the least (anu) dharma is there - found or got at. Therefore is it called 'utmost (anuttara), right and Perfect enlightenment'. That's just a play on a word. It's not even the same anu really. You notice the first 'anu' has a little dot under it: it’s called a nasal N. In the case of the second anu there is no little dot. Actually it's a different word, a different prefix. But nonetheless it makes a metaphysical point. It's called anuttara, 'the utmost, right and Perfect enlightenment', because 'not even the least (anu) dharma is there found or got at.' And in the same way, it's called 'samyak', because it's identical and because there is "nothing therein at variance". And then the Buddha goes on to say: "Self-identical through the absence of a self, a being, a soul or a person, the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment is fully known as the totality of all the wholesome dharmas". That's something more accessible, isn't it? That enlightenment consists in the totality of all skillful dharmas, all skillful mental states, one could say in a sense. Though of course, it mustn't be forgotten that these "wholesome dharmas" are yet at the same time no-dharmas.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: What about self-identical? I didn't really understand that.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: "Self-identical through the absence of a self." Again, this is how the law of contradiction is being abrogated, the law of the - what is that? - that A is A; that means, that is, A is identical with A, but the self is self-identical through the absence of a self: I mean, A is identical with A through the absence of A, because it is not A. The again, the same abrogation of this law of contradiction. The law of the excluded middle, by the way, says that a thing must either be or not be, it must either exist or not exist, it cannot be something other, but of course, that is also not even accepted by ordinary Buddhist teaching, not to speak of the Perfection of Wisdom.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Is there any relationship between the two words 'sama' and 'sam'?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, I don't think there is an etymological connection actually. I think it is something of a play on words for the sake of making a metaphysical point. It's not a scientific etymology, I think.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: The reason I was asking was that when we use the term 'Sambhogakaya', as I understand it, it's usually translated as meaning mutual, 'body of mutual enjoyment'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, something like 'together'.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: And yet it tends to suggest 'sama', and yet we use the [293] - the word 'sam' is used, not sama, suggesting more of a sort of perfect enjoyment.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hm. No, I think as far as we know, it's not the same root. I'd have to check that but I think it isn't. I think we shouldn't take any of these traditional etymologies too seriously. Perhaps one shouldn't even take the scientific ones too seriously. I mean sama (unclear) literally means 'same'. I don't know whether that is etymologically identical with our word 'same'. I wouldn't like to of presume that it was, just from the similarity of sound, but on the other hand it might be. They look the same on the printed page, but 'sama' is pronounced samaa, our same is pronounced 'same' - a different sound.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: No, I was thinking more of the relationship between the two words 'sama' and 'sam', not the English 'same'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, I appreciate that, but I think one should beware of assuming an etymological connection, a scientific etymological connection, from the similarity of the sound, even in the case of Sanskrit itself. But there might be; one would have to check it up and trace it back.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Is the force of "nothing is therein at variance", simply to sort of put "self-identical" in a different way? Is it saying anything new?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It seems not to be saying anything new. "Not at variance with itself" would seem to be saying the same thing as that it is self-identical. (long pause) Perhaps we'd just better go on. 7g.
"7g. Once more about the Buddha's Merit

"24. And again, Subhuti, if a woman or man had piled up the seven precious things until their bulk equalled that of all the Sumerus, kings of mountains, in the world system of 1,000 million worlds, and would give them as a gift; and if, on the other hand, a son or daughter of good family would take up from this Prajnaparamita, this discourse on Dharma, but one stanza of four lines, and demonstrate it to others, compared with his heap of merit the former heap of merit does not approach one hundredth part, etc., until we come to, it will not bear any comparison. [294]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Anything new here?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: The actual use of the word 'Prajnaparamita'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It doesn't simply refer to a text, it doesn't say 'Vajracchedika', [it's] "this Prajnaparamita" or this version of the Perfection of Wisdom teaching, this sutra.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: When he refers to talking to demonstrating, "but one stanza of four lines", is it actually written in stanzas of four lines?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: One counts, a shloka consists, a far as I remember, of eighteen syllables. So one can count even prose as consisting of units of eighteen syllables. So in this way, one gets so many lines, even when it is a prose-text. The eighteen-syllable line in a particular metre is quite common in Sanskrit, just as the iambic pentameter is common in English. You could presumably reckon lengths of English prose in accordance with how many units of ten syllables there were in it.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: So does a stanza correspond to a 'shloka'?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I'm not sure but a shloka might be a double line. I wouldn't be absolutely certain of that but it is a metrical unit consisting of a certain number of syllables and prose can also be counted in terms of those units. There aren't any extra syllables as there are sometimes in English verse, no redundant syllables, no syllables which are metrically redundant.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: (few words unclear) in the tradition, as far as you know, taking this thing about stanzas of four lines literally? I mean are there parts of the Diamond Sutra which are particularly reverenced or repeated or used?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Not that I know of, not that I recollect. There are some actual verses coming, one might have thought, well they might have been made the subject-matter of more concentrated attention, more detailed exposition. I don't know that they have been, but then again, I mean so much has happened, say, in Chinese Buddhism and Tibetan Buddhism, it may well be that there were monks who were doing this, and concentrating on the exposition of just short texts. Just as in Ceylon there was a bhikkhu who was famous for his exposition of the forty (karma-panas) and nothing but the forty (karma-panas). In the same way one could have someone who was famous not just for the exposition of a single [295] whole text, but even for a small part of it, even four lines. (long pause) Alright, let's (unclear) on verses 7h and 7i and finish this section:
"7h. The Buddha as a Saviour, and the nature of emancipation.

"25. What do you think, Subhuti, does it occur to a Tathagata, 'by me have beings been set free'? Not thus should you see it, Subhuti! And why? There is not any being whom the Tathagata has set free. Again, if there had been any being whom the Tathagata had set free, then surely there would have been on the part of the Tathagata a seizing of a self, of a being, of a soul, of a person. 'Seizing of a self', as a no-seizing, Subhuti, has that been taught by the Tathagata. And yet the foolish common people have seized upon it. 'Foolish common people', Subhuti, as really no people have they been taught by the Tathagata. Therefore are they called 'foolish common people'."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: They are "no people" in the metaphysical sense. (laughter) So here the Buddha says much the same thing about the Tathagata' s leading beings and liberating them, as he has said before about his teaching of the Dharma itself. It's not a self-conscious activity in the ordinary sense. It's something he does naturally and, as it were, un-self-consciously. Or in a sense doesn't do at all. Go on to the next section and those verses.
"7i. The true nature of a Buddha

"26a. What do you think, Subhuti, is the Tathagata to be seen by means of his possession of marks? - Subhuti replied: No indeed, O Lord. - The Lord said: If, Subhuti, the Tathagata could be recognized by his possession of marks, then also the universal monarch would be a Tathagata. Therefore the Tathagata is not to be seen by means of his possession of marks. Subhuti then said: As I, O Lord, understand the Lord's teaching, the Tathagata is not to be seen through his possession of marks. Further the Lord taught on that occasion the following stanzas:
"Those who by my form did see me, And those who followed me by voice Wrong the efforts they engaged in, Me those people will not see.
"26b. From the Dharma should one see the Buddhas, From the Dharmabodies comes their guidance. Yet Dharma's true nature cannot be discerned, And no one can be conscious of it as an object." [296]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. "Those who by my form did see me, And those who followed me by voice, Wrong the efforts they engaged in, Me those people will not see." In other words, the Buddha is to be identified with his Dharmakaya rather than with his Rupakaya. There's a trace of this in the Pali canon itself, where the Buddha says, "He who sees the Dharma sees me". You don't see
the Buddha when you just behold his bodily form. You see the Buddha in a deeper and truer sense when you see the Dharma, when you realize the Dharma.

So therefore, he says here: "Those who by my form did see me, And those who followed me by voice, Wong the efforts they engaged in, Me those people will not see. From the Dharma one should see the Buddhas, From the Dharmabodies comes their guidance. Yet Dharma's true nature cannot be discerned, And no one can be conscious of it as an object." It just about up the matter. From the Dharma one should see the Buddhas; one should see the Buddhas as embodiments of the Dharma, as embodiments of Bodhi if you like, not just see them in their ordinary, human, phenomenal personalities. Their guidance comes from the Dharmabodies, comes from the higher spiritual realities with which they've become one, which they've realized. At the same time one is not to think this Dharma, this higher spiritual reality, as existing as an object as distinct from a subject. It transcends the subject-object duality. It's what I've called a 'pseudo-object' - something which is essentially not an object, which transcends the subject-object duality, but which is perceived or spoken of as though it was in fact an object. It is a pseudo-object. (pause) Is there anything further on that, or anything further on anything we've done this morning?

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: Were these verses found anywhere else?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Not that I know of. But one could almost imagine that first verse at least being found in the Pali canon somewhere in an equivalent version.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It's curious that they sort of pop up in the Diamond Sutra in a completely different form.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It could be that they are part of a quite old tradition. To the best of my knowledge they don't occur in the Large Perfection of Wisdom sutras.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: The Indian number system: is it to the base 10?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, yes. The Indians are supposed to have invented the decimal point, as well as the zero. [297] There's a lot of things that we take for granted, were actually once upon a time invented. The Romans couldn't get very far with mathematics because they had a very cumbrous set of numerals. You can do lots of wonderful things by simply shifting the decimal point. They didn't know that, they didn't have that, and even the Greeks didn't have that. (pause) All right, let's leave it there for today. (End of tape) [298]
"7k. The effectiveness of meritorious deeds

"27. What do you think, Subhuti, has the Tathagata fully known the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment through his possession of marks? Not so should you see it, Subhuti. And why? Because the Tathagata could surely not have fully known the utmost, right and perfect enlightenment through his possession of marks. Nor should anyone, Subhuti, say to you, 'those who have set out in the Bodhisattva-vehicle have conceived the destruction of a dharma, or its annihilation'. Not so should you see it, Subhuti! For those who have set out in the Bodhisattva-vehicle have not conceived the destruction of a dharma, or its annihilation."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Hmm. Because one cannot conceive the destruction or the annihilation of that which in reality does not exist. Any further point in connection with this chapter? There's a slight variation that the Tathagata does not know the "utmost, right, and perfect enlightenment through his possession of marks". He himself even, apparently, does not infer that because he possesses those marks as a result of his previous good deeds, that therefore he is enlightened. In other words the Buddha knows the difference between chakravasti raja and Buddha, which no doubt was only to be expected!

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Is there any particular reason why he's (unclear) the concept with the destruction of dharmas arising at this point?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It may be that we are in fact concerned with subtler doubts, as Han Shan maintains. The fact that one reduces dharmas to sunyata does not mean that one destroys them because the mere fact that they are reduced to sunyata means that they are not entities to be destroyed anyway. (one minute pause). Anyway, go on to 28 which has got some connection with 27. [299]

"28. And again, Subhuti, if a son or daughter of good family had filled with the seven precious things as many world systems as there are grains of sand in the river Ganges, and gave them as a gift to the Tathagatas, Arhats, fully Enlightened Ones, - and if on the other hand a Bodhisattva would gain the patient acquiescence in dharmas which are nothing of themselves and which fail to be produced, then this latter would on the strength of that beget a greater heap of merit, immeasurable and incalculable.

"Moreover, Subhuti, the Bodhisattva should not acquire a heap of merit. - Subhuti said: Surely, O Lord, the Bodhisattva should acquire a heap of merit? - The Lord said: 'Should acquire', Subhuti, not 'should seize upon.' Therefore is it said, 'should acquire'."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well this "patient acquiescence in the dharmas which are nothing of themselves and which fail to be produced", this is in a way quite important, this is the well known, anutpanna- dharma-kshanti, that is, the patient acceptance of non-arisen dharmas or the non-arising of dharmas. It's this which the Bodhisattva experiences on entering the eighth bhumi, or which

he enters the eighth bhumi by experiencing. In other words he does not see dharmas as real entities, he sees dharmas as rising and falling, as it were coming into existence and passing away, but he doesn't see them as real entities coming into existence and passing away. He sees that in reality though they appear to arise, though they appear to be produced, in fact they do not arise, they are not produced, but he patiently accepts that, so to speak. This is called anutpanna-dharma-kshanti. You can look it up in the Survey.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Could you say the Pali again?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: This is Sanskrit. (S spells it out) Anutpanna-dharma-kshanti. The comparison which is often given is that of the illusion conjured up by the magician or conjurer. He makes you see an elephant. After a while you don't see an elephant, but has any elephant really come into existence? Or supposing the magician conjures up a figure who kills the elephant, has an elephant really been killed? No, it's all an illusion, not that you don't [300] perceive something, but what you perceive does not correspond to any ultimate reality. So it's the same with the dharmas. They don't really arise, though you see what you think are dharmas arising. (pause) So it's this vision of the acceptance of the non-production of dharmas which makes a bodhisattva irreversible. Of the eight stages it's the eighth bhumi, which is the stage of irreversibility, in other words it's a form of Insight, a form of Wisdom, a form of the Perfection of Wisdom in quite an advanced form. So paradoxically by seeing dharmas in this way the bodhisattva acquires an even greater heap of merit. (pause)

turns.speaker: Vessantra
paragraphs: What is it that distinguishes this anutpanna-dharma-kshanti from say the level of insight that arises earlier on in a bodhisattva's path? What's the distinguishing feature?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It's difficult to say, because all are degrees of prajna (or) even degrees of prajna paramita, so it's as though the conceptual formulations of these different degrees coincide even though the experiences which they express belong to different levels. In a sense, inasmuch as you see things in terms of sunyata at all you see things like things conjured up by a magician, this is a simile which is used throughout the Mahayana. But perhaps one could say that the distinction here is his patient acceptance, his full acceptance of that way of seeing things. Perhaps up until that point he's struggled with it, there's been some resistance. He's not been able to quite fully see things in that way but now he has. So perhaps the emphasis is more to be on kshanti than on anutpanna-dharma. Perhaps he's been trying to see these things in those terms for a long time. Perhaps it's only now that he's fully succeeded. He fully accepts that particular vision of reality, there's no further resistance. (pause) So that would suggest irreversibility. But, there still of course remains a further point, a further question: why should seeing things in that way and accepting seeing things in that way constitute irreversibility? Why that in particular? What special [301] connection has that got with samyak sambuddhahood as distinct from that arahantship? I don't know that this has ever been explained in the texts. It's

as though from that point on you don't take anything seriously, ontologically speaking. It's all like a dream, a vision, not that you don't perceive it, but you see through it quite thoroughly. It's like having a dream and knowing that it's a dream. In the same way your mundane experience or your experience of the mundane, of conditioned existence is quite vivid, perhaps it's more vivid than ever, but you're less misled by it than ever, you're mot misled by it all in fact. Perhaps it's at this point that you see not only that there are no beings to save but among the so-called beings there's yourself, there's no question of any self to save. So perhaps from that point on, even the possibility of thinking in terms of a so-called individual emancipation no longer exists. One could say, from that point onwards you genuinely see no difference between yourself and others, they're all equally dream-like and as it were illusory. It's all just like a game, you no longer mind which particular game you play. But putting it as it were more positively: it's just like a play in another sense, a sort of spontaneous creative expression, perhaps I should say am spontaneous expression of creativity. (pause of one minute)

So if one - an interesting question arises - if one thinks of irreversibility in that way, in those terms, and if the irreversibility of the bodhisattva is analogous to, at least analogous to, the irreversibility of one on the so-called Hinayana path, in other words if irreversibility is much the same thing, broadly speaking, from a comparative standpoint, as stream entry, what bearing has this notion of anutpanna-dharma kshanti and so on got on the subject of stream- entry? It's as though the bearing is in connection with the first of those fetters: sakayam ditthi. It's as though in both cases you're no longer thinking that there is in fact anybody to be saved, or anybody to be delivered in the ultimate sense, whether yourself or anybody else. That aspect isn't stressed in the case of the so-called the Hinayana path. Certainly in the case of the Hinayana path there is no metaphysical emphasis. It's all quite practical, straightforward, and in a sense [302] psychological. (pause)

turns.speaker: Then the Buddha says
paragraphs: "Moreover, Subhuti, the Bodhisattva should not acquire a heap of merit. - Subhuti said: 'Surely, O Lord, the Bodhisattva should acquire a heap of merit? - The Lord said: 'Should acquire', Subhuti, not 'should seize upon'. Therefore it is said 'should acquire'". In other words, the Buddha seems to make a distinction between acquiring merit and seizing upon merit. There's no harm if merit is acquired, as it were spontaneously produced, but one mustn't be preoccupied with the thought of merit, nor think that merit is a real thing, or that the acquisition of merit is a real acquisition by a real person, who really does acquire. Let's go on to chapter 29.

"29. Whosoever says that the Tathagata goes or comes, stands, sits or lies down, he does not understand the meaning of my teaching. And why? 'Tathagata' is called one who has not gone anywhere, nor come from anywhere. Therefore is he called 'the Tathagata, the Arhat, the fully Enlightened One'."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The word 'Tathagata' can be understood literally as either meaning 'the one who has gone thus' or 'come thus'. The usual Theravada interpretation is quite plain and straightforward, not to say prosaic: that he is one who has gone to or come from the state of enlightenment, as his predecessors have gone to it or come from it. In other words, the Tathagata means: one who has attained enlightenment in the same way as his predecessors have attained it. That is the meaning. The Mahayanists often try to give a profounder meaning. One explanation is that the Buddha has gone to a state of enlightenment through Wisdom, and come from it, i.e. back into the world of samsara through Compassion. Tathagata means, 'thus come', or 'thus gone', according to how you divide the word. You can either make it Tatha-gata which mean, 'thus gone, or Tatha-agata, which means 'thus come'. If you join together Tatha-gata and Tatha- agata, they both make Tathagata.

So the Sutra says the Tathagata, despite the literal meaning of his title, neither comes nor goes, sits or lies down. In other words, one could look at it as meaning in the first place that the Buddha is not to be identified with his physical body, not with his rupakaya, but with his dharmakaya. And clearly the dharmakaya neither comes nor goes, and the dharmakaya certainly doesn't sit or lie down or stand.

But one could understand it even more deeply than that. That even the phenomenal personality of the Buddha, in an ultimate sense, doesn't come or go, because in the ultimate sense there's no coming or going [303] at all, any more than there is any arising or destruction of dharmas. Sometimes it is said, I'm not sure if it's in the Perfection of Wisdom itself, that everything remain in a state of, as it were, immobility in the ultimate sense - that's perhaps a difficult concept. (pause) There's another little thought that occurs to me, just seeing how much more we have to do, I am reminded of the fact that the Sutra has thirty-two chapters, according to the traditional division. Do you think that this is entirely coincidental or whether there is any meaning in it?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: - the thirty-two marks...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There's the thirty-two marks. So, it's as if each of the chapters is like a mark. But, can you recognize the Buddha from his marks? Can you recognize the Perfection of Wisdom from these thirty-two chapters? (laughter) One might say that was quite a question. I can't believe that the coincidence is not deliberate. And if it is deliberate, it must have some such meaning. It must be a sort of spiritual joke, not to say a transcendental joke. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Is this traditional way of breaking the text up? It's not something that Conze has done, is it?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: No, it would seem not. No. It would seem that traditionally there are these thirty-two chapters. 1 don't know whether in the very beginning but I think, - I don't know whether Conze says anything about this - but I think, I have a vague recollection - that Kumarajiva divided the text into thirty-two sections or that his translation was divided into thirty-two sections. I'll have to check. But I don't think Conze has, so to speak, invented these sub- divisions. Perhaps his Rome edition of the text will give information. We have, or should have, a copy of that at Padmaloka. I did have a copy. It might have been one of the books that disappeared when all the library was at Aryatara. We definitely had one. If it has disappeared, and we don't know where, we should try to get one though it's probably out of print. So it seems there's a kind of play on the title 'Tathagata'; that the Buddha is called Tathagata because he does not come and does not go.
Now we've come to the eighth section of translation. "Advice to the Imperfect" which may or may not be relevant! [304]
"8. Advice to the imperfect
"8a. The material world

"30a. And again, Subhuti, if a son or daughter of good family were to grind as many world systems as there are particles of dust in this great world system of 1,000 million worlds, as finely as they can be ground with incalculable vigour, and in fact reduce them to something like a collection of atomic quantities, what do you think, Subhuti, would that be an enormous collection of atomic quantities? - Subhuti replied: So it is, O Lord, so it is, O Well-Gone, enormous would that collection of atomic quantities be! And why? If, O Lord, there had been an enormous collection of atomic quantities, the Lord would not have called it an 'enormous collection of atomic quantities'. And why? What was taught by the Tathagata as a 'collection of atomic quantities', as a no-collection that was taught by the Tathagata. Therefore is it called a 'collection of atomic quantities'.

"30b. And what the Tathagata taught as 'the world system of 1,000 million worlds', that he has taught as a no-system. Therefore is it called 'the world system of 1,000 million worlds'. And why? If, O Lord, there had been a world system, that would have been a case of seizing on a material object, and what was taught as 'seizing on a material object' by the Tathagata, just as a no-seizing was that taught by the Tathagata. Therefore is it called 'seizing on a material object'. - The Lord added: And also, Subhuti, that 'seizing on a material object' is a matter of linguistic convention, a verbal expression without factual content. It is not a dharma nor a no-dharma. And yet the foolish common people have seized upon it.

"The principle of the simultaneity of contradictions is now extended to the material world. Overawed by the immensity of the universe outside us, we may well despair of reducing it to emptiness. And our spirit is often weighed down by the mere thought of the mass of matter which seems to escape the spiritual laws in which it feels at home. Spiritual aspirations threaten to be swallowed up by this senseless bulk into a sort of nightmare of meaninglessness. The enormous quantity of matter that we perceive around us, compared with the trembling little flicker of spiritual insight that we perceive within us, seems to tell strongly in favour of a materialistic outlook on life. But if we look more closely, then we discover that there is no bulk of matter at all, but only thoughts and words. The material world also obeys the laws governing thoughts and statements. These dialectical laws are valid everywhere, even light-years away, and they apply to all that is contained in the immensity of space. These material things have their roots in our own minds, and it is there that they can be uprooted. If you grab at the idea of world-systems and make yourself miserable, allowing them to crush your spirit, it is still a matter of what you do, it is still a state of mind which worries you. [305]

"Archimedes once divided the universe up into particles the size of grains of sand, and decided that there would be [8 times] 10^63 of them. About twenty years ago I read that the universe contains 10^79 pairs of ultimate particles, i.e. protons and electrons at that time. I do not know what the present estimate is - and the Sutra is content to describe the number as enormous. The things which are composed of atomic dust are not, however, real genuine units, and therefore each one of them is a non-collection. And a universe is no more than a fortuitous conglomeration of various elements (see p. 40), and therefore it is really a no- system.

"Seizing on a material object, the Sanskrit pindagraha, is deliberately ambiguous. The range of the meanings of the word pinda is rather wide. It may mean 'lump, ball'; (2) 'morsel of food given in alms'; (3) 'livelihood'; (4) 'body, person, individual'; (5) 'material object'.

"A verbal expression without factual content - this is a rather free translation of avacya, which at chapter 7 was translated as 'cannot be talked about'. Haribhadra explains that one 'cannot distinguish the various objects to which the different words refer' - And yet the foolish common people have seized upon it. Asanga explains: How is it that the profane believe to grasp that which in reality cannot be grasped? Because they are foolish, the knowledge they possess is merely a matter of linguistic convention. Entirely imprisoned in their verbal habits they do not know that all these things, like the notion of mass, etc., are mere conventional expressions, and so they grasp all this in a different way from what it really is, otherwise than it really is."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right. Bottom of page 65, bottom of that last paragraph. Do you think that Conze is correct in saying that a universe is no more than a fortuitous conglomeration of various elements and therefore it is really a no-system? Is that why it's a "no-system" according to the text?
Voice. No. It's because the various elements themselves have no existence.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. It's because the various elements themselves have no existence, because the conglomeration is a 'no-conglomeration'. It's not because of the 'fortuitousness' that it is a 'no- system'. You see what I mean? Conze gives just, so to speak, a scientific reason, but that isn't the reason given by the sutra. Does the Sutra even say that the universe is no more than a fortuitous conglomeration of various elements? [306] One would have thought the operation of the law of karma prevented things from being completely fortuitous, at least. I think he's slipped up a bit here. (pause)
Does anyone know what the present count is? I think if Ratnaprabha was here he'd probably tell us.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: We seem to be getting to the point where ultimate particles aren't really seen as particles.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, we've got beyond the protons and electrons haven't we? That's rather old-fashioned now, isn't it?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: We've got strange particles now.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We need a very recent edition of Encyclopedia Britannica or the very latest number of Scientific American! Just, entirely as a digression, it does occur to me it may be, we ought to keep a little bit abreast of some of these more recent discoveries or speculations. Perhaps it wouldn't be a bad idea if we didn't incline exclusively to the literary side of things and perhaps subscribe to a scientific periodical, I mean at Padmaloka, which will keep us a little bit up to date with regards to what is going on in the world of science. It might be useful. I mentioned the 'Scientific American'. I believe that is the outstanding popular scientific publication in the States.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Nature.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There's Nature in Britain, though isn't that biologically rather than physically orientated? Perhaps we should consider subscribing to something like that.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: There is a publication called 'New Scientist' which is the English equivalent of 'Scientific American'.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Perhaps we could look at each of them and decide which would be more useful. Otherwise, we are rather weighted in the literary and arts side of the spiritual life, so to speak. Well, perhaps there's more of an overlap in that direction. But nonetheless, perhaps we can't ignore the other side.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: I heard a story about a contention between Neils Boor and Einstein. Einstein said that the universe worked by cause and effect, and Boor said that the universe worked from conditions. And Einstein [307] devised an experiment which would prove whether it was cause and effect or not. It took them about sixty years before they were able to do the experiment and they found out that it seemed to indicate that the universe didn't work by cause and effect. Einstein said that God didn't play dice.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. That's his famous remark.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Einstein made some very positive statements about Buddhism.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, perhaps we should take note of these without going so far as to think in terms of Scientific Buddhism. Of course, I mean, there might be some tiny area of Buddhism where there is an overlap with some, - one can only say speculations or theorizing of science. (pause) What about this statement of Conze's:

"Overawed by the immensity of the universe outside us, we may well despair of reducing it to emptiness. And our spirit is often weighed down by the mere thought of the mass of matter which seems to escape the spiritual laws in which it feels at home. Spiritual aspirations threaten to be swallowed up by this senseless bulk into a sort of nightmare of meaninglessness. The enormous quantity of matter that we perceive around us, compared with the trembling little flicker of spiritual insight that we perceive within us, seems to tell strongly in favour of a materialistic outlook on life."
Is this the sort of way that one feels? Or what many people feel? That some sensitive people feel? Do you think? Does one feel weighed down by the brute mass of materiality?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: I think, say, looking at the night sky can sometimes be very inspiring. I don't get the feeling of great masses of matter.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It all seems rather ethereal, if anything, wouldn't one say? Some people do say on thinking of the immensity of space, there is that famous remark of Pascal, though now it's believed to

have been not his own thought as it were, but the thought of some other person whom he perhaps was going to imaginatively (few words unclear) notes of his so-called thoughts were only notes for a larger work he was going to write. Anyway he said: "The silence of those empty spaces terrifies me". A statement of his own feeling (words unclear). I suppose one can feel like that. But as far as I can remember from my own limited studies of the subject space is mostly just empty. There's very little sort of solid matter so to speak, and that only relatively solid, diffused very very thinly [308] throughout space. So it isn't exactly crowded. You don't get the impression of a vast bulk of matter. If anything you have an impression of lots and lots of empty space with a few particles floating here and there. Isn't that so?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: There are different theories about there being quite fine clouds of matter in what seems to be empty space.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: I don't get the feeling of a great mass of matter in the universe. What's a lot more common is the feeling of being very insignificant and very (words drowned by jet aircraft overhead).

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: "A verbal expression without factual content" - this is a rather free translation of avacya, which at chapter 7 was translated as 'cannot be talked about'. Unspoken, unspeakable - 'that cannot be talked about'. One cannot distinguish the various objects to which the different words refer. Cannot distinguish them as ultimate entities. (pause) Anyway, let's go on in the text.
"8b. Views and Attitudes

"It is therefore not the suppression of either atoms or worlds which leads to enlightenment, but the elimination of the wrong views about them. This theme is now further clarified in chapter 31. Two kinds of false views are mentioned in succession, first the assumption of a self (31a), and secondly that of a dharma (31b).

"31a. And why? Because whosoever would say that the view of a self has been taught by the Tathagata, the view of a being, the view of a living soul, the view of a person, would he, Subhuti, be speaking right? - Subhuti replied: No indeed, O Lord, no indeed, O Well-Gone, he would not be speaking right. And why? That which has been taught by the Tathagata as 'view of self', as a no-view has that been taught by the Tathagata.? Therefore is it called 'view of self'. 31b. The Lord said: It is thus, Subhuti, that someone who has set out in the Bodhisattva-vehicle should know all dharmas, view them, be intent on them. And he should know, view and be intent on them in such a way that he does not set up the perception of a dharma. And why? 'Perception of dharma, perception of dharma,' [309] Subhuti, as no- perception has this been taught by the Tathagata. Therefore is it called 'perception of dharma'.

"The view of self is in fact a no-view, because without a really existing object. The perception of a dharma is a no-perception, because there is nothing to perceive. Both views have originated from wrong imagination. It is therefore, as Asanga says, 'impossible to win enlightenment by the suppression of either self or dharma, because they do not exist', and can therefore not be suppressed. 'In consequence, both the affirmation of self and dharmas, as well as their negation, derive from the assumption of something unreal and refer to something which is wrongly conceived. Both therefore represent a subtle 'cover' (see p. 95) which must be eliminated by the 'cognition' taught in this Sutra."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: This is all in a way familiar ground pudgala-nairatmya and dharma-nairatmya: seeing both pudgala and dharmas in terms of sunyata, seeing these ultimately mental constructions.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: In what sense would Conze be using the word imagination here? "Both views have originated from wrong imagination."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: He might be thinking in terms of the Yogacara abhutapari which is the sort of illusion- making faculty. It's a different use of the word imagination from that which we use. (long pause) Let's carry on then. 8c.
"8c. The key to supreme knowledge

"32a. And finally, Subhuti, if a Bodhisattva, a great being had filled world-systems immeasurable and incalculable with the seven precious things, and gave them as a gift to the Tathagatas, the Arhats, the fully Enlightened Ones, and if, on the other hand, a son or daughter of good family had taken from this Prajnaparamita, this discourse on Dharma, but one stanza of four lines, and were to bear it in mind, demonstrate, recite and study it, and illuminate it in full detail for others, on the strength of that this latter would beget a greater heap of merit, immeasurable and incalculable. And how would he illuminate it? So as not to reveal. Therefore is it said, 'he would illuminate'. So as not to reveal: The Prajnaparamita concerns the unconditioned Absolute and Emptiness. This should not be revealed directly, if only for the reason that that cannot be done. It is illuminated, or explained, indirectly by stating the wise attitude to conditioned things, as in the stanza of four lines which follows.
