From this Conditioned the Unconditioned is then different, although identical with it (see p. 83). Nevertheless, the verse contains the essential teaching, because, when a complete understanding of conditioned things, and a mastery over them, have been attained, their impurities can no longer defile, and thereby ipso facto a state of Nirvana is attained. [310] When we have realized what conditioned things really are, we will take no more delight in them, will become increasingly purified, become more and more independent of them, and finally cease to rely on them. And that is Nirvana and Emptiness. Kumarajiva's translation
makes this connection clear by adding, just before the verse, this sentence: 'When he does not seize upon signs, Suchness remains unmoved.'
"As stars, a fault of vision, as a lamp, A mock show, dew drops, or a bubble, A dream, a lightning flash, or cloud, So should one view what is conditioned."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Let's just stop there. To illuminate the Absolute or Emptiness would seem to me to give expression or to communicate by metaphorical means. Conze doesn't say that, perhaps he wouldn't agree with that, but it does seem to amount to that because it says: "It is illuminated, or explained indirectly by stating the wise attitude to conditioned things, as in the stanza of four lines". This attitude is expressed, or the way in which one sees conditioned things is expressed, entirely in terms of similes. It's almost as though there can be no direct, so to speak, conceptual revelation of the Absolute, but it can be illuminated through metaphor and through simile, possibly through poetry, though again Conze not agree with that. Anyway, ponder that thought for a moment. This verse is a very famous verse. It's probably the most famous part of the whole Sutra. It's very often quoted. One could even say it's a useful verse on which to meditate, so to speak, or to reflect in such a way that insight or wisdom arises.
Anyway Conze gives an explanation no doubt basing himself on the commentaries of exactly what each one of the similes means. So let's go into that. [331]
"The characteristics of all conditioned things, or of all the elements of Samsara, should be considered from nine points of view:
"1. Like stars. (a) Stars are distant and unreachable. One cannot get to them, they are unattainable, as all dharmas are. One cannot lay hands on them, cannot possess them.
"(b) They are small in size, insignificant, when seen on the background of the vast vacuity of space.

"(c) They can be seen only when there is no sun. As the stars are no longer visible when the sun comes to shine, so also the things of the world are seen only in the darkness of ignorance, and are no longer noticed when the normal mental reactions to them come to a stop, after the true non-dual cognition of the Absolute has taken place. All mental elements, as Vasubandhu says, disappear when right cognition is realized, just as the stars disappear when the sun shines. They become invisible, and all views cease.
"(d) Alternatively, the word taraka can also mean 'meteor'. In that case, because of its short life, the meaning is analogous to that of no. 8, the 'lightning flash'."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: So is this clear why dharmas are like stars? "Stars are distant and unreachable, small in size, and can be seen only when there is no sun." (pause)

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: What do you think would be these "mental elements" which "disappear when right cognition is realized"?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: If one was to (unclear) it could be what the Sarvastivadins called the (?shaita) dharmas, the particular mental states associated with consciousness. It could mean samskaras; it isn't really very clear. In a general sense it's clear, but what specifically "mental elements" means isn't clear. Anyway, you can look up the text of that.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: These commentaries generally, they are not available in English translations.
S,: No, there's only the Rome edition by Tucci of the Sanskrit text. These are also things we ought to have.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: You could also say that stars are quite attractive. They do attract the eye.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes. Only when the sun hasn't risen.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: We were talking about scientific knowledge or understanding. It tends to militate against seeing stars. I think I've been brought up to think of a star as being a huge mass; it's got real solidity, but then you [312] see that it's a tiny fragile point of light. I project onto that scientific knowledge I have gathered. So in some ways, it's not so easy to see stars...

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: But even the biggest star is tiny in comparison with the magnitude of space, even those stars which are many times bigger than the sun.

turns.speaker: Amoghavira
paragraphs: I find it quite interesting in this Sutra that we've been dealing in concepts for the last thirty-one chapters and knocking them down, building them up and knocking them down, and then eventually it ends up in similes.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, perhaps, one can't do without poetry after all. - Let's go onto the second item:

"2. The second item, a fault of vision, timira, may mean 'darkness, blindness, a cataract'. The ignorant are often compared to people blinded by darkness (see chapter 14g). 'Ignorance overpowers beings through lack of vision, or through false vision, just as a cataract

overpowers the eyes' (VM 583). - 'Ignorance' means that (a) intellectually, one sees nothing, for ignorance is a condition unfavourab1e to knowledge; (b) emotionally, one feels lost and terrified; (c) volitionally, one knocks against things, stumbles about, is irritated, and wants to get to the light. The world as it appears to the ignorant is like a hallucination which springs from an eye-disease, and the things which are wrongly perceived are about as real as the spots which liverish people see in front of their eyes, or as the hairs which a monk afflicted with timira believes to see in his almsbowl."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: The basic point being that things are not as they appear to be. In a way, it is as simple as that. Perhaps this illustration is a bit extreme, because it suggests that there's nothing, absolutely nothing, corresponding to those spots dancing in front of your eyes at all. I mean the Yogacara does draw a distinction between what is relatively real and what is illusory. So that which exists relatively or at least has only relative existence, can be compared, in relation to that which is absolutely existent, can be compared to an illusion. But the point of the comparison is not that the relatively real is illusory, but that it is only relatively real in relation to that which is absolutely real.

So to make this even clearer the Yogacara introduces a distinction between that which has an illusory existence or truth, that which has an relative existence or truth, and that which has an absolute existence or truth. The absolute is the [313] parinispanna. The relative is the paratantra, and the illusory is the parikalpita. We did this when we studied Conze's "Outlines of the Mahayana". That was the most useful chapter in the whole book, if I remember rightly, on the three kinds of knowledge of the Yogacara. Was anyone present on that occasion. Otherwise Conze came in for a bit of a knocking. But that chapter was quite good. Sorry, not Conze, Suzuki.
I don't know whether there's any significance in the fact that there are nine comparisons here. (unclear) one for each yana of the Nyingmapas.

"3. The simile of the lamp illustrates two aspects of this world of ours: (a) A lamp goes on burning only as long as fuel is fed into it. So also this world continues only while we have cravings. when craving ceases to supply the drive, the world will come to an end. (b) In their Indian form, before glass chimneys came to be used, lamps were liable to be blown out by the wind. Likewise one conditioned thing is very easily disturbed by others, and the continued existence of conditioned dharmas is all the time distinctly precarious."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: This is clear enough. Dharmas are dependent upon other dharmas and are very easily disturbed. Sometimes it seems strange that anything should exist at all, should manage to survive, because its coming into existence and its survival seems to be dependent on so many different factors. If you think, even our being here in this place, being here at Il Convento,

depends on so many different factors. First of all, it depends on the fact that there is a place called Il Convento for us to be at anyway. So why is it possible for us to be here? Well, because the owner doesn't want to be here. Perhaps he needs some money and is renting the place out. Why is that possible at all? Why has he got it? Well because there was a Napoleonic war and this place, which was a monastery, was knocked out by Napoleon, ad wasn't a monastery any more. If it had still been a monastery, well, the present owner wouldn't have had possession (unclear). The Franciscans wouldn't have been happy to vacate it for three months while we had a Buddhist course here. So there's a wonderful series of causes and conditions that has led up to it even being possible for us to have this place for three months at all. I mean, ultimately it's all thanks to Napoleon. So all the more reason perhaps for some people regarding him as one of their heroes. We have Napoleon to be thankful for. [314]

And how is it possible for us to get here? Well, clearly, money was needed, and we had to work in cooperatives and other ways, otherwise the money wouldn't have been forthcoming, etc. etc. If we hadn't, of course, come along to the FWBO it wouldn't have been possible for us to be here. And that might have depended upon someone taking us along, or telling us something, or us seeing an advertisement as we saw with people telling their life stories. So the fact that thirty people are assembled here for three months at this particular time represents a sort of concatenation - a coming together - of thousands upon thousands of millions of different causes and conditions. We don't even know the full story of how this place was closed down as a monastery. We don't even know that. Or how the present owner came to acquire it.

turns.speaker: Gerry
paragraphs: Also we may have been involved in those conditions in the past.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Who knows? Well, one of us might have been Napoleon. (laughter) It might have been anyone! (More comments and laughter) So really the continued existence of conditioned dharmas is all the time distinctly precarious, and to get a number of conditioned dharmas that are cooperating in a particular way, for any length of time is quite a feat.

turns.speaker: Jinavamsa
paragraphs: Do you think it's good the way he puts it: "when craving ceases to supply the drive the world will come to an end"?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, yes and no. This is in a way the Hinayana way of looking at it. Perhaps it's not even true, completely true, from the Hinayana point of view. The world comes to an end for you, ceases to exist for you on certain terms, but it goes on for other people presumably. I mean to the extent that other people do exist. At least they exist as much as you exist. So if for you the world ceases to exist, well, as much as for you the world ceases to exist so much for them, the world continues to exist. Hmm? And, well, what about the standpoint of the Mahayana, and

of the Bodhisattva, hmm? What about building the Pure Land? So cessation is not the last word, presumably. I mean one could say 'when craving ceases to supply the drive, the world comes to at end to the extent that it is dependent on craving.' [315]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, let's go on to 4.

"4. Furthermore, the appearance of this world is like a mock show, a magical illusion. Like a magical show it deceives, deludes and defrauds us, is false when compared with ultimate reality. Things, as Vasubandhu says, are not a trustworthy support. It is assumed that the world of conditioned things is manufactured by ignorance, and Nagarjuna, in his great commentary, shows that ignorance and the products of the magician's art have the following attributes in common: They are neither inside a person, nor outside, nor both inside or outside; they can therefore not be localized with reference to persons; there is nothing real that has been either produced or destroyed; no real event, with an essence of its own, has taken place. And yet, although ignorance is not real, it is the condition for all kinds of activity. Similarly, the musical instruments conjured up by magic are empty, deceptive, without reality, without objective basis, and yet one can hear their music and can see them."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: In other words, things are not what they seem, or rather they are what they seem to be, but you simply misinterpret what you see. You're not wrong in seeing what you do see, but you're wrong in the way in which you interpret what you see. (pause)

This illustration of the mock show, the magical illusion, is a very common one, a very favourite one in Indian literature. You know the sort of thing that it represents, you know, the sort of thing that the illustration is derived from. Of a magician who conjures up a sort of magical appearance, and you see it, or you think you see it, but actually it's not there. It's not there in the u1timate sense. You see it but there is in fact no objective reality corresponding to it which exists. I did actually see one of these magical performances, I don't know whether it was of this kind or another kind, in fact I'd forgotten all about it, but I found a note of it in an old diary. This was about 1951/2 when I stayed in Kalimpong, a wandering sort of magician or yogi came along and he was performing on my veranda the customary trick - one of the customary tricks - you have a flower-pot, and the magician takes a mango-stone and puts it in the pot, in the soil there, covers it with a cloth, and then you see the cloth hanging off like that and he rips it off and there's a grown mango plant. (laughter) [316]

Now whether that's an example of an illusion which he produced or whether he did actually make by some magical means that mango seed or stone shoot forth extraordinarily quickly, I don't know, but that is what happened, that is what I actually saw.

So some magicians, according to literary accounts, do things on a much grander scale; they can conjure up elephants and musicians and things of that sort. That is the point of the comparison here. At least in some way the can conjure up this mango plant according to their personal wishes. Some explain the rope trick like that, that it is a sort of magical show, you know the famous rope trick; a rope is thrown up into the sky and it hangs there, then a boy goes up and disappears and a man goes up after him and disappears and then bits and pieces of a boy fall down, then the man comes, and the master magician puts all the bits and pieces under a cloth, whips off the cloth and then the boy stands up and makes his bow to the audience, then of course they pass the hat round.

Some say it's mass hypnosis probably. But anyway that is the sort of performance that is at the back of this kind of illustration; that is the mock show, that is the magical illusion. The point is that all the experiences of life are essentially the same. You see things, you hear things, things happen, you're involved in them, but ultimately they're no more real than is the mock show or magical illusion.

As I said, not that relative reality and illusory reality so to speak are the same thing, not that there's no difference between them. (While) you could say that relative reality stands to ultimate reality as illusory reality stands to relative reality.

turns.speaker: Jinavamsa
paragraphs: In the case of a magician in India, I can see that he can create the illusion, but if everything was like that who actually makes it happen? Do you know what I mean?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, you do, is the answer. You and other sentient beings, collectively hallucinate, so to speak. You have the same sort of karmas, you have a common sort of background, so you tend to see things in much the same way; you 'hallucinate' in much the same way, 'hallucinate' in inverted commas, so you agree upon your hallucination, so it's very difficult for you to realize that they're hallucinations, because they're hallucinations which everybody agrees that [317] do exist. I mean, if everybody in this room saw a pink elephant in the middle of the room, well the pink elephant would exist! You see? It's only if one of you either sees or does not see the pink elephant that questions arise. So the Buddha is like either the man who sees or does not see, depending on how you frame the analogy, the pink elephant. He sets others wondering, then, whether the pink elephant which they perceive and the Buddha doesn't is really there, or whether in the other case the pink elephant that the Buddha sees but which they don't see is in act really there, but they just can't see it. In the first case the pink elephant represents illusory reality and in the second it represents absolute reality. Questions about the nature of reality only seem to arise when different people see things in different ways or when oneself sees different things in different ways, or even the same things in different ways at different times. Historically, as far as Indian philosophy is concerned, the comparison of the dream state with the waking state seems to have led to a lot

of philosophical reflection about the nature of reality. Anyway, that's the mock show, appearances of this world: "the appearance of this world is like a 'mock show'". Now, let's go on to the dewdrops, a bit more poetic?
"5. The dew-drops may also be 'hoar frost'. That depends on the climate. Each thing is as evanescent as the dew-drops which soon evaporate under the sun's rays."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Or as Keats put it "ere the hot sun count his dewy rosary on the eglantine", that's a more poetical way of putting the whole thing. (pause) The English poets are full of philosophy and even little insights. There's this famous poem by Herrick which makes much the same point on two daffodils: Daffodils we weep to see you pass away so soon, as yet the something something sun has scarce attained its noon. But then the last verse brings in the dewdrops, "Or like the drops of fallen dew ne'er to be found again. Something like that. So this sort of reflection, even insights as they sometimes amount to, seem to be quite universal among thinking and feeling people. (pause) We die as your hours do and dry away like the pearls of morning dew ne'er to be found again. That's the conclusion of the poem. Well, on to the next comparison; that one is pretty obvious.

"6. Like a bubble each experience bursts soon, and it can be enjoyed only for a moment. In Buddhist tradition, the bubble, a particularly unsubstantial entity, is usually related to the skandha of feeling. Dependent on three factors - enjoyer, enjoyable thing, and enjoyment - all joys quickly burst apart. And the same holds, good of painful feelings also." [318]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well, the bubble is of course a reflection of emptiness. Again, there's a poem, by Francis Bacon. "The world's a bubble and the life of man less than a span". There aren't many verses by Francis Bacon, this is one of them.
Voice. Do you know if Conze ever read much in the way of poetry?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: He seems to have read quite a bit of Latin poetry, especially Horace. I think he ought to have read the English poets more, even Burns. "For pleasures are like poppies shed, you seize the flower, its bloom is shed." That's from Tom O'Shanter. "But pleasures are like poppies shed, you seize the flower, its bloom is shed." "Or like the snowfall on the river: a moment white, then melts for ever." (amusement) There's plenty of wisdom in your own Robbie Burns! No need even to go to Keats and Shelley. Well, I mean that expresses much the same meaning as this verse in the "Perfection of Wisdom". Maybe not against the same profound metaphysical background, but the words are pretty much the same or in a general way pretty much the same kind of insight, the same kind of understanding, that worldly things, the conditioned things, are evanescent. It's a commonplace of Chinese poetry; their poets are constantly telling themselves or telling one another that rivers flow on, they flow to the sea,

but they never turn round and flow back in the opposite direction, they never flow back to their source, once things have gone they've gone inevitably never to come again, especially in the prose poetry. But with most of the poets this reflection on the impermanence of things is a source of sadness, even melancholy, but for the Buddhist it can be a source of exhilaration because if things are evanescent, if things are impermanentt, it means that they change, and if they can change, they can change for the better. If there was no such thing as change there would be no such thing as development or work or spiritual progress, no higher evolution, nor even any lower evolution. If there was no such thing as change, no such thing as impermanence, nothing would ever happen at all. So you'd be frozen as it were either into a state of timeless perfection, that would be alright, or into a state of timeless imperfection, that certainly wouldn't be alright. So its fortunate for the 'imperfect' least to whom this 'advice' is addressed, that there is such a thing as change. Development is after all a form of change. So we should reflect on the impermanence of things with a great sense of exhilaration. Well suppose the Roman Empire had lasted for ever, suppose we were still living under the Roman Empire? [319] (laughter) We'd also have crucifixions. We'd all be thrown to the lions by this time! Let's go on to number seven.

"7. Only the enlightened are awake to reality as it is; compared with their vision of true reality, our normal experience is that of a dream, unreal and not to be taken seriously. Nagarjuna, in his great commentary (Mpps pp. 373-5) gives the following explanation of the simile of the dream: '(a) There is no reality in a dream, and yet, while one dreams, one believes in the reality of the things one sees in the dream. After one has woken up one recognizes the falseness of the dream and laughs at oneself. Just so a man who is plunged into the dreamy state which results from his fettered existence, has a belief in things which do not exist. But when he has found the Path, then, at the moment of enlightenment, he understands that there is no reality in them and he laughs at himself. (b) A dreamer, by the force of his dream, sees a thing where there is nothing. Just so a man, by the force of the dreamy state which results from ignorance, believes in the existence of all sorts of things which do not exist, such as I and mine, male and female, etc. (c) In a dream one rejoices although there is nothing enjoyable, one is angry although there is nothing to annoy, one is frightened although there is nothing to frighten. So do the beings with regard to the things of the world.' Therefore, like the things seen in a dream, the dharmas, although they do not exist, are nevertheless seen and heard and one is aware of them.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That third item, C, is probably the most accessible in the sense that, yes, when one is involved in a dream one goes through all sorts of experiences. One experiences all sorts of emotions; you may get angry with someone in a dream, you can get very frightened, but when you wake up what is your experience?

You think, well that was just a dream, it means nothing to you any more. So the point of the comparison is that all the emotional experiences of ordinary life, of relative existence, appear like a dream, as unreal as a dream, when you wake up, that is to say when you are fully enlightened. They just lose their importance, lo se their significance. While you're involved in the dream it's very very real, you experience it, you really are frightened, you really are angry. But when you wake up you know that you're awake and that was all a dream, well, those emotions very quickly die away. It's just the same when one becomes enlightened. All the passions, all the desires, all the annoyances of ordinary life, just fade into insignificance. They no longer exist for you, it's all like a great dream. But sometimes people feel that even on a more mundane level. For some people the time before they came into the FWBO seemed like a bit of a dream. (It's as if) they have woken up. They look back and see how they were motivated by this, [320] motivated by that, and now it just doesn't seem to matter, it's not important any more. It's not important any more to carve out a career or have a good job etc. etc. But once upon a time those things may have seemed very important and very real. I mean, now in the case of some people you mention a career to them and they just laugh. They might have taken that very seriously a few years ago. You notice Nagarjuna says "Then, at the moment of enlightenment, he understands that there is no reality in them, and he laughs at himself."

This note is very often struck in Indian Buddhism though it comes up again and again in the case of Zen, that someone laughs when he sort of sees through his own illusions. What do you think that represents, that laugh? Freud has a book on laughter, or jokes, on wit, hasn't he?

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: It's a relief

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It's a relief, also a release of energy, energy that's locked up in worldly things, in worldly emotions, it's freed, it's liberated, it comes up, expresses itself in the form of a great laugh. Now you see the point of the joke. It seems so ridiculous: here you were, worrying about things which didn't really exist, there you were worrying about your career, your possible success or your possible failure, but now it just doesn't matter, you wonder why you bothered. It all seems just so ridiculous, so pointless, you can't help laughing, at yourself of course. Then, when one sees children, children very often play very seriously, and they get very involved in their games, don't they? They're feeling things so strongly even though from the point of view of the adult there's nothing in it, it's all just so silly. But for the child it's very real, it's very serious. Let's go on to eight and nine.
"8. Like a lightning flash each event is short-lived. Lasting but one brief moment, it disappears again.

"9. Finally, we should look upon things in the spirit in which we watch the clouds on a hot summer day. They change their shape all the time, but as far as our welfare is concerned, one shape is as good as another. So do the transformations of the earthly scene not at all concern our true welfare."

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That last (bit) is a quite important point in a way, quite relevant, "the transformations of the earthly scene not at all concern our true welfare." One can see for instance in England, and certainly in London, that people, including people in the FWBO [321] are quite affected by the general sort of psychological social, economic climate prevailing. Even changes of government seem to affect the mood of the Movement to some extent. Do you see what I mean? Actually that should not be the case. Maybe one needs to take certain changes into account for purely practical reasons but one shouldn't be either elated or depressed by any changes that happen to take place, just watch them, just see them as clouds, adopt emotionally speaking an attitude of detachment towards them. I certainly have noticed the difference in atmosphere broadly speaking, I was going to say in England, perhaps I should say in London, perhaps I shouldn't even say in London, perhaps I should say in Bethnal Green. One notices the difference of atmosphere coming from India. There's a difference of economic climate, because India is a developing country, that is to say the economy is expanding. That does create a definite sort of … atmosphere is the only word I suppose. People look forward to increased opportunities. They definitely feel things can be expected to get better, materially speaking. But in England, certainly in London, the economy isn't expanding, so there isn't that same sort of expectancy that one had for instance throughout the Victorian period, and perhaps much later than that. Do you see the sort of thing I mean? So it's as if - so if something as basic as the economy isn't expanding, it was expanding during the Elizabethan period and during the Victorian period, perhaps it's been expanding until quite recently for hundreds of years, when it isn't expanding and the economy is sort of running down, it seems to communicate a general sense, a general air, a general feeling of things running down, which can communicate itself to even infect, at least affect, a spiritual movement. Do you see what I mean? But we shouldn't allow ourselves to be affected by it, after all samsara rises and falls; sometimes things are better, sometimes worse, that shouldn't affect our basic attitude our fundamental spiritual optimism at all. In other words our spiritual attitude, our spiritual condition, our spiritual state shouldn't be dependent upon mundane contingencies.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: There's no way out of it. Say the economy starts running down and that affects you psychologically, it's an ongoing process.. (unclear)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, the only way to work out of it is to go and emigrate to a country where the economy is expanding, which seems pretty drastic, also in a way pretty useless because you still remain
dependent on the state of the economy. 'That which is supported has no support' as we know. [322]

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: It affects such things as businesses.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: It affects such things as businesses, yes, or some businesses.

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: It doesn't have to, does it? I mean this is in the short term it will do. It's up to a business to try and find some scope.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Right, yes. You don't have to peg away at a business which in the existing circumstances cannot be anything else except a failure. That is not virya paramita, that's just stupidity. (laughter) It must be conjoined with, if not transcendental wisdom at least wisdom in a more mundane form. But no doubt there are certain areas of the economy which are expanding and no doubt some of our friends are involved in those, but on the whole the economy isn't expanding, is it? In Britain?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: It's quite a good time to actually get really involved in the economy, because in a sense there isn't a lot of real competition, because most of the economy is either static or going down. So it's a good chance to go in and try and develop something.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: That sounds fine in theory, but how does it work out in practice?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: I think to start with you've actually got to find a project which is really quite new and something which can be taken off in a completely different way.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Presumably there's lots of other people doing that too? And presumably even that would need capital too?

turns.speaker: Shantiprabha
paragraphs: Isn't it the case there isn't capital available?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: I don't know.

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: It's expensive.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Yes, after observing at very close quarters the operation of our own tiny co-operative business at Padmaloka, I've developed a scepticism as regards to business which will be quite difficult to dislodge. Or maybe not so much business but businessmen, people who run businesses.

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: In theory in a recession the co-op business should be able to pull in its belt a lot tighter than the average ordinary business where people need wages. So co-ops should be able to ride out a recession.

S:. and increase their business because they should move in where others are having to move out because of the greater production costs. Once you've captured that little bit of extra market you usually manage to hang on to it once you've got it. [323]

turns.speaker: Vessantara
paragraphs: Then when the recession comes to an end your competition has been thinned out.

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: And you're well established and can expand perhaps. Again that sounds very good in theory perhaps.
Vessantara:: It does!

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: There are equal and opposite factors working against it probably. Insofar as a co-op usually starts off with it's belt well pulled in, (but) its actually running around with its trousers down. (laughter)

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well in this recession there are no trousers even. I mean some co-ops could be run only by a team of Milarepas. And even they'd not be very successful. With a very limited supply of nettles and practically no clothes at all and certainly no holidays or any shelter or heating. Maybe the slogan or motto should be 'Join our co-op and be a Milarepa' or ' Do you want to be Milarepa? Join our co-op.' 'Do you want a beautiful Tara-like complexion? Join our co- op!' (laughter) Do you want to look as though you are carved out of green jade?...

turns.speaker: Subhuti
paragraphs: (unclear) and fall flat on your face every time you stand up?

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: There's no fear of thieves and robbers whatever! They'd probably leave something for you afterwards. (laughter) I think it's very easy though for people to allow themselves to be caught up and influenced by the current atmosphere outside the movement. I think one needs to watch that and not allow oneself just to go along with it. Perhaps it's a bit difficult in Bethnal Green sometimes not to be affected by the surroundings. One shouldn't be,... one should, you know, allow oneself to be much more affected by one's immediate surroundings, especially by the surroundings so to speak, within one's own mind, when one meditates and studies and so on. Anyway let's read the last section of the very last chapter.
"9. The second conclusion

"32b. Thus spoke the Lord. Enraptured, the Elder Subhuti, the monks and nuns, the pious laymen and laywomen, and the Bodhisattvas, and the whole world with its Gods, men, Asuras and Gandharvas rejoiced in the Lord's teaching. This is the traditional ending of a Mahayana Sutra. [324]

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: We seem to have a larger audience now that was mentioned at the beginning. Perhaps they've just been quietly sort of gathering all this time, perhaps they realised that something's going on, something's happening, and didn't want to miss out.

turns.speaker: Voice
paragraphs: Subhuti's aged a bit. He started off as "venerable" and ended up as an "elder".

turns.speaker: Sangharakshita
paragraphs: Well one might say that the Diamond Sutra is enough to age anybody! Anyway that is the Diamond Sutra. Conze's got, by the way, quite a useful table of contents at the very beginning which will enable one to see at a glance the topics covered. Of course some of the more important topics are not actually listed here, because they come in as it were incidentally. It might be a question of humbling and humiliation. (pause) In the frontispiece there's a picture of Subhuti, as well as the Buddha and the rest of them, and he looks a very old man in the illustration.
(End of seminar)
